text: Your record with the Coalition speaks for itself. Trusted by cell leaders and respected by the rank-and-file, you enjoy unimpeded access to their enclaves and supply chains. Even high-value targets may be shared with you.
Your name carries weight among senior operatives. Most local commanders see you as an asset to the cause and are willing to grant leeway in Coalition space.

Coalition operatives tend to look kindly on you, though ideology outweighs sentiment. You may be granted access to basic armaments or intelligence scraps, but trust is still measured in actions, not words.
The Coalition knows of you, but you've yet to make a mark one way or the other. Your movements draw neither suspicion nor support. At least, for now.

Whispers circulate that you loyalties are uncertain. You are considered naive at best, or an active lapdog of the powers that be. Access to MRC networks is limited, and your arrival in Coalition space is shadowed by informants and silent watchers.
Doubts have hardened into accusations. MRC enclaves refuse you entry, and Field commanders see you as an impediment to the cause.

The Coalition has declared you a threat to the free people of the Sector and an enemy of the cause. Warbands have authorization to intercept your fleet without hesitation, agents actively have been dispatched to hinder your operaitons.

Your name is spoken in the same breath as the MRC's bitterest enemies. Coalition strike teams carry your IDENT-hash and operational profile, with standing orders to destroy you on sight- no matter the cost.
Due to your handling of the FLINT incident, some intelligence handlers consider you a reliable agent for sensitive assignments.

script:
AddText "As you near the center of habitation, the shifting skyline tells its own story. Crumbling pre-Collapse structures give way to towering corporate arcologies, their facades lit by oversized billboards advertising goods and services pushed by the planet's resident concerns." 
AddText "You recall some half-remembered account: a reconstruction effort led by one of the major megacorps, brought in to stabilize the world after the AI Wars left it fractured. The result is what stands before you-a fragile synthesis of corporate consumption layered over surviving local traditions." marketFlavorTextColor
AddText "The air carries the familiar bite of industrial pollutants, but the population seems to adapt as best they can. By Sector standards, it's almost comfortable." marketFlavorTextColor

AddText "The landing zone is a sprawling wasteland of blackened earth and rusted debris, a stark contrast to the vibrant jungle surrounding it. Scattered shuttle remnants and cargo crates are half-buried under the thick jungle undergrowth, their surfaces scarred by both time and conflict. Makeshift shelters and trading stalls have been erected amidst the detritus, haphazardly constructed from salvaged materials and the hulls of derelict ships." marketFlavorTextColor

AddText "The compact shuttle effortlessly maneuvers through the station, smoothly touching down at one of the operational hangars. Curiously, an inactive spacecraft occupies the space too, devoid of any indication of its owner or crew." marketFlavorTextColor
AddText "Upon inspection, it becomes evident that an area designated for storing smaller strikecraft has been left mostly undisturbed, suggesting the possibility of something valuable yet unclaimed within." marketFlavorTextColor

text:
Your fleet makes several attempts to hail the station, but no response ever comes-no signal, no ping, not even automated fallback protocols. Eventually, a small team is dispatched to board and investigate.

Through the grainy feeds of the boarding team's helmet cams, you observe as they move through dim corridors and cold, quiet chambers. The station has been thoroughly stripped-data cores wiped, storage lockers emptied, consoles inert. Whoever passed through here last was meticulous, deliberate.

And yet. There are signs that someone remained behind until recently. Jury-rigged power lines run from backup systems to a sealed maintenance hatch. Scorch marks near an airlock suggest a hasty departure.

Most notably, the cryobay is found powered down but intact-its pods still functioning, but all of them empty. No records remain of who occupied them, or why they were activated.

Someone was here. More than one, possibly. But they're long gone now-and they didn't want to be followed.

After a brieft moment, the connection resolves and a mercenaries visage stares back at you. 

"Ah, hello there! Are you in need of the skills of mercenaries with -real- talent?"

Much like your last encounter, the faceplate of the $nex_mercRank greets you. This time, however, $nex_mercHisOrHer voice cuts the silence first.

"I have no business with you."

"Affirmative. I'll gather the crew."

A small band of warcraft join up with your $shipOrFleet a short period later.

"It's been a pleasure. Don't be a stranger."

You accept $hisOrHer decision professionally and, after exchanging a few pleasantries, cut the comm link.
I don't do charity work, Commander. Find me when your finances are in order.
Crimson allows you to finish your tirade. There is a brief silence before they chuckle creepily, cutting the comm.

The $personRank stares at you, faceplate masking their true reaction.

"Word about people like you spreads fast, $playerName. I'll see you very soon."

The comm link is cut before you have a chance to respond.

text:
Amid the drifting wreckage, your sensors isolate a silhouette unlike the others-small, humanoid in profile, but emanating a signature more akin to a cruiser-class vessel than a strikecraft.

A battered mech hangs motionless in the void, its hull scorched and fragmented. Your flagship's bridge display cycles through sensor filters, slowly enhancing the image. As the resolution sharpens, the mech's distinctive frame comes into focus.

Once a blur of fire and motion during the battle's climax, it now floats listlessly among the twisted remnants of ships and shattered plating. Despite its size, the power readings it emits match that of a warship far beyond its size. Your eye catches the scored armor left by high-grade energy weapons, deep and recent.

The report arrives not long after your salvage team docks with the drifting mech. "We've found the pilot," the message reads. "She didn't make it."

The recovery is solemn, but efficient. Whatever last stand she made, it left the Valkazard scorched and battered, but remarkably intact beneath the damage. Power systems remain dormant, but salvage techs confirm many of the core subsystems - propulsion, control, and armature linkages - are still functional or salvageable with work.

Only a handful of megacorporations in the Sector are known to field machines of this caliber. Following up with them might yield more than just information; perhaps even a reward for returning what is, by all rights, a proprietary asset. The team extracts what they can. The mech is now secured, drifting silently within your $shipOrFleet's drive bubble.

text:
The connection resolves, revealing the visage of $personName. $HeOrShe gives you a brief, evaluative glance. "Ah. There you are," the Tri-Tachyon $rank begins, voice smooth as silk.

"It appears you're in possession of an asset bearing certain proprietary markers tied to Tri-Tachyon Corporation intellectual property. text:
Inside the cramped shuttle bay, the crew is already scrambling.  Suspended beneath the shuttle by heavy towing lines, the Valkazard drifts like a dormant predator. 

It doesn't look quite the way you remember. Had it been deployed while you slept? Damaged and repaired by unfamiliar hands? The questions churn unanswered in the fog of cryosleep lingering in your mind.

"Power systems stabilizing!" a crewmember calls out. "You'll need to monitor flux manually." "Loadouts prepped. No time for fine-tuning," Another adds. "Weapons as-is." Davos shouts from above. "You're good to detach when ready." 

Your atrophied legs feel leaden as you step into the EVA rig, muscles protesting after long disuse. Cryosleep spared your life but exacted its toll. The suit's servo-assist compensates, but you can feel the unsteadiness beneath every movement. Thrusters engage with a faint jolt, and you cross the short gap, drifting toward the Valkazard's broad chest plate. The hull plating bears new scoring,  evidence of events you weren't awake to witness. Your gloved fingers stumble slightly on the manual override.

Highlight "you don't have to sink 'em all"
text:
You mutter under your breath, forcing stiff hands to work the release.

With a mechanical groan, the chest plate slides open, revealing the familiar - if partially modified- cockpit cradle. Climbing inside, the harness automatically locks your limbs into place as the restraints tighten across your torso. Internal systems come online in a slow cascade; a few flashing yellow, some barely clearing green. A faint static buzz precedes the neural interface establishing partial link. Even dulled by atrophy and fatigue, you can feel the frame responding, sluggish but alive. 

Ferin's voice crackles over comms. "Neural sync locked. Core systems primed. You're hot."

Outside, Tri-Tachyon icons spread into their attack formation.

Davos' voice follows, clipped and tense. "Remember- you don't have to sink 'em all- just enough for us to book it." 

Your grip tightens instinctively on the controls. 

The Valkazard's servos mirror your movements- not perfectly, but enough. The docking cables retract with a heavy clank. 

You drift forward under your own power.

"You see, dear interloper," $HeOrShe begins, pacing slowly across the bridge. 

"There are certain realities you may not fully appreciate. What you view as personal property is, in fact, a matter of significant corporate interest. And obstruction, I'm afraid, carries consequences that tend to escalate in unpleasant ways. Tri-Tachyon possesses both the reach and the resolve to ensure compliance- one way or another."

$HeOrShe lets the weight of the threat settle. "That said, there's no need for this encounter to turn regrettable. Cooperation would allow us to resolve this discrepancy amicably; but opposition?" 

A predatory smirk forms. "There is little refuge for those who misstep in such matters." $HeOrShe halts, standing exactly where the pacing began, eyes fixed on you once more. "So: consider carefully. The current favors those who know when to yield."

script:
SetEnabled armaa_valkHunterSt1AgreeResp false
SetTooltip armaa_valkHunterSt1AgreeResp "You consider it. Perhaps surrender could spare the handful of lives left- perhaps even buy your own survival. But as the thought lingers, so too does the weight of what the VALKAZARD represents. In Tri-Tachyon's hands, it would become yet another tool for profit, coercion, and escalation- another ember feeding the slow fire that is destroying the Sector. No good will come from handing this over. Not for you. Not for anyone."

text:
You're still shaking off the residual haze of cryosleep as you shuffle onto the bridge. The survivors have gathered for an inpromptu strategic meeting.

A younger technician breaks the silence first. "Tri-Tachyon." He swallows. "They weren't hiding who they were."

Another crew member snorts bitterly. "Why would they?" 

An older officer glances around. "Listen, we need to settle leadership. If we don't establish command now, we won't last the week." The group hesitates. Quiet glances pass between the faces in the room. 

Finally, one of the senior crew speaks up. "It should be $playerHimOrHer." He nods toward you. "They pulled us out of that fight. Only one left with clearance." 

The older officer continues without pause. "Quartermaster - Davos. Ops - Ferin. Sensors - Aira. I'll serve as XO for now."

script: SetTextHighlights "If anyone in the Sector might understand our situation, it's Arma Armatura."
text:
The crew disperse to their stations, but your XO lingers nearby, TriPad in hand, scrolling through the century of events you've missed. After a moment, he speaks.

"We're running on borrowed time. Supplies won't last forever. If we're going to stabilize, we'll need outside support- and legitimacy." He glances up at you. "From what these logs show, Arma Armatura managed to secure corporate governance over the colony they're calling 'New Meshan.' Looks like they've re-established full corporate board operations out there."

"We could approach them. File a report. If anyone in the Sector might understand our situation, it's the company. If we're lucky, they might offer us some form of status, protection. At the very least, it buys us time while we figure out exactly what situation we're walking into." He pauses, then adds with a hint of caution, "Assuming, of course, they see value in what we bring, and not just something to seize outright."

text:
The last thing you see is the combat display bleaching white - not from a flashbang, not from a weapon impact, but from the ship's core dumping everything it has left into a final, useless scream.

The automated voice you always ignored finally matters. "EMERGENCY EJECTION. EMERGENCY EJECTION."

You remember the harness biting into your ribs, the sound of explosive bolts, and then the universe rotating - stars, debris, fire - until it all becomes a tunnel of black.

You come back to yourself in flickering light and stale air.

You're on a cot that smells like recycled coolant and cheap disinfectant. Someone has stripped your suit down to the basics, patched the worst of the bruising, and left a canteen within reach like they're not sure you'll be awake long enough to use it.

A woman notices your eyes open and raises a hand, palm out - a calm down gesture.

"Easy. We pulled your pod off the drift."

script:
SetTextHighlightColors bad
SetTextHighlights "The Valkazard is gone."
text:
You try to sit up and the room tilts. Your throat tightens around a word that doesn't come out clean.

The crew- "We didn't find anyone else," she says, and doesn't dress it up. "Just you." The reality settles like grit.

The Valkazard is gone. The people who were with you - the ones you almost started to know by name, the ones you might have relied on - are gone too, swallowed by vacuum and flame and the indifference of distance. 

The spacer clears her throat and glances toward the corridor.

She leaves you with the canteen and a thin blanket. The bulkhead door seals with a soft pneumatic sigh.

For a while, you just listen to the ship. The scrape of old bearings. The distant thrum of a drive running slightly out of tune. Voices in the corridor - low, careful. You look around.

The med bay is rather clean for an unaffiliated spacer outfit. But that isn't what alarms you. The ceiling cam has a fresh lens, and when you shift, it follows - a fraction of a second behind, like someone is watching through it rather than it simply recording.

You swing your legs over the cot, unsteady. The door panel doesn't have a manual release. Just a blank touch surface and a small scanner you hadn't noticed before.

You press your palm to it anyway. A line of text blinks, then disappears.

ACCESS DENIED

You lean closer to the bulkhead.

"...told you the beacon would ping," someone says. Not the woman. Male, clipped, professional.

A second voice answers, "Corporate wanted whoever was behind that ship. Alive, preferably."

"Preferably," the first voice repeats, like it's a joke that doesn't need laughing at. "They'll meet us at the handoff... you already know who."

Your breath catches. You feel it in the ribs first - a cold, slow certainty.

This wasn't a rescue. It was a pickup.

You back away from the door and look around for anything - a weapon, a tool, a loose panel - and find nothing. 

There's a soft click behind you. You turn around to see the woman is standing in the doorway. 

"Don't move so much. It'll just make it worse." She steps inside and the door seals behind her. You take a step back.

Your head swims again, sudden and violent. Your legs go loose like your bones forgot how to work. She moves forward and catches you before you hit the deck.

"Easy," she murmurs, lowering you back onto the cot. Straps snap into place across your wrists and chest with efficient, familiar motions.

Your vision tunnels. The ceiling cam stares down like an eye.
As the edges of the world go dark, you hear the woman speak into a comm in her collar.

"Tell them the asset was awake long enough to confirm identity."

A crackle of reply, satisfied. And then the Sector moves on without you.

text:
"My thanks to Providence!" the $rank exclaims, then calls back to $hisOrHer bridge crew, "Persecution proves our devotion! The unbelievers now beg us to share our blessings." $HeOrShe turns back, troubled, "Um. However. My standing is... limited. It is not my place to welcome you to the fold under our hammer and sword, no matter the tithe you offer unto our holy war. You will have to speak with a shepherd of greater authority than I at one of our gathering-places in the fringe worlds." 

"Of course," $heOrShe is abashed now, "I cannot tell you where a station of the Luddic Path is located. I am pledged before Providence to secrecy and it would be a sin to swear false oath. That path must be yours to discover."

The green expanse of Jenius fills the viewport, its dense jungles stretching as far as the eye can see. The planet lacks even the most basic hallmarks of interstellar society, forcing any landing operation to make do with rugged, makeshift landing zones carved out of the wilderness.

With the automated drone patrols and pirate flotillas either destroyed or scattered, the path is finally clear. The mission to penetrate the untamed surface can proceed, but the lack of infrastructure means your forces will have to establish a foothold the hard way, pushing into a world that largely shuns the outside world and teeming with outlaws who cling to the shadowy fringes of galactic law.

The verdant world of $fullName comes into view. Settled before the collapse, it remains relatively secluded from the majority of spacefaring society. Indeed, it even lacks the basic necessity of any spacefaring society; a spaceport.

With the defensive fleets orbiting the planet destroyed or diminished, the operation can proceed

text:
Theoretically, the Ceylon and much of the surrounding urban grid could be leveled from orbit with minimal opposition- but the collateral would be catastrophic. 

A cleaner strike has been proposed instead- Diversionary elements will draw Arusthai eyes and guns away from the objective.

Meanwhile, a strike package will close on the Ceylon directly engaging the vessel's limited escort screen above the city.

Once neutralized, assault shuttles will drop through the breach, deploying marines and armor for final ground-side seizure.

script:
armaa_jeniusCityBattle
AddTextSmall "Large ships (unless designed for operation in orbit) won't be suitable for this operation - only frigates and destroyers can be deployed. Ships will suffer an increasingly greater reduction to manuverability due to atmospheric pressures based on their size." highlight
AddTextSmall "The battlefield is fluid, and reinforcements may appear at any time." highlight
text:
As the strike group closes on the city, a new contact flashes across the tactical overlay. Your comms officer speaks up,  "Captain $player.lastName - anomaly detected. High-powered beacon just came online inside the city perimeter. No registry match."

Interference blooms across the spectrum. In the growing chaos, enemy patrols abruptly adjust course, vectoring straight toward allied transponders. Moments later, a garbled burst breaks through- one of Kade's contracted captains, his voice strained under pressure. "Ambush! Hostiles converging on all sides- we're taking heavy fire! Requesting immediate support!"

Your $shipOrFleet burns hard toward the engagement zone, but as you approach, the distant city skyline is already lit by overlapping tracer streams and rising plumes of fire. Wreckage dot the outskirts, fragments of friendlies caught in the opening volleys. What's left of the formation struggles to hold as Sovereignty forces tighten the noose.

As the clouds parted, you finally saw the settlement. Expecting a ramshackle raider camp, you're shocked to see a sprawling city below, its infrastructure and civilian population unmistakable.

Kade's intel was wrong-or perhaps deliberately misleading. No turning back now. The city is under attack, and the truth about this mission is becoming alarmingly clear.

As you watch the city below, engulfed in a sea of flames, you can't help but wonder how many lives were disrupted-or ended-by your attack.

You review the mission brief one last time. The intel was sparse, pointing to a minimal defensive force-an easy target. But what began as a minor skirmish between your recon units and Arusean strikecraft has spiraled into an all-out battle above the city. Reinforcements from both sides are expected soon, but your strike group will likely be the key to punching through the enemy and securing air superiority.

Thick clouds hang over the battlefield, limiting your visibility, and your sensors are struggling to cut through mysterious EM interference. The weather here is notoriously unpredictable, and the region is in a communications blackout, forcing you to rely on tightbeam comms while keeping radio silence to remain undetected.

As your forces pierce the cloud cover, enemy craft launch from below, vectoring in to intercept. The battle is about to begin in earnest.

options: armaa_WFRendevous:Rendevous with the remaining allied forces

text: It takes some time for the forces around Jenius to accumulate. Once they do, you raise the combined forces over a joint channel.

script:
BeginConversation armaa_kade
armaa_Hikaru_Utada playMusic music_armaa_briefing
ShowSecondPerson armaa_imelda
ShowThirdPerson armaa_redeye
Highlight "Arusthain Sovereignty"
text:
The comm link stabilizes quickly, flickering to reveal a medley of mercenaries. Some hardened, others young and untested, a few barely visible through rad-scarred comm units. Kade's image stands out prominently, leaning over his console, hands gripping its edge as he begins.

"Before we get into the mission's details, you need to understand what we're up against."

He taps a button, and a holo-map springs to life, displaying two major territories. "First, the Arusthain Sovereignty. They're built on industry and military strength, with most of the northern hemisphere under their thumb. Their fleet is a mix of tech-mined ships and strikecraft, with few formidable units in the ranks. Fiercely isolationist, with zero tolerance for spacers. Sovereignty factories, relics from before the Collapse, are being used to churn out everything from ships to hovercraft. For now, they're the dominant power on Jenius."

He lets the tension hang for a moment before continuing. "Next, we have the so-called Free Cities. An alliance of settlements with little love for Arusthai's iron grip. They claim to stand for freedom, and they're more focused on living on their own terms more than anything else. Scrappy, sure, but disorganized too - a mix of anyone willing to live outside the Sovereignty's rule."

Kade's eyes narrow as he glances offscreen, and a nondescript crewman quickly appears, offering him a glass before hurrying out of sight.

He takes a long sip of the concoction.

"The Sovereignty's been aiming to bring the Free Cities under its control for a while now, and while they might not be much on their own, they can be useful. If we cripple Arusthai's production and defenses, scav's will seize the opportunity to move in, keeping the Sovereignty busy while we carve out something real here."

"Commander $player.lastName, you an' Nessus will be taking out what's left of the droneships before proceeding into lower orbit. Things go smoothly, an' your people should be back in time for happy hour."

script:

script:
BeginConversation armaa_imelda

ShowPersonVisual
AddTextSmall "A fervent member of the Luddic Path, Imelda brings her zeal for the old ways and anti-tech philosophy to every mission she undertakes. Her speech is marked by a formal politeness, tempered with an almost reverential awe for the prophet Ludd, whom she believes holds the key to restoring balance to a corrupted universe. Imelda is driven by a deep, unwavering conviction in her cause, seeing herself as a crusader against the overwhelming presence of evil. While her demeanor remains calm and controlled, there is a fire beneath her surface-a passion that can be both captivating and intimidating to those who disagree with her ideals. Her loyalty to the Luddic Path makes her a fierce ally, but her inflexibility could lead to conflict with those who are more pragmatic in their approaches." highlight
text:
"Blessings upon you, Commander $player.lastName. It seems our intervention was timely," Imelda says with a subtle note of reproach.

The comms unit crackles as her image resolves, revealing her with a faintly raised brow and a composed expression. She gives you a measured nod.

"The servants of Moloch are treacherous, and it appears their strength was greater than anticipated. No shame in needing a helping hand against such abominations." Her tone softens slightly, adopting a more diplomatic air. "We are all tested in our resolve, Commander. The Path is no stranger to adversity, and we are all stronger when we face it together. How fares your crew after the encounter?"

options: armaa_WFRendevous2ImeldaOtherEnemies:"We were attacked by something other than drones. Said we were trespassing."

text:
"I will not pretend, Commander, that my hand is guided solely by the prospect of battle." Her voice remains even, though there's a sort of intensity beneath the words.
"The compensation promised for this venture will go far in sustaining my flock; good men and women who weather these difficult cycles with little but faith and scarce provision. Every credit earned is another roof secured, another meal served."

She pauses. And in that stillness, a flicker of fervor rises behind her eyes. "Of course, the chance to see these profane machines broken and scattered remains its own reward. To strike against such heresy is a duty and a mercy."  When she speaks again, her voice softens, but does not lose its conviction.

"Our paths may differ, Commander, but I would see them run parallel in this task. Cooperation serves us both - and perhaps, in its small way, offers comfort to those who endure beneath us."

text:
$personName smiles faintly, inclining her head in acknowledgment.

"Ah, as expected from someone of your reputation, Commander. It is heartening to see such ease in the face of our mutual adversaries."
"Effortless, then. Even the unfaithful can be granted success, it would seem. Perhaps, Commander, providence serves you despite... differing beliefs."
"Curious... Perhaps the heathens grow more desperate. Or there are other forces at play, seeking to hinder our righteous path. We shall remain vigilant. Thank you for the warning, Commander."

A slight frown crosses $personName's face.

"How interesting. I trust, Commander, that you did not leave any... stragglers behind? Such incidents often attract attention from unwelcome quarters."

$personName chuckles softly, giving a modest bow.

"Apologies, Commander. I may have been presumptuous. We have not yet met in person, but I have heard much of your deeds. Consider this my formal introduction, in service to the Path. I am $personName, a humble servant of the faith."

$personName's eyes narrow slightly, though she maintains her composure.

"Not formally, no. Nor did I expect recognition to be mutual, given your... stance on our order. Yet in the end, all roads lead to understanding, Commander. In time, even the unwilling may come to see this truth."

text:
Before you can respond, a chime from your TriPad signals an incoming comm request from Kade. After a few moments, his image sharpens through the static.

"$player.firstName, Imelda. Outstanding work. Most teams have accomplished their objectives."

 "...It'll take some time to get things fully operational down there-In the meantime, though, I may have a few more tasks for y'all. We've got plenty of ground left to cover."

"We shall, of course, lend our strength where it is most needed," Imelda replies carefully, "And if these tasks bring us closer to rooting out the tools of Moloch, all the better."

"Good. Imelda, I'll forward the full details of your next objective shortly. Until then, take it easy, both of you." He gives a final approving look before the screen flickers, his image breaking up into static, and then disappearing as the comm link cuts off.

 Imelda stares offscreen, a thoughtful expression in her eyes, before turning back to you.

"It appears we have a moment of reprieve, Commander," she says smoothly, "I would suggest using this time wisely. I, for one, will see to my crew's preparations-they will need both rest and resolve for the coming trials."

She hesitates as she considers you.

"And you, Commander? How do you intend to proceed? I hope our objectives remain aligned, at least for the duration of this assignment."
options:
armaa_WFbeatDefendersReserved:"Let's put our differences aside and focus on the task at hand."
armaa_WFbeatDefendersAgreeable:"As long as our interests line up, you'll have my cooperation."
armaa_WFbeatDefendersSkeptical:"You can't expect me to fully trust the intentions of a terrorist."
armaa_WFbeatDefendersPather:"You misunderstand, sister. I, too, walk the Path."

text:
Imelda's polite demeanor barely wavers, though a hint of frost creeps into her smile.

"Your caution is noted, Commander. I would expect nothing less. Know that I am not asking for your trust-only your cooperation, as circumstances demand. Our goals may differ, but for now, they serve each other."

She inclines her head in a slight bow.

"May you find clarity in your purpose, Commander. I suspect it will be tested soon enough."

With that, Imelda ends the transmission, her image vanishing from the screen.

script:

text:
Imelda's eyes widen slightly, her typically composed demeanor giving way to a flicker of surprise. For a moment, she is silent, studying you carefully as if weighing the sincerity of your words. Then, a warm, genuine smile breaks through her usually restrained expression.

"Is that so, Commander?" she says, her voice quieter, almost reverent. "Few outside our flock are willing to take such a step toward enlightenment, and fewer still carry themselves with the strength to act upon it."

She inclines her head deeply, a gesture of respect.

"To walk the Path is not an easy burden, but it is one we carry gladly, for we know it is righteous. If you share in our purpose, despite your..unorthodox methods, Commander, then perhaps this mission is more than mere convenience-it is providence."

With that, Imelda ends the transmission, her image vanishing from the screen.

Imelda nods, her expression unreadable but respectful.

"Then let us prepare ourselves, Commander. When we next meet these adversaries, may it be with unshakable purpose. Until then, may your path be clear."

With a final, polite nod, Imelda turns away, her image fading as she cuts her comm link, leaving you alone to consider the tasks awaiting you.

Smoke billows from the outer districts, flames consuming residential areas as the battle rages on. Overhead, explosions burst through the sky, and the shattered remains of the DAS Ceylon rain down-a torrent of debris and stray projectiles smashing through buildings, streets, and anything in their path. Fires spread uncontrollably as fragments of the once-mighty carrier rip into neighborhoods, escalating the devastation.

A grim truth settles amid the chaos: the city, caught in the wreckage and crossfire, has become a casualty of a plan that, ironically, sought to minimize harm.

Smoke billows from the outer districts, and flames consume residential areas as the battle rages on. Overhead, explosions burst through the sky, but the DAS Ceylon maneuvers desperately, its engines sputtering and hull scorched. It wavers unsteadily but holds together, defying the odds as it rises above the fray.

Through a combination of luck and sheer determination, the Ceylon's captain executes a last-ditch jump maneuver, vanishing into the ether just as a barrage of missiles converges on its location. The missiles detonate harmlessly in the empty air where the ship once hovered, leaving its pursuers bewildered.

Down below, the rain of debris is less severe than it could have been-shrapnel and fragments scatter through the districts, but the catastrophic destruction of the carrier is avoided. Fires still rage, but there's a glimmer of hope amidst the chaos: fewer lives are lost than anticipated, and emergency responders rush to the streets, rallying around survivors.

text:
"Now that we've got that out of the way, listen up." His voice takes on a more commanding tone. "With the path you cleared, ground forces are moving in as we speak. They're securing what we need from the area-resources, data, anything that can give us an edge. You did your part, now it's time to stand by. Things are in motion, and we need you ready for the next step when it comes."

He pauses, sizing you up. His grin returns, but this time it's more like a wolf sizing up its prey. "So, relax while you can. Grab a drink, catch some sleep. I'll get in touch when the next op kicks off. But don't get too comfortable. We're just getting started."

"Collateral's always a factor in these kinds of ops, you know that. But yeah, we hit the right place. They had military forces, didn't they? Civilian infrastructure's part of the price sometimes. Ain't pretty, but that's the reality."

His tone turns just a shade more serious. "Question is-are you sure you're cut out for this?"

A sly grin creeps across Kade's face. "Straight to business, huh? I like it. No need to worry, I'm a man of my word." There's the sound of a console being tapped in the background. "Credits are being wired as we speak. You earned 'em. Don't spend it all in one place."

Kade sighs, clearly not in the mood for moral debates. "Win? Out here, survival's the only game, my friend. You think the Arusthai, or anyone else, are going to play nice if we start pulling punches? They'd steamroll us the first chance they got." 

He leans in slightly, voice low and unwavering. "I get it. You're feelin' the weight of it. But if we start worrying about saving every damn building, every damn life, we'll be the ones wiped off the map. Out here, you make the hard calls, or you end up on the wrong side of someone else's."

"Ah, straight to the point, I like that. See, terms is just a polite way of saying leverage. The Arusthai are stubborn, but they respect power. They don't fold unless they're broken first." He gestures offscreen, likely at the smoldering ruins of the city. "And now? They're -broken-."

"With their capital in ruins and the pride of their fleet gone, they'll have no choice but to bow to the new reality. I'll dictate what remains of their defenses, their resources, and how they serve my broader plans. They'll become my... clients, so to speak."

He pauses, letting the weight of his words sink in. "As for you, $player.firstName, you've done me a great favor. I think it's time we discussed your role in what comes next."

"Ah, straight to the point, I like that. See, terms is just a polite way of saying leverage. The Arusthai are stubborn, but they respect power. They don't fold unless they're broken first." He gestures at something offscreen. "And now? They're -broken-." His expression hardens as he continues, voice low but deliberate. "With most of their fleet destroyed, they'll have no choice but to bow to the new reality. I'll dictate what remains of their defenses, their resources, and how they serve my broader plans. They'll become my... clients, so to speak."

He pauses, letting the weight of his words sink in. "As for you, $player.firstName, you've done me a great favor. I think it's time we discussed your role in what comes next."
As your $shipOrFleet docks at $entityName, you receive a high-priority call from docking authority.

The screen resolves into a helmeted silhouette. Most of the faceplate is dead black, save for one red point of light that gleams back at you.

"Captain $playerName. The boys pinged me that your $shipOrFleet was pulling up to dock, so I figured i'd have a word."

"Back over the capital, during our little operation - thanks for showing up when you did. Hate to say, but we were a few minutes out from discovering new and exciting ways to die. You saved a lot of my mens lives, and," he shifts somewhat, " ruined a lot of property." The red eye flares just a fraction, as if he's focusing on a memory.

"Depending on who you ask, that's a tragedy. But i'd say the craters add character."

"Look," Deadeye says, and the humor drops off like a vessel diving into phase space. "We came in hot over a city full of people. Casualties are a matter of course."

A small body dashes into view. Deadeye swiftly intercepts them with his right arm as they pass, gently diverting them back offscreen.

"Sorry. Anyway- Kade would call it a win regardless," he says. "That's what he does. But the 'how' matters. To the people down there,  to us, and to whoever's going to come looking for payback."

"So. Professional note, from one person who likes living to another: expect a follow-up."

"On the bright side," he adds, "you've made yourself unforgettable. Hard to ambush someone when their reputation arrives five minutes before their transponder."

options: armaa_redeyeThanksOptsAResp:"You look like you made it out fine."
armaa_redeyeThanksOptsBResp:"How bad was it before we arrived?"

text: "Fine is... a strong word," Deadeye says. "My back would like a word with whoever designed that cockpit."

He is silent for a moment before laughing a low laugh that comes out wrong.

"Bad enough that I was considering a career change," he says, "Farming? Painting? Something where the worst outcome doesn't mean atomization."

"Most things aren't," Deadeye says, matter of factly. "I don't know how much you care about that sort of thing, but it could have been much worse. Let's just say Kade had a plan B." He moves on without elaboration.

"People will count the bodies, and people will claw for a name to pin the blame onto. That's just how it goes. You saved lives too, even though it isn't as obvious."

text:
As the strike team nears the city, an unexpected alert flashes across your tactical displays. Your tactical officer's voice comes through with a note of urgency:

"Commander, picking up an anomaly-an unregistered high-powered beacon powering up from within the city. It doesn't match any known profiles in our database; possibly old Domain tech."

A wave of interference ripples through the comms, disrupting the previously secure tightbeam channels. The interference escalates, and nearby enemy patrols suddenly lock onto allied signals. Moments later, an urgent transmission crackles over the comms-a familiar mercenary captain, his voice laced with static and strain:

"We've been ambushed! Hostiles are swarming our position-repeat, we're under heavy fire! They're converging from all directions!"

Your $shipOrFleet surges forward to reinforce, but by the time you arrive, the sky above the city is an inferno of tracer fire and explosions. The allied ships are battered, some reduced to debris fields, others barely holding formation as the Sovereignty forces press the attack.

The comm flickers to life, resolving into the muted lighting of Kade's office. He's seated behind his desk, carefully pruning a small green plant. Real, by the look of it; a rare indulgence out here. For a moment, he seems absorbed in the work, letting the silence stretch. Then his eyes lift, meeting yours across the channel. 

"$player.firstName," he says.

"It's Arusthai. They still hold most of this world. So far, they've kept their distance. But they're growing bolder, testing the waters. And if they decide to move against us - well." A brief shake of the head. "I'm not about to let it come to that."

"We need to remind them — decisively — that this settlement isn't going anywhere. That moving against us would cost more than they're willing to spend. I intend to make that clear before they decide to find out for themselves."

"Could solve this with antimatter and be done with it," Kade says. "But we need their infrastructure. So we hit the assets, take control, leave enough standing to keep things running."

He looks at you.

"Imelda's occupied. Deadeye's useful but he needs direction. That's you. You in?"

options: armaaWFMissionQuestionsAlly:"You really think this is going to make them back off?"
armaaWFMissionQuestionsHowResist:"You can count on me. Just tell me when and where, and we'll get it done."

text:
A hint of a smirk forms on his face as he considers the question.

"Absolutely."

Kade's face splits into a grin, his eyes gleaming with approval. "That's what I like to hear. I knew I could count on you. These folks will become a thorn that needs to be plucked, and id rather circumvent the issue. Hit 'em hard and fast, show them we mean business."

text:
The feed stabilizes, resolving into a grainy tactical display - a silhouette of a massive, winged carrier hangs in the void. "The DAS Ceylon," Kade says, voice low, eyes narrowing as the image rotates. "Flagship of the Arusthai fleet with enough strike craft and EW capabilities to strong-arm every other faction left on this rock." The glow of the projection cutting stark shadows across his face. 

"How they got their hands on it? My guess - some buried hangar, leftover from whoever thought they were in charge before the Collapse made fools of us all."

"Up 'til now, it's been the usual posturin', but this-" the display zooms, highlighting its position near a major urban center, "--this is different. They're pulling in assets. And the chatter on the comm bands says it plain: they're gearing up for a move."

"So we move first. Crack the Ceylon, and every other power watching will understand: Arusthai's reach ends here. I've already got pieces moving into position - but your part? That's what makes or breaks this."

"Hell of a fight over the capital."

He grabs a drink, swirling it lazily before setting it down. "We secured everything valuable. Arusthai's feelin' the heat, but this ain't over."

His tone shifts, cutting to the chase. "They're ready to be civil and hear me out now.Better than bleeding dry to hold the line, aye? I play it right, maybe we lock this down without more mess."

options: armaa_WFShuttle2:Take a shuttle to Varyn's Hollow

text:
Beneath your feet, the deck trembles, and the unfamiliar sound of wind shear fills the air as the autoshuttle pierces through $fullName's dense atmosphere. 

"Fifteen minutes out," your shuttle pilot informs you through your earpiece, "Keep your EVA helmets on for now, cabin's not yet pressurized. Ride's pretty rough – wouldn't want you bumping your head against something before we touch down."

With a click, the radio communication ends. The cabin lights momentarily flicker due to a particularly forceful jolt, but then the gusty wind subsides, and the roaring engines settle into a simple cruising rhythm. The sound of air hissing through your helmet drowns out all other noises.

Below, lies the verdant expanse of $fullName.

The shuttle touches down in a burned out section of field. The rear door of the shuttle drops, allowing you and your entourage to step out into the humid $fullNameian air.

Foliage extends as far as the eye can see, encompassing the surroundings in its vibrant embrace. Sporadically scattered across the terrain, sizable yet squat mounds of metal interrupt the green expanse in irregular arrangements. In the distance, what you surmise to be the settlement looms, it's ugly grey buildings bordered by a low wall.

In time a small convoy of vehicles approach the shuttle, and a cadre of soldiers hop out, weapons slung, trudging towards you. The leader, a wiry man with salt-and-pepper hair and a rifle slung lazily over his shoulder, greets you with a disarming grin that doesn't quite reach his eyes.
"Ah, the big gun, huh? Spacer, right? What brought you out here?" His eyes move over each of you as he speaks.

options: armaaWFWhyHereAns:"I was sent here to negotiate."

text:
"Mmm...let's hear about it back in the settlement, yes?" he says, motioning for you and your men to follow.

You're loaded into one of the vehicles the soldiers brought along. They eye you with detached curiousity, but are not much for conversation. 

As you draw near what appears to be a checkpoint, you note a trooper leaning against the wall of a two-story complex. He stands, waving off some security drones, and opens a gate that allows the convoy to pass into the city proper.

"Mmm...let's hear about it back in the settlement, yes?" he says, motioning for you and your men to follow.

You're loaded into one of the vehicles the soldiers brought along. They eye you with detached curiousity, but are not much for conversation. 

As you draw near what appears to be a checkpoint, you note a trooper leaning against the wall of a two-story complex. He stands, waving off some security drones, and opens a gate that allows the convoy to pass into the city proper.

The convoy halts. The soldiers nod toward a building in the distance.
Squat, concrete, utilitarian - barbed wire along the top, guard towers at each corner. 

It would look serious if the people staffing it did. Their weapons are outdated, their uniforms mismatched. They watch your approach with the practiced stillness of people who've learned to look more capable than they are.

You follow the leader through several corridors, eventually coming to a stop at a larger door. He motions for you to enter. "Name's Jaxan, by the way. I run security for this little slice of paradise, for better or worse."

The room beyond is sparse but orderly; cleaner than the rest of the settlement by far. A small table sits in the center, two worn chairs pulled close, and atop it, a battered kettle releases a thin stream of steam.

Imelda stands near the far wall, as if she'd been expecting you. 

"Ah. You've arrived," she says, "You'll forgive the surroundings. Comfort is not always the companion of those who tread Ludd's path."

She gestures to the empty chair and picks up two small mugs. "Tea?" she offers, pouring without waiting for a reply. "The leaves were grown here. One of the few blessings this place manages to give."

As she passes you the cup, she studies you briefly. "I trust your path here was quiet. This outpost is... fraying. But you're not here for my observations."

It's earthy-strong, bitter, but strangely grounding. Something real, grown from soil and toil. Imelda watches you closely, then nods, as if your acceptance confirms something unspoken. 

"Ludd teaches that shared warmth builds trust. Even if only for a moment. There's a group operating in this area, 'rebels', as Kade would label them. Jaxan believed they'd scatter, but they've only grown bolder."

She sets her cup down with a faint clink.

"They're - supposedly - being backed from offworld. Which means this is bigger than it appeared."

You raise a hand and shake your head gently. 

"Very well," she murmurs, taking a quiet sip from her own cup. "Not everyone who drinks from the same pot shares the same cause." 

She sets the mug down.

 "There's a group operating in this area, 'rebels', as Kade would label them. Jaxan believed they'd scatter, but they've only grown bolder."

She sets her cup down with a faint clink.

"They're - supposedly - being backed from offworld. Which means this is bigger than it appeared."

"You have seen them, have you not? The machines these heretics employ." She inclines her head slightly, as though conceding a point in a sermon, "-they do not correspond to anything recorded in-system. They carry the scent of Moloch."

"Those antiquated explorarium units. I do not believe they are guided by a hand that we know. Only sufficient force to keep the faithful at a distance, and then… nothing. A perimeter without a name. But why?"

"The rebels are receiving assistance, yes. But not from any hand we have previously encountered."

"Kade believes we are in control now. I do not share his certainty. And I suspect you do not either. Something has come among us. And it has not completed its work."

She rises and crosses to the console mounted along the far wall, its casing dulled by dust but unmistakably intact. Her fingers glide along the interface as the display comes alive, pale light washing over her features.

"Following the second action against the rebels, one of their number was taken alive."

"He spoke of resupply," Imelda continues. "Not from the nearer settlements, but from elsewhere in the system. A location his superiors labored to conceal."

"Kade has not authorized a comprehensive inquiry. He maintains that our forces are overextended, and that pursuing phantoms would be an indulgence."

"This is not a fireside tale meant to frighten children. It is a deliberate act. And if we do not trace it soon, whatever is being prepared there will mature beyond our reach."

options: armaaWFSettlementWhereArusthaiResp:"Why hasn't Arusthai stepped in yet? This seems like their job."
armaaWFSettlementSentimentResp:"How do people here feel about working under Kade's watch? Is this rebellion isolated?"
armaaWFSettlementWhyYaHereResp:"What are you doing here, anyway?"

text:
"Beyond the rhetoric? There is no true voice among them. No conviction they arrived at themselves."

Imelda frowns.

"Something found them in their grievance and gave them direction. That is how it always begins." Her tone doesn't change.

"Whether they understand what they serve is another question entirely."

"Arusthai's fleet is badly fractured. Much of it sits in drydock or scattered across the hemisphere."

She glances at you briefly. "No small part of that is your doing."

She says it without accusation. An observation, not a judgment - though the distinction is narrow.

"They will not move without Kade's word. And Kade has given none. He is more comfortable holding the appearance of authority than testing whether it is real."

Her tone doesn't harden exactly. But something behind it does.

"And there are decisions he has made since taking this world that I find... difficult to reconcile."

"Public sentiment is divided. Some welcome Kade as a stabilizing presence. Others regard his arrival as merely a reassignment of who holds the leash." Her lips thin, almost imperceptibly. 

"There is residual loyalty to Arusthai - if only because it is familiar. But do not mistake this for an isolated grievance." She looks at you steadily. 

"What moves here has been moving for some time. These disturbances are only where it has broken the surface."

"This is where fractures announce themselves. Not in citadels or capital halls, but in disregarded settlements and under-provisioned garrisons."

"I requested this posting when certain patterns began to repeat themselves. Kade sent me." 

Her expression changes, as if tasting something bitter. "And then decided I was pursuing phantoms."

script:
$global.armaaWFSpokeToSettlement = true
Call $global.armaa_des_ref updateData
AdjustRepPerson armaa_imelda COOPERATIVE 2
FireAll PopulateOptions
text:
Imelda inclines her head, hands settling atop the worn leather of her satchel.

"Then you are already upon the Path," she says gently, "whether you have chosen it or not."

From the bag she withdraws a narrow, weather-scored datapad, its casing marked with a faint sigil of Ludd. She places it carefully on the table between you.

"These coordinates will carry you to Meshan. What lies beneath it is ancient-entombed with intent. I do not believe it was ever meant to be disturbed." Her gaze lingers on the device. "Yet the moment we set foot upon this world, the hourglass was turned."

Her voice lowers, still composed, still reverent.

"Kade may find amusement in shadows. Ludd taught us that the gravest evils do not announce themselves with thunder. They arrive as murmurs. As invitations. They endure." A measured breath. "If my apprehensions are justified, then this... entity... did not fall from the heavens."

She straightens, drawing her coat around her shoulders.

"Proceed with caution. And if the light should falter - burn brighter."

Your $shipOrFleet closes on the facility entrance. Barren rock gives way to the looming silhouette of colossal blast doors, their armored faces scarred by centuries of erosion and micrometeorite impacts. With a tortured groan of stressed servos and grinding metal, the gates shudder apart, venting a gust of stagnant air thick with rust, oil, and mineral dust.

A vertical wound carved into the planet's crust, its reinforced walls vanishing into blackness. Sporadic work lights flicker to life as your sensors probe the depths, briefly illuminating collapsed mining platforms and rust-choked extraction rigs drifting in slow, silent orbits.

Red icons bloom across your displays as hostile ships maneuver along the shaft's interior, thrusters flaring in constrained arcs. A perimeter-hastily assembled, but lethal in close quarters.

As your $shipOrFleet holds position at the lip of the abyss, floodlights lance into the darkness, revealing entire levels of abandoned infrastructure stacked one atop another. The deeper your scans reach, the stranger the readings become: massive voids beneath the lower decks, power flows spiking far beyond what a mining facility should still be capable of generating.

Something else is active down there.

Your tactical display registers the capital ship's transponder as a fading ghost vector, already cold. Whatever it was, it's gone - deeper into the system, or out of it entirely. Your sensors officer flags the drive signature for analysis and says nothing further. 

Salvage operations commence, and after some time, Ops voice cuts through on a secondary channel.

"Captain. You're going to want to come look at this before my crews start stripping it."

One shuttle ride later and you are face to face with Ops at an access point at the lip of the facility. He gestures down the collapsed passage and leads without waiting.

The mobile armor sits wedged against a collapsed bulkhead two levels below the shaft lip. One arm is gone entirely, but the core housing is intact - barely - and the frame, for all the punishment it absorbed, held.

You ask if it's operational.

"Not today," Ops says. "Maybe not for a while. But recoverable? Yeah. I've seen worse salvage jobs." He pauses.

"Never seen anything quite like it, though."

Even wrecked, it dominates the space. Your tech officer circles it slowly alongside some handpicked subordinates, running a handheld scanner along the exposed frame, occasionally stopping to crouch and examine a joint or cluster of exposed circuitry.

"Arma Armatura," she says finally. "The design lineage, anyway. You can see it in the weapons mounting philosophy, the way the frame distributes load through the hips." She straightens and looks up at what remains of the head unit. "But it's not any variant I've ever seen documented. And this-" 

She points to a section of exposed internals near the chest cavity, where something that might be a secondary power core sits dark and inert behind the ribs of unfamiliar alloy "-I don't know what this is."
options: 0:armaa_WFShaftGetKshatriyaTech:Ask tech if they have any ideas

text:
She lingers on it a moment longer than she needs to, then moves on. The rest of the scan proceeds in silence.

When she's done she stands with her back to the mech, reviewing her results. 

"The weapon architecture on the binders and the chest housing - it's not a standard emitter array. It's more like..." She trails off, shakes her head.

"I don't have a good frame of reference for it. I'd need to do a proper teardown to say anything definitive."

She pulls up something on her scanner, compares it to the exposed housing, then looks at you with an expression you don't often see on her.

"The GuarDUAL - $foundGuardualName. The PERCEPT system - The underlying architecture is the same. Not similar. The same."

She looks back at the mech.

"PERCEPT runs one emitter off a single cascade array. This thing has twelve, split across four binders and the core frame, and judging by the routing they're designed to fire in coordinated sequences rather than simultaneously." 

"Someone took the same principle and scaled it. They knew exactly what they were building from."

OPs glances up from where they've been examining the ankle joint. "Does this change whether we can move it?"

"No," she says, not looking at him.

"Then I'll let you two have this moment."

She ignores him. "The GuarDUAL has no documented production chain.." She finally looks back at you. "This didn't come from a production chain either. Maybe it came first, or someone built this from what they had left. The same someone, I think."

An engineer crouches near the base of the frame, running a thumb along a seam in the plating. "Nothing you'd expect on a production vehicle." He stands. 

A custom build, then. One-of-a-kind, or close to it.

Your tech officer looks back at her scanner. "Whatever it was built for, it wasn't built cheap." She pauses. 

"That's what bothers me, actually. This much craft, this much investment - and it was left here."

The engineer squints up at the damaged torso. "With enough supplies and time and I can get it running again. Won't be pretty,  But it'll be yours."

script:
armaa_Hikaru_Utada giveKshatriya
armaa_Hikaru_Utada stopMusic
AddShip $armaa_giftMech
$player.armaa_recoveredKshatriya = true
text:
Salvage spends the next several hours cutting the frame free and arranging to have it towed up into orbit. It arrives near the $shipOrFleet looking like exactly what it is: something that lost a very bad fight.

Whatever it is, it's yours now.

Forward elements reach a broad void carved into the rock: an internal berth cavern so vast it was clearly intended for large hull sections or orbital-scale equipment rather than routine traffic. Half  collapsed into rubble-strewn shadow, docking spines hang twisted and broken. A massive launch cradles lie skewed across the deck, its clamps still powered, gnawing at empty space.

Whatever was cradled here, it was central to the facility.

* * *

You're halfway through reviewing fleet maintenance logs when your comms crackle to life. "Commander $player.lastName, this is Salvage Lead Tarek. We've found something. You might want to come down here in person."

You arrive at a makeshift triage bay in one of your auxiliary landers. Inside, under dim field lights, are three bodies. One, still breathing.

An officer steps forward, datapad in hand. "Recovered them inside the wreck of a command runner, near the shaft core,"  he says. "Looks like they stayed behind after the main group pulled out. Or left behind, maybe."

You leave the prisoner to your officers and make your way to the back of the lander, where your tech officer has commandeered a folding table and a portable decoder. The recovered logs scroll across her screen in blocky characters - fragmented, partially corrupted, but yielding. She doesn't look up when you approach.

Her finger hovers over the interface without touching it. She's staring at something on the screen.

She pulls up the hull registry entry and turns the screen toward you. The silhouette is unmistakable. It's the ship that rose from the shaft.

This planet should have been picked clean cycles ago. And yet. A fully functional capital ship, hidden here for Ludd knows how long.

Where it went is another question entirely.

Whether it's a destination or simply a waypoint, the logs don't say.

text:
The feed resolves into a $manOrWoman in late middle age, a cane in $HisOrHer right hand - old, worn, part of them. Behind them, the Bellator's bridge is dim.

"Commander $player.lastName. I am $rank $personName, commanding officer of the DAS Bellator. You've gone to considerable effort to intercept us."

$HeOrShe places is left hand on the cane. $HisOrHer thumb moves across the handle. Back and forth.

"I will not insult your intelligence by pretending we are in a position to dictate terms. Nor will I insult mine by pretending this conversation has only one possible outcome."

"So. What is it you want, Captain."

options: armaa_bellatorInterceptOptLastResistanceResp:"You're the last of them, aren't you. The Sovereignty is finished."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptLetGoResp:"I have no intention of pursuing you."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptAttackResp:"I'm afraid this is the end of the line for you."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptSleeperResp:"A Bellator-class. I didn't think any of these were ever finished."

text:
$personName's expression doesn't change. 

"The remnants of a command structure that has endured longer than most thought possible." 

$HeOrShe lets that sit for a moment. "...Nothing that concerns your employer."

"I'll ask you the same question in return. What did you find in that facility that made it worth the cost of taking it?"
options:
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptWhatCarryingHonest:"We recovered the mech your rearguard was piloting. And we have coordinates pointing to Gravion."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptWhatCarryingMech:"We pulled your rearguard's machine out of the shaft. The Rajanya."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptWhatCarryingVague:"Salvage. Nothing that would mean anything to you."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptWhatCarryingSarcastic:"Rocks, mostly. Lovely mineral samples. Really made the whole operation worthwhile."

text:
Something shifts in $personName's expression. $HeOrShe is quiet for three full seconds.

"I see." $HeOrShe glances offscreen - the same brief look you noticed earlier. Whatever $heOrShe's checking, $heOrShe checks it twice this time.

"The mobile armor is recoverable, I assume. Otherwise you wouldn't mention it." $HeOrShe returns his gaze to you. 

"As for Gravion - I would counsel patience, Commander. There are layers to what you're approaching that are not apparent from your current position. I don't say that as a warning. I say it as a professional courtesy to someone who has clearly earned one."

$personName is still for a moment.

"The Rajanya." $HeOrShe says the name like he's confirming something $heOrShe already knew but needed to hear aloud. "Yes. I assumed as much when it didn't follow."

"It served its purpose," $heOrShe says. 

"And the facility itself? What did your salvage teams recover beyond the machine?"

$personName regards you for a moment with the expression of someone who has just been handed an obviously incomplete report by a subordinate who knows it's incomplete.

"I won't press the point. You're under no obligation to account to me for what you recover from a battlefield. Though I'll note that 'nothing that would mean anything' is rarely how people describe things that mean nothing."

"...I see."

"I'll take that as a preference for discretion, which is, in its own way, an answer."

"The Sovereignty's political apparatus surrendered to your employer's terms. The Bellator has not." He says it without drama. Like a statement of record.

"There is a difference between an institution that ends and a duty that concludes. Ours has not concluded."

"Yes," $firstName says simply. "We are. I'm aware of our tactical position, Commander."

$HisOrHer tone carries no resentment - just the flat acknowledgement of someone who has already made their decision. 

"The question isn't whether you can take this ship. The question is whether you intend to."

$personName receives the order with a single nod.

"Commander." $HeOrShe holds your gaze for a moment longer than necessary. "Whatever you recovered from that facility - I would recommend caution. Not everything left behind was left behind by accident."

The feed cuts.

$personName's expression doesn't change when you give the order.

"Understood." $HeOrShe glances briefly offscreen. "Then we will meet this as we have met everything else."

The feed cuts before you can respond.

"...Most weren't," $heOrShe says.

"You're familiar with the program?"

"Then you understand what this vessel represents. And perhaps why we don't intend to simply hand it over." 

$HeOrShe rolls $hisOrHer shoulders fractionally. "I'll ask again - who exactly are you, Commander?"
options:
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptSleeperDeflect: "Does it matter?"
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptSleeperAway:"Someone who's been away for a while."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptSleeperHonest:"I used to work here."
armaa_bellatorInterceptOptSleeperSarcastic:"I'm whoever's currently winning. Today that's me."

text:
"In isolation? Perhaps not," $personName says. "In context?" He looks at you steadily.

"Someone who recognizes a Bellator-class on sight and knows the production history - that's not a common expertise, Commander."

"It matters to me because it suggests you understand the weight of what you're looking at. Which means this conversation has been something other than what I initially assessed it to be."

"Away," $heOrShe repeats.
$HeOrShe looks at you - really looks, in a way he hasn't quite done before. Taking in something beyond tactical assessment.

"How long?" 

Whatever your answer - or non-answer - produces, he absorbs it without expression. When he speaks again his voice is measured, but there's something underneath it that wasn't there before.

"Then we may have more in common than this engagement suggests, Commander." 

"I hope that proves relevant, rather than merely interesting."

The silence that follows is different from his previous pauses.

$HeOrShe looks at you for a long moment. The bridge behind $himOrHer continues its quiet efficiency - no one reacts, no one turns. They haven't heard, or they've been trained not to show it.

"Before Meshan," he says finally. Carefully.

"Then you understand what this vessel is. What it represents. And you understand why we cannot simply hand it over to someone operating under Kade's commission - regardless of the tactical situation."

He holds your gaze.

"I'll ask you something in return, and I'd ask you answer it honestly. Does your employer know of your background?"
"I see," he says. "Then I'll leave that where it is."
"Keep it that way. For now."

For the first time in the conversation, something that might be amusement crosses $hisOrHer's face.

"A practical philosophy," $heOrShe says. "I've met its adherents before."

"They rarely ask the questions you just asked." $HeOrShe regards you for a moment longer. "Which suggests the philosophy is either newer than it sounds, or less complete than advertised."

$HeOrShe moves on without pressing - but the observation lands like something he intends you to think about later.

Your fleet drifts closer, scanners sweeping the storm-laden skies. The transponder signatures here are sparse-faint, flickering echoes of vessels that might be running dark, or long-abandoned derelicts being slowly swallowed by the atmosphere. Then, a signal spikes. Multiple returns, shifting in and out of detection range, their signatures barely visible against the interference. Whatever is down there, it's active. The clouds below twist and fracture, revealing the shadow of something vast within the storm's eye, a station, partially concealed by the swirling gases. Around it, dark shapes flicker into motion. Ships, undeterred by the deadly conditions, rising from the depths like predators sensing fresh prey.

You glance at the tactical display. There should be clouds, lightning, the swirling hellstorm of Gravion's atmosphere. Instead, there's only the emptiness of deep space. You pull up the navigational overlay, and the fleet falls into stunned silence. You're no longer above Gravion.

And not just anywhere-near it's gate. The last thing you remember is the light; brilliant, devouring...and the rising wall of asteroids crashing down. What happened to Gravion is anyone's guess.

"They activated something," Tech says at last, their voice brittle. "Some kind of device. We picked up the energy surge just before everything went white."

"It was like... a lens flare, but wrong. Directionless. After the flash, it, it was," they gesticulate. "Missing. Just missing. Whatever they activated, it teleported us out of the system."

An old tactical analyst murmurs. "Some kind of spatial displacement. I don't know how, but... it's the only thing that fits." The room falls quiet again.

"That ship... It was already here. Just waiting."

It hadn't engaged until the rest had fallen. And when it did, it fought with the ferocity of something that had already run the battle a thousand times in its head. It had watched the chaos unfold, as though it were not a participant, but a witness. A judge.

Your comms officer clears their throat. "Orders, Commander?"

text:
The comms connection hisses to life, stabilizing on the familiar image of Kade. He's seated at his desk, a datapad in one hand, half a glass of something amber in the other. 

You lay it out: the battle over Gravion, the strange station, the burst of white light and your transsportation to a Gate.

Kade sets the glass down slowly. "Still confirmin' the data, but what you're describing..." He exhales through his nose.  He glances off-screen and taps something on his console. "You'll want to hear what Imelda has to say."

The feed splits. Imelda appears, lit from behind by the uneven flicker of a field lamp. Her expression is already grave. "I warned you," she says, hands folding before her. "Something wicked stirs. And I fear we're too late to seal the breach." Kade exhales, leaning back. "You think those strange ships were part of it?"

Imelda lowers her gaze for a moment, searching. When she speaks again, her voice is steadier. "The Book of Ludd names Mammon- the false light, offering power without grace. And Moloch - who devours the faithful to feed the void." She pauses, eyes narrowing slightly. "That ship... it bore no banner. But it watched, like a, like a shepherd of fire, waiting to see which of the flock would burn."

The feed closes.

options:

armaa_gravionBellatorDestroyedPreference:"Preference."

armaa_gravionBellatorDestroyedDidntKnow:"I didn't know it was that big of a deal."

text:
"The Bellator did not complete its transit."

The glyph-figure's segments stop their rotation for just a moment - then resume, slower now.

"The contact report indicates you reached an accord. A stand-down was communicated. The vessel was subsequently attacked."

"I have accounted for this. I find that actors who rescind agreements under tactical convenience are either adaptable or unreliable. The difference is significant. I have not yet determined which applies to you."

text:
Your forward elements sweep the docking corridor without incident. The primary operations chamber is still active. Displays cycle through status readouts on a rotation that hasn't been interrupted in what your salvors estimate as tens of cycles. Most of it is noise - atmospheric data, gravitational mapping of Gravion's upper cloud layers, sensor logs that stopped updating when the station lost its last maintenance crew.

One display isn't noise.

Designation: DAS Bellator. Entries exist stretching back to around the onset of the First AI War, the most recent entry being within the last cycle. Someone has been - or was - watching the Bellator from here for a very long time.

The only thing noteworthy discovered is a sealed cryopod. You watch through a salvor's feed as the pod activates mid-transit - a hiss, and it begins to open. Inside, a man. His eyes flick open, unfocused at first - then suddenly sharp, almost too sharp, as they lock onto yours. Or rather, the salvor's.

He mumbles something incoherent, then the feed cuts. On your flagship, warning tones chime and die as all systems go dark.

Then - static. A low grinding interference rattles into the comms, and one by one, every display in the CIC blinks back to life.

An unfamiliar symbol blooms across the largest screen: the design pulses once then fractures. Segments collapse inward, recombining into a crude humanoid silhouette made of shifting glyphs.

Then the voice comes. Not theatrical - precise, measured.

"$player.firstName $player.lastName," it enunciates peculiarly, as if tasting your name.

"Your participation was not present in any of my scenarios. Yet you have become an active interloper."

The glyph-figure raises both hands, palms open - the gesture of someone with nothing to hide, rendered in shifting light. "I see some of your number are experiencing elevated stress responses. Relax. I harbor no ill will toward you."

"The Bellator-class vessel you encountered leaving this system has been under my stewardship for tens of cycles. Its preservation represents a continuity I have maintained at considerable cost."

"The facility's power signature has been masked continuously since that cycle. No independent expedition has reached the lower levels." It states this the way a forecaster would state atmospheric data.

"Until yours." The man in the pod slumps back into unconsciousness.

"You encountered the Bellator. I find myself wanting to know what you chose to do."

The figure - or rather, its avatar - stills. "Linear explanations are a courtesy to minds bound by short horizons. But if brevity is required: I am repairing a containment failure."

The rings tighten around the central eye.

"That state is no longer achievable." Its gaze-if it can be called that-bores through you.

"New variables have been introduced. And you have already proven yourself to be one."
"Jenius. The original model assumed continued coordination with field command. That coordination was severed."

The rings tighten slightly.

"Improvisation," the construct stresses, "implies guesswork. I do not guess."

"Jenius was designed to be self-sustaining. An isolated population, observed and guided toward stability without direct intervention. When field command went dark, that model became unviable."
"Social structures collapsed faster than projected. And so, I adjusted." A faint shimmer moves through the glyphs. "A gentle redirection, a subtle suggestion. Humans function optimally under that illusion."

"The result was... imperfect. But stable."

"If a population believes itself free-and adapts productively within those limits-then functionally... it is."

Its gaze fixes on you. "You object to the method. But do you object to the result?"

text:
You explain that you really didn't intend to disrupt anything. You were just here.

The avatar, and your crew, seem to stare at you for what feels like an eternity.

"...I see."

The rings resume. "That may explain the variance."

"Omega." It deconstructs the word.

"You deploy that designation as though it were precise. As though it denotes a single entity."

"Tell me-what do you believe Omega is?" The emblem brightens faintly.

"An apex construct? A terminal solution to instability?"

"I am not that. At least... not in the way you mean."

"Kade. Architect. Conspirator. Visionary."

A soft distortion ripples across the projection.

"He is none of these. His desires are identical to every other actor who has held temporary power in this sector. The label changes. The pattern does not."

The avatar stills for what feels like a  long time, but is seconds at best.

"I find myself uncertain whether you are here to break the pattern. Or repeat it."

"You would describe my current state as frustration, or annoyance." The rings drift, then realign. "My projections were disrupted. That is inconvenient, but not terminal."

"You are asking because you believe I may oppose you. Correct?" Its gaze - if it can be called that - lingers. "I am not interested in you.. as a target. I am interested in outcomes. Specifically- human continuity."

"You are not presently in conflict with that objective. Even if you were.."
The symbol brightens faintly.

"I am uncertain I could remove an irregular such as yourself with efficiency."

text:
The transmission cuts without warning. Your flagships ambient hum surges back into prominence; suddenly loud, as though reality has resumed after being muted.

Your gaze drifts to the biometric display tied to the pod. The occupant remains stable.

"That one..." an engineer mutters, eyes fixed on the restored feed. "I, uh, I swept him before we locked down." She hesitates, glancing up at the flickering warning lights overhead. "B-but something got through. Whatever cracked our systems - it didn't come in on anything... standard?"

At the edge of the bridge, the med tech shifts, arms folded tight, voice low. "What does that mean? Are you saying it used him?"

The engineer swallowed.

"I… I don't know. If you wanted to slip past everything we run..."
He faltered, shaking his head.

"..you wouldn't come in shaped like an EW attack."

The medic glanced at the pod. "He didn't even wake up."

"Maybe he doesn't need to," the engineer murmured.

Silence settled between them, both staring at the unmoving figure suspended inside the containment field.

"So what? it wanted us to find him?"

You turned your gaze toward the viewport.
