text:
The comm panel pings- sharp, insistent.A face resolves in the feed under hard white light. The med-bay behind them is all hiss and chirp and hurried footsteps.

"Your Gravion popsicle just sat up and started looking around. Vitals are holding. Warming protocol's doing its job, but he's still coming up slow."

"He gave a name. Cassian Pha. And he's asking for you."

The lift drops you into your flagships midline with a muted thunk. The corridor outside medical smells faintly of antiseptic and overheated circuitry. Two of your security detail stand off to the side of the hatch, deliberately casual. They nod once and don't say a word.

The same medical officer from the comm feed intercepts you before you can take three steps. 

"Captain," they say, then jerk a thumb toward the back ward. "He's in the observation bay." They start walking without waiting for you to follow.

They stop at a transparent partition. Beyond it, Cassian sits upright on the edge of a medical cot, wrapped in a thermal blanket that looks comically small on him.  

The officer taps the glass softly with two knuckles. Cassian doesn't startle. He doesn't even look up, not immediately. He finishes reading whatever line he's on, then sets the TriPad down on his lap with care, like it's an artifact.

"Captain's here," the officer says. Then, to you, more quietly: "We gave him a TriPad and a filtered brief. State of the Sector,  quick timeline of events."
"No, mine."

They drop their voice again. "We floated some names. Tri-Tachyon. Hegemony. The AI Wars. He took it like a man reading a casualty list."

"Then we asked about the intelligence that contacted us over Gravion."

The officer watches you, gauging your reaction. "Nothing, No tells. He asked if it was a ship class. When we clarified, he said he didn't know the name."

They thumb a control and the speaker intercom clicks on - one-way, so Cassian can hear you if you speak, but you can keep the distance as long as you want it.

"Here's what you should know before you walk in," the officer says. "He's cold-slept. He's underweight. He could barely throw a punch hard enough to bruise a fruit." Their eyes flick to the old interface marks on Cassian's temples.

"Also: he asked who currently heads House Pha. Maybe it's some kind of Eventide thing." Cassian looks up again and meets your gaze through the glass.

options: armaa_cassianPost7aResp:Ask Medical what, exactly, Cassian asked on the TriPad
armaa_cassianPost8:Enter the observation bay and speak with Cassian directly

text:
They thumb their TriPad awake and bring up a list - not a transcript, not quite. More like a triage summary.

"Asked the cycle once, got the answer, and never went back to it." They tilt the pad so you can read the headings he kept circling:

Polities, claims of sovereignty, domain-era infrastructure status, AI prohibitions, corporate continuity, and... legal personhood?

"He didn't ask about family. He didn't ask about the wars. He read right over it. And he didn't ask about you.  Who you are, what you fly, what you're allegiances are."

"So," they finish, voice dry. "He knows what he's looking for." They look at you, then back to the door controls.

text:
The door cycles with a soft hiss. Warm air rolls out of the bay - carrying that sharp antiseptic bite that sticks in the back of your throat.

He reaches for the TriPad on the cot and taps the screen awake. A Sector map blooms in dull colors. "Petty crowns on a graveyard. Your people told me my pod was recovered above Gravion."

"They did not tell me why a ship was in a position to recover it, or why it was there. And they did not tell me why the pod survived a planetary displacement event intact." He looks back at you.

"I imagine you have not heard of my surname, and If you have, you heard it attached to a company. As you are aware, I was put in that pod a century ago."

"Pick your preferred lie. I agreed to it because the alternative was becoming a liability to the people who claimed to need me."

His eyes narrow by a fraction. The first real emotion you've seen, and it isn't fear.

It's irritation.

"Sera Pha," he says at last, enunciating it like he's reading it off a bill of lading. "Your TriPad lists her as the Chief Executive of Arma Armatura."

 The calm cracks. "I have no siblings," Cassian says. "Nor legitimate children. No extended family."

"So you will explain this to me." He holds the gaze for another moment, then continues as if he hasn't just demanded an answer. 

"Before you try to trade stories," he adds, "understand that I am not asking to reclaim anything. I am asking to understand what has been built on top of my name." His eyes flick to the side, to where the pod debris would be stored.

"And I am asking," he says, voice still even, "because something out there took hold of my pod, and placed it where your hands would find it."

"And I know why such a mind would keep a man asleep for a century and then wake him in front of an audience." Cassian sets the TriPad, slow and deliberate.

"So," he says, "tell me, Captain." "Who is Sera Pha?"

"Then she survives by being useful to people who'd rather not admit they need her." He shifts the tri-pad on his lap.

"Which means..."

Cassian studies you for a long moment, then gives a small, almost polite nod. "Understood." Then, colder:

"That tells me enough."He taps the tri-pad once. "If you have intimacy with her, and you refuse to speak of it, then you believe she would be harmed by the truth… or you would."

text:
He looks past you - at nothing in particular. His eyes come back to you.

"I don't need her biography," Cassian continues. "I need to see." He taps the side of his temple with two fingers - near the fused port - as if indicating a point of failure. "Because my name is not something you wear by accident. And if she is wearing it," he says, voice even, "then she is either the heir to a system I built, or some creature that crawled out of it."

"You said she does business," Cassian goes on slowly. "Good. Then she understands terms." He reaches for the TriPad again, brings up the Sector map, then zooms in with a slow pinch gesture until the jumble of factions resolves into a familiar patchwork.

New Meshan sits there like a bruise that never fully healed. Cassian's thumb hovers over it. "Take me here," he says. "I will speak with Sera Pha in person."

"And before you say it: yes, I understand the risk. If this is a message, then the only way to read it is to put it in her hands and watch what she does with it. I will pay for transport," he adds, almost casually.

His eyes lift. "It seems the Sector has been generous while I slept. Compound interest is one of the few things that still behaves predictably." Cassian relaxes back against the cot, the posture of someone making a reasonable request of a service provider - as if this isn't a century-spanning identity crisis being delivered to a corporate throne.

"New Meshan," he repeats. "I want a seat on your ship. A stateroom." 

"And Captain?" He meets your gaze directly. "If you intend to lie to her about me, do it well. If you intend to sell me, do it openly. If you intend to protect her, understand you may be protecting her from herself." His voice stays level, but the weight behind it is unmistakable.

"Either way, we leave soon."

id: # Arriving to new meshan with cassian

text:
The docking clamps bite with a dull, mechanical certainty. New Meshan fills the viewport - familiar angles, familiar scars, a city that never quite decided whether it was rebuilding or just learning to live with the damage.

Cassian stands at the bridge edge, wrapped in a coat that hangs wrong on him. He watches like he's reading a balance sheet.

"Alright. This is the part where you stop being a courier and start being the hand that knocks." Cassian turns slightly - not quite facing you, but acknowledging your presence.

"I need a line to the one carrying my name. Raise Sera Pha on comms."

He says it like a routine instruction. But his fingers flex once at his side, the smallest tell you've seen yet.

You nod to your comms officer, who raises the appropriatae channel. New Meshan's local routing tags populate the list, a maze of corporate relays and polite dead ends.

You request a channel.

There's a pause - just long enough to feel intentional - and then the feed resolves into a clean, expensive frame.

Sera Pha appears under warm light. Impeccably put together.  "Captain."

Her voice is smooth and unhurried, with that specific cadence people use when they want you to feel like you've intruded into their schedule.

"I hear you've arrived in-system with something interesting in your hold."

Her eyes flick once, subtly, to the side of her display - reading metadata. She's doing it as she speaks.

"And you didn't even say hello first. I'm wounded."

"Talk to me. What did you bring home?"

Her smile sharpens. 

"You're not one of my usual captains. That means you either have very good reasons… or very bad ones."

She inclines her head slightly, almost looking down at you.

"Which is it?"

text:
"Coherent, is he." she states. "That's generous of him." Her eyes dart to the side of her display again.

"Captain, you understand what you've just said to me. Is he with you, physically? On your ship. Right now." She exhales, slow, and the warmth comes back like a curtain rising. 

"Alright. Here are the rules." She flicks a finger and a new overlay appears.

"Bring him to the surface. I'll send your people coords to where i'm staying currently." 

"I'll be the welcoming commitee. You'll find that reassuring. He might find it insulting."

Not a moment sooner, you're notified that the location has been transferred. You're free to depart via shuttle at your command.

options: armaa_hcChalet:Take a shuttle to visit Sera Pha with Cassian

text:
New Meshan's lights slide beneath you in neat grids and stubborn clusters, the city's scars softened by altitude until they look like design choices.

The coordinates for the rendevous point take you away from the docks and into the hills.

The chalet sits above the city in a pocket of cultivated darkness - glass, stone, and clean lines; exterior lights are low and warm, positioned to flatter the structure rather than illuminate it. A thin perimeter fence traces the property. 

The landing pad is private and immaculate. A woman waits at the edge of the pad, standing there under the low pad lights like she stepped out for an evening appointment. Dark coat, immaculate cut, hair arranged with the kind of care that looks effortless because it wasn't. A small comm earpiece sits flush at her ear, almost invisible.

Sera Pha smiles as you disembark.

"Captain," she says, and the way she says it makes the title sound like a personal favor.

Her eyes flick once to Cassian, and the smile doesn't change. Only the temperature behind it does. "Cassian Pha."

Cassian steps down slowly. He doesn't look around. His attention stays on her like he's afraid the outline might change if he blinks. "You built a nice perch," he says.

Sera's smile brightens. "New Meshan has its charms. Some of them even survive contact with reality."

She turns slightly, angling herself so the chalet's entrance is behind her. "Come inside," she says. "It's colder up here than people expect."

Inside, the space is tastefully minimal. Stone floors, dark wood, soft lighting placed to guide the eye. A wall display shows a slow-moving Sector map in muted tones, more art piece than tactical tool.

Sera leads you down a short hall to a door with no markings. She stops there and turns back to face you and Cassian.

"This room is shielded," she says conversationally. "No surprises. I'm sure you'll still assume the opposite."

"And you?" he asks. "Do you assume the opposite too?"

Her smile doesn't falter. "I assume everyone is lying to me until they prove they're not." She opens the door.

A small meeting room. Simple table, three chairs and a carafe of water. The lighting is warm and even, flattering in a way that feels intentional.

Sera steps inside first and takes one of the seats. She folds her hands neatly.

Cassian doesn't sit right away.

He stands across from her, coat hanging off his shoulders, posture straight despite the frailty. For a moment he looks absurdly out of place in the room; a relic of a different era brought into a modern corporate set. Then he looks exactly like he belongs.

"You're using  my surname." Cassian says, and the calm in his voice is the calm of someone holding a vibroknife behind their back.

"Am I?" she replies, softly, pleasantly. Cassian's gaze doesn't move. "I woke up and found you in a registry," he says. "A person i've never met, who claims authority over the company I built." Sera's smile doesn't falter. Her voice stays warm. "Arma Armatura has been many things across many crises," she says. "Names move. Titles change. People die. But systems persist."

Cassian steps closer to the table, stops just short of sitting. "Do you intend to beat around the bush?" he asks. "Or do you want to answer the question?"

"Which question, Cassian?"

Cassian's eyes narrow, finally. "Who are you?"

The room goes still. Sera's expression goes along with it, and she holds the silence for exactly long enough to make it clear it was her choice. Then her smile reassembles - the same rehearsed smile.

And for the first time, it looks like a weapon being primed.

"Who I need to be," she says plainly. She glances toward you, brief and assessing - like you are a variable in an equation that just became interesting.

Then her eyes return to Cassian. "And tonight," Sera Pha says, "I need to be someone who convinces you," her eyes flick to the nearby seat, "to sit down."

Cassian stares at her for a long time.

The, slowly, he pulls the chair out. "Then convince me," he says.

Sera's smile widens by a fraction.

A few meters down the hall, a woman in a skirt stands like a statue that learned to breathe, hands folded at the waist. She doesn't introduce herself. After a minute, she speaks anyway-eyes on a point over your shoulder, voice low and unhurried.

"You're not required to stand still, Captain. Please, make yourself comfortable while you wait."

From behind the door, you hear nothing. The room might be empty, for all your senses can prove. Your security detail lingers by the corridor junction, keeping their distance. They don't look at the Arma Armatura staffer. The staffer doesn't look at them. It's a mutual agreement to pretend either party doesn't exist.

A second figure appears soundlessly from an adjacent corridor: an aide with a tri-pad and a tray, dressed in soft fabric that looks like it never wrinkles. They stop at a polite distance and hold the tray out without speaking.

A glass. Something amber in a small cup that smells expensive and unnecessary. Afterwards, the aide retreats and vanishes again like a ghost that's been paid by the hour.

* * *
Cassian steps out first. He looks the same at a glance - but his calm has changed shape. His face carries a strange expression; you'd almost say it looks like defeat. His eyes find you immediately. "She's very good," he says. Cassian's gaze flicks once to the door behind him, then back.

"She offered me truth in measured portions," he continues. "And she refused to answer the only question that matters. Which is its own answer, I suppose."

The staffer's mouth twitches, almost a smile. "Ms. Pha will see you now," she says, as if you're arriving for an appointment that didn't involve a century-old identity crisis.

Cassian doesn't move to let you pass. He leans in just slightly, lowering his voice. "Captain," he says, carefully, "whatever she is… she is not surprised I came here." He straightens back up, expression neutral again. "That means someone told her in advance," he adds. "Or she already knew."

The staffer steps aside, smooth as a door mechanism.

"Inside," she says. "Please."
options:

armaa_hcChalet7b:Ask Cassian what she said

text:
"She didn't threaten me," he says. "That would be simple. She did something clever: she refused to confirm whether she carries my name because she inherited it… or because she stole it."

Cassian's eyes narrow, not angry, focused.

"She kept insisting on 'continuity.' Cryptic nonsense."

"And the part that changed my perception," He exhales once. "She said she wasn't surprised to see me."

Cassian watches your reaction like a man reading an instrument panel. "Ah - those were not her words," he corrects, precise. "Her words were: 'You're earlier than I expected.'"

The staffer's earpiece clicks again. She remains perfectly still, pretending not to hear.

Cassian lowers his voice another fraction. "...Captain. If this were some kind of trick, she should be panicking right now."
He glances toward the door. "She isn't."

Cassian steps back, giving you room. "Now you go in," he says.

The staffer steps aside, smooth as a machine. The door cycles shut behind you with the same soft, expensive hiss. Inside, Sera is already seated. Her smile arrives on schedule.

"Captain," she says, as if this is the first conversation tonight. "Thank you for indulging my preference." She gestures to the chair opposite her.

Her eyes flick once toward the door- not to check if it's sealed, but to acknowledge what it represents. "You chose the version of this where I speak freely," Sera continues. "So."

"Tell me what you want out of this," she says. "And tell me what you believe Cassian wants." She folds her hands.

"And then," Sera adds, pleasantly, "we'll decide together how much truth he can afford."

options: armaa_hcChaletOptMercResp:"I want compensation and a clear exit. Whatever this is, it's not my business."
armaa_hcChaletOptDirectResp:"I want to know what kind of danger I just hauled into your backyard."
armaa_hcChaletOptIdealResp:"I want to make sure you don't disappear him. He's under my protection until I'm satisfied."
armaa_hcChalet9:"That's all I had on my mind."

script:
SetEnabled armaa_hcChaletOptMercResp false
SetTooltip armaa_hcChaletOptMercResp "You don't take coin to sign away someone else's freedom."

SetEnabled armaa_hcChaletOptIdealResp false
SetTooltip armaa_hcChaletOptIdealResp "You're not going to play guardian now. Whatever you are, you're not that."

text:
She taps her TriPad once. You get a clean transfer prompt - escrow-backed, local, no drama. 

"You've delivered a volatile package without breaking it. That has value." A brief pause.

"You'll leave this room with compensation, and you'll leave this property with your shuttle. Cassian will remain here." Her eyes hold yours, calm as glass.

"And because you're thinking it: no, I'm not going to 'disappear' him. If I wanted him gone, he wouldn't have made it past the shuttle pad." She folds her hands again, neat and patient.

"As for Gravion - and your Iron King - I don't have the courtesy of certainty. But I do have a working read: you were used to deliver him to New Meshan. That's all."

Sera's expression warms by a degree, like she's indulging a habit she knows you have. "Of course you do." She flicks her tri-pad.

"You're not wrong. This isn't your business. It just landed on your deck. But don't confuse 'not your business' with 'not your problem.' You can walk away from this, $player.firstName. It won't necessarily walk away from you."

"Cassian isn't in danger. I expected him, eventually. Names like ours don't stay buried forever."

The word ours is soft, almost casual. Then she moves past it.

"Take your compensation. If you want a clean exit, you'll get it. If you want a favor instead… ask later, when we're not standing over a loaded question."

Sera's smile thins into something more professional. "Fair."

"The danger is not Cassian himself. Physically, he's a man who has been asleep since the First AI War and woke up into a Sector that would rather forget that era existed. The danger is what he represents." She sets her hands flat on the table, not quite a gesture of honesty, more like a way to anchor the room.

"He carries a legacy some would rather leave buried. Systems recognize it. People will recognize it, too, once it circulates." Her eyes lift. "That attracts predators."

A pause, then a small, controlled shrug.

"As for the Iron King: I don't see a direct threat in the act itself. If it wanted Cassian dead, he'd be dead. If it wanted me dead, you wouldn't be sitting here."

Her smile returns, mild. "It released him because it expected him to come here. The reason it cares about that… is still unclear. That doesn't mean it's harmless, mind."

Sera studies you like she's deciding how much candor you've earned, then sighs lightly - "Alright. Cassian is not the sword, but it's sheath. Which means whoever carries him is assumed to have the blade."

"He's a piece of legitimacy that certain systems still obey. That means there are factions in this Sector who would pay obscene sums to control him, and others who would kill him on principle."

"And if they can't get to him, they'll get to whoever carried him."

"Which is you." She reclines again, smile faint. "This is why I wish to keep him in my orbit."

"As for IRON KING… I think it's placing pieces on a chess board."

She looks past you for a moment.

"It let you find him because it knew where he'd go. New Meshan. Or more accurately, me." Sera's eyes return to you.

"I don't know why it wants that. But I don't feel threatened by the gesture."

Sera's smile turns polite in a way that makes it clear you've pushed the room into formal negotiation.

"That's cute. And unrealistic." She simply states.

"Your protection ends at my door, Captain. Not because I wish it to - because this is my jurisdiction, on my world, under my roof." She holds your gaze.

"However." The word arrives like a concession placed carefully on a table.

"I will not harm him. I will not confine him against his will. He may leave when he chooses."

"If you're worried about quiet endings, understand this: I gain nothing from one. Cassian alive is leverage. Cassian dead is a problem that breeds questions."
She folds her hands again, calm.

"So here's what you get: you may remain within the premises. Your crew may keep eyes on the perimeter. And if you want proof he eventually walks out of this building on his own feet some day, you'll have it."

Sera snorts. "$player.firstName, please. You don't need to posture with me, of all people."

"I'm not going to disappear him. I've planned for him, yes. That's not the same thing as wanting him." She glances toward the closed door as if she can see through it.

"Let's say we have a common history. The details aren't mine to share. If you want to protect him, protect him from the Sector. Stay on the premises if it helps. Keep your people at the perimeter. I won't object."

"But you don't get to supervise the conversation. That's his price for the truth, and mine for keeping it contained."

"Besides. If I meant him harm, you wouldn't have gotten an invitation. You'd have gotten an invoice - for cleanup, hazard pay, and the privilege of not asking questions."

Then Sera's smile softens, almost companionable, as if she'd only been discussing docking fees.

"That's a joke. Also," she adds, lightly, "I try not to kill family on the first meeting."

Her tone stays pleasant, almost conversational, as if she's outlining shipping arrangements instead of deciding the shape of a man's second life.

"Once he can stand without shaking and speak without sounding like a man reading an obituary, I'll let him walk the grounds. Then the facility. Then, if he insists, the city."

"The more complicated part is not keeping him alive," Sera continues. "The more complicated part is integrating him into a modern corporation without turning him into a spectacle." Her fingers tap the table once. Thoughtful.

"Arma Armatura has an executive board, of course. They are capable people. They are also... easily spooked. Imagine how they'll react when I tell them the company's former CEO just walked into my office." She meets your eyes.

She stands, smooth and unhurried, as if the conversation has already been filed and archived.

"Either way, Captain… you did the difficult part. You got him here intact." She steps toward the door and opens it.

Sera's expression turns gently approving. "Cassian," she says.

Cassian inclines his head. Barely. "Sera."

Not a greeting - more like a mutual recognition of an unresolved equation. Sera turns to you.

"Captain's leaving," she says, as if you're a courier completing a delivery. "

Cassian watches you for a moment longer than necessary.

"You did what you said you'd do," he says. "You brought me to the door." He goes briefly still - interfacing with something, probably. Sure enough, your TriPad chimes with a notification of your payment.

For a moment, you can almost pretend this is normal: a contact meeting, a handoff, a problem that belongs to someone else now.

text:
You graciously thank Kade for his concern, but insist it is not necessary. Weaving an intricate tale of your exploits - perhaps with a little bluster, perhaps not, you set forth a strong case for your employment, despite your $shipOrFleet's appearances.

He is silent for but a moment, before shrugging. "What do I have to lose, eh?"

options: armaa_IKBarEvent1Resp:"And what's in it for me?"
99:armaa_IKBarEvent2Resp:"I appreciate the offer but I've got my own business to attend to."
armaa_IKBarEvent3Resp:"I'll need assurances that the credits are worth the risk."

text:
"'Sides the credits, you're meanin'?" he says, as if stating the obvious. "There's old tech stashed in the dead ruins above and below the surface-stuff the locals and looters have only scratched the surface of. They've dug up scraps here and there, but the real prize is still buried deep."

He softens his gaze just a bit, as if letting you in on a secret. "Look, you take this on, and you'll be among those who get the first pick of what's down there. Could be lost tech, maybe intel worth its weight in gold."
"Fair enough, mate. Can't fault a spacer for looking out for themselves. But remember, opportunities like this don't come knocking every day. If you ever change your mind, you know where to find me."

"Look, I get it-no one's looking to stick their neck out just for some chump change. But this ain't some blind gamble. The risk's there, no doubt, but the reward? Enough to make you glad you took the chance. You got my word on that."

"As for oppo? Nothin' we can't handle. Mostly ground forces and some locals barely keepin' their scrap-heaps afloat. The approach should be the most difficult part, what with them drones about. They'll likely flip to KoS mode when things heat up."

"Just another casualty of the AI Wars," he says, shaking his head. 

"Oh, and the place is crawling with droneships," he adds with a raised brow. "No one has a clue who's behind 'em. They've been circling Jenius specifically for as long as anyone remembers, like they're making sure the place stays off-limits, but they sometimes wander further in."

He pauses, then smirks. "But that's where we come in, right? It's about time someone brought a little 'civilization' to this backwater and reminded these people what the real world looks like. I'll be the one to put this rock back on the map."

Those drones up there? We'll clear them out and proceed from there."

"Maybe one day I'll find my way back to greener ground. But for now?" She shrugs. "I'll keep plantin' what seeds I can, wherever I end up."

text:
"Long time to be gone. The Sector's had whole wars in that span. Factions rising and falling. And you just... stepped back in." She shakes her head slightly.

"Don't know how you carry that around."

"Reckon that's the only honest answer, ain't it."

She turns her glass.

"I grew up hearin' about what the Sector looked like back then - not fondly, mind you. Lot of it sounds like it needed changin'. Some of it sounds like it didn't deserve what it got either."

"Can't argue with that." The laugh settles into something quieter. "Though I wonder sometimes if the people who didn't make it through would've said the same, given the choice." She takes a sip.

"Glad you're here though. For what it's worth."

"Alright." No pressure in it. "Somethin' like that doesn't have to come out in a bar."

"Just figured I'd ask while I had you. Curious what a person does with that kind of weight. ..Still am, if you ever feel like answerin'."

"Most people out here are. Some of 'em find it, some figure out halfway through they were lookin' for the wrong thing. You seem like you've got enough sense to know the difference. Hope so, anyway."

"I started there too. My first gig was hauler crew out of Baetis. Figured if I could fly I'd see enough of the sector to know what I actually wanted." She laughs.

"Still working on what that means, honestly."

"Yeah? That's a weight to carry. Someone else's road."

"Question is whether it's still yours, or just somethin' you inherited and kept goin' with 'cause nobody told you to stop."

"My father's father built a chapel on Baetis with his own hands. Stone by stone. Took him eleven years."

"Honest answer. Most people dress it up - 'freedom,' 'adventure,' whatever sits better at night. Credits keep you breathin'. Nothin' wrong with sayin' so."
She takes a sip. "Long as the work don't start costin' more than it pays. Seen that happen to good people."

"I had a number," she says. "When I started. Hit it and go home, buy something nice, stop living out of a bunk. 'But Dawn,' you're wonderin..'why're still here then', right?"

"Took me a while to figure out the number was never really the thing. It was just something to aim at while I figured out what the thing actually was. Still figuring that out. But at least I know that's what I'm doing now."

"We've got ourselves an idealist." 

"I've met people who meant it. Fewer than you'd think, more than you'd expect."

"Sector's got no shortage of things that need fixin'. Just make sure you've got a clear picture of what justice actually looks like before you start handin' it out." She taps her temple with an index finger.

"Gets messy otherwise."

She laughs.

"I guess it's one way to pass the time. Could do worse."
She laughs."Sometimes the truth is you just ended up here and kept goin'. Nothin' wrong with that either."

She stares at you for a long moment and takes a very slow sip of her drink.

"...Right. Well."She sets the glass down carefully.
"You're ...interesting, I'll give you that."

She picks the glass back up.

"Gonna pretend you said somethin' else and we'll move on."

"Started out workin' crew on a hauler," she says. "Eventually wrangled my way into a cockpit." She turns her cup. "That's the short version, anyway. Some jobs good, some you don't talk about."

"Then Arma Armatura came sniffin' around. Saw something they liked, made an offer." She exhales. "Been a hell of a run since." After a moment she flags the keep and produces a bottle. She nudges the glass toward you.
"Go on."

You take a sip. Honey-sweet, faint citrus, sharper at the back than you expected. "Picked this up on one of my runs." She pours. "Colony drink from a place got wiped in the AI Wars. What's left of 'em still make it somewhere."

text:
The night runs longer than either of you planned.

A few nearby patrons drift into the orbit of the conversation - and at some point your guards make their  presence known with a look that means 'when you're ready'.

Dawn clocks it and sets her cup down. "I see, I see. Duty calls," she announces. She stands and tucks the bottle under her arm. "And one for the road."
"Good talk, Captain. I'll hang back and cover your retreat. Guard this cargo with your life." She hands one of your guards the bottle and fires a lazy salute before marching into the bar at an exaggerated pace.

Your guards fall in around you as you move toward the shuttle. The air outside is cooler than the bar. 'Good talk' indeed, you figure. The sector's full of worse ones.
You politely excuse yourself.

text:
As you approach, Dawn waves you over with a particular enthusiasm.

She says conversation like it no longer fits that term.

$armaa_dawnGuestName raises their glass without a hint of embarrassment.

Dawn points at them. "That is a lie and you know it."

"It's absolutely not."

"We've been debating something important," Dawn announces, leaning forward with the gravity of someone who has had enough to drink to find everything important. "You're just in time! Help me prove a point."

She looks at you.

"Simple question. You're out in the black. Long haul. No comms, no landmarks, nothing but stars. What do you do with the downtime?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName raises their glass without a hint of embarrassment.

"Captain. She started it."

She looks at you.

"Simple question. You're out in the black. Long haul. No comms, no landmarks, nothing but stars. What do you do with the quiet?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName straightens slightly - not quite snapping to attention, but close. The drink in their hand suddenly looks like evidence.

"Captain. I was just - we were discussing..." They glance at Dawn.

"Navigation philosophy," Dawn interjects.

"That is not what we were discussing."

"Coulda been. Look - We've been debatin' something IMPORTANT," Dawn announces, leaning forward with the gravity of someone who has had enough to drink to find everything important. "And I want a tiebreaker."

"Simple question. You're out in the black. Long haul. No comms, no landmarks, nothing but hyperspace. What do you do with the downtime?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName gives you the careful look of someone running a quick calculation about how this looks and deciding it probably looks fine.

"Captain." A measured pause. "I want it on the record that I'm off duty."

Dawn snorts. "Nobody asked you to put anything on the record."

"I'm just establishing context."

"We've been debating something important," Dawn announces, leaning forward with the gravity of someone who has had enough to drink to find everything important. "And I want a tiebreaker."

She looks at you.

"Simple question. You're out in the black. Long haul. No comms, no landmarks, nothing but stars. What do you do with the quiet?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName looks briefly like they might stand up, then decide against it.

"Captain. I - Dawn invited me. I wasn't - I mean, I would have mentioned it if I'd known you'd-"

Dawn puts a hand on their arm. "Breathe," she says simply.

They breathe.

"We've been debating something important," Dawn announces, leaning forward with the gravity of someone who has had enough to drink to find everything important. "And I want a tiebreaker."

She looks at you.

"Simple question. You're out in the black. Long haul. No comms, no landmarks, nothing but stars. What do you do with the quiet?"

You lean back, close your eyes, and let the quiet take you.

Dawn points at you immediately. "See," she says to $armaa_dawnGuestName. "Efficient. Practical. No nonsense."
"That's...an answer, I suppose."  $armaa_dawnGuestName says.
"...How? I just lie there thinking about everything I could be doing."  $armaa_dawnGuestName says it like this is a genuine mystery they've never solved.
$armaa_dawnGuestName offers a small nod. "Reasonable."
"...Also my answer, actually."  $armaa_dawnGuestName says.
"I usually can't sleep.",  $armaa_dawnGuestName laments. "I just didn't want to say that."

You keep busy. There's always something that needs doing.

Dawn exhales through her nose. "Of course you do." Not unkind - just the response of someone who suspected as much.
"Same."  $armaa_dawnGuestName taps the table. "Idle hands and all that."
"Same, but I always end up doing three things at once and finishing none of them."  $armaa_dawnGuestName offers a cheerful shrug. "Works out eventually."
"Same, usually. Though I've started keeping a book. Just in case.",  $armaa_dawnGuestName says.
"I make lists," $armaa_dawnGuestName offers. "Maintenance priorities, contingency reviews..." They trail off, aware of how that sounds. "It helps."
$armaa_dawnGuestName nods vigorously. "Yes. Exactly. Work is good. Work makes sense."

You sit with it. Let your thoughts wander.

Dawn tilts her head, somewhere between impressed and suspicious.

"Think? Think about what?"
"That's either very deep or very concerning.",  $armaa_dawnGuestName says.
"Everything. Nothing. Whatever shows up."  $armaa_dawnGuestName taps the side of their head. "It's loud in there."
"That's honest." $armaa_dawnGuestName considers it. "I do that too. More than I'd admit in a briefing."
"I think too,"  $armaa_dawnGuestName says carefully. "Mostly about what could go wrong. That's... probably not the same thing."
"Me too." $armaa_dawnGuestName says. They don't elaborate.

You don't do anything. Just exist in the moment.

Dawn stares at you for a moment. Then she turns to the $armaa_dawnGuestName with an expression of complete vindication.

"That," she says, "is exactly what I said."
"That's insane." $armaa_dawnGuestName says it with genuine respect.
$armaa_dawnGuestName leans forward. "Yes. Exactly. You just - let it happen. Most people can't do that." They point at you like you've passed a test they invented on the spot.
"...I think I understand that," $armaa_dawnGuestName says finally. "I'm not sure I can do it. But I understand it."

"How do you know nothing bad is happening if you're not thinking about it?" $armaa_dawnGuestName asks.

Dawn snorts. "That's the whole point, hon."

$armaa_dawnGuestName looks at you like you've said something in a language they're trying to learn. "I don't think I've ever done that."

Dawn smirks. "Maybe try it sometime."

Dawn is quiet for a moment.

"Used t'hate it," she says. "The quiet."

"My first long run on the ISS Edith Mae - freight hauler, nothing glamorous - was three weeks out past the Church lanes with the same five people and nothing but engine noise. I was the 'engineer'," she emphasizes this title with air quotes, "..close enough to one, anyway. Kept the drive cooling from becoming everyone's problem."
"But the quiet..." She exhales. "I'd pick fights just to fill it. Nothing serious. Enough to keep things interesting." She glances at her cup.

"Took me an embarrassing amount of time to figure out I was the problem."

"Ludd teaches that stillness is where you find what's true. Spent a long time wishin' that wasn't the case." She takes a sip. "Still not sure I've got it right. But I stopped fighting it somewhere along the way."

* * *

The conversation drifts from there. Dawn refills glasses without asking, and $armaa_dawnGuestName settles into whatever version of relaxed their personality allows. At some point a natural pause arrives - the kind that leaves room for you to make a graceful exit.

Dawn raises her glass slightly as you push back from the table. "Good talk, Captain."

text:
You spot Dawn before she spots you: several drinks past where she probably intended to stop, holding court with one of your officers and talking with absolute confidence.

She squints at you for half a second, before recognition fills her face and lights up. "There ya are! Captain-sit, sit, sit."

She gestures a little too broadly, nearly knocking her drink over, then corrects it with surprising grace.

"This is-" she pauses, searching for the word, "-important."

"You know $armaa_dawnGuestName-" she starts, then waves that off. "Doesn't matter. That's not the point."

She leans in slightly, like she's about to share something confidential.

"We were just talkin' about how easy it'd be."

She pauses, watching you with sudden, focused intent.

"To just... not."

She sits back, satisfied with that explanation.

$armaa_dawnGuestName scoffs lightly.

"Yeah, I heard you the first three times."

They glance at you, then back to Dawn.

"And now you're dragging the Captain into it?"

The bottle makes another round. Dawn watches it go with quiet satisfaction.

"Alrigh- no, wait. One more." She settles back. "You could drop everything tomorrow. No fleet, no contracts, no obligations. Just go."

"Where do you go?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName is already laughing before you're fully in earshot.

"-I'm telling you, that's the point-"

They spot you and immediately lift their glass a little too high.

"Captain. Perfect. Great. Excellent." Some of the drink sloshes, but they don't seem to care.

"She's been trying to convince me this is hypothetical." They grin, a little unsteady.

The bottle makes another round. Dawn watches it go with quiet satisfaction.

"Alrigh- no, wait. One more." She settles back. "You could drop everything tomorrow. No fleet, no contracts, no obligations. Just go."

"Where do you go?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName exhales slowly, like this has been going in circles for a while.

Their collar's slightly off, one button undone; nothing dramatic, but $armaa_dawnGuestName certainly looks... less put-together than usual.

"Right." They give you a small, familiar nod. "Captain."

"She's decided this requires outside input."

Dawn raises an eyebrow, satisfied. "It does."

They glance at her, then back to you. "I disagree. But here we are."

The bottle makes another round. Dawn watches it go with quiet satisfaction.

"Alrigh- no, wait. One more." She settles back. "You could drop everything tomorrow. No fleet, no contracts, no obligations. Just go."

"Where do you go?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName hasn't touched their drink in a while. They turn it slightly in their hand anyway, like they're checking something. "$playerSirOrMadam."

A small recalibration - not quite snapping to attention, but the posture shifts. You've changed the room.

"We were..." They glance at Dawn, who is watching you both with the unhurried patience of someone who has had enough to drink to find this genuinely interesting. 
"...working through something."

Dawn tilts her head. "That means I was askin' questions and they were decidin' how honest to be."

"I was being perfectly honest."

"You were being precise. There's a difference." They don't argue that.

The bottle makes another round. Dawn watches it go, then looks back at you with the particular focus of someone who has decided the night just got more interesting.

"Alright. One more. And now the Captain gets to answer too." She settles back.

"You could drop everything tomorrow. No fleet, no contracts, no obligations. Just go."

"Where do you go?"

$armaa_dawnGuestName goes still when you arrive.

Not startled. Just caught mid-something they weren't expecting to be caught mid.

They look at Dawn - who is watching them with the particular, unhurried attention of someone who's had enough to drink that they've stopped performing patience and started actually having it - then back at you.

"She asked me where I'd go. If I could go anywhere."

They look back down at their drink.

"I was still thinking about it."

The bottle makes another round. Dawn watches it go, then looks back at you with the particular focus of someone who has decided the night just got more interesting.

"Alright. One more. And now the Captain gets to answer too." She settles back.

"You could drop everything tomorrow. No fleet, no contracts, no obligations. Just go."

"Where do you go?"
Dawn grins. "Now we're talkin'."
"Frontier. Somewhere nobody's mapped properly yet."  $armaa_dawnGuestName says it like a challenge to the universe.
"Yes. Exactly. Pick a direction and burn."  $armaa_dawnGuestName makse a gesture indicating the entire galaxy. "All of it."

"There's a system I've had marked for a while."  $armaa_dawnGuestName doesn't elaborate immediately.  

"Just to see."

"I'd research it first. But yes. Somewhere new." 

The way they say it, it seems like a small concession to something $armaa_dawnGuestName doesn't usually allow themselves.
$armaa_dawnGuestName looks at the table. "I think about that sometimes. Just going somewhere nobody knows me."
Dawn tilts her head. "Unfinished business, or just comfortable?"
"There's a place I left before I was done."  $armaa_dawnGuestName doesn't specify. "Unfinished business."

"Both? There was this one station..."  

$armaa_dawnGuestName starts a story that clearly has no ending yet.
"Comfortable,"  $armaa_dawnGuestName says. "And someone I should have written to."
"Somewhere I know the layout.... and the exits."  $armaa_dawnGuestName says.
"Home. Or - or, what's left of it."  $armaa_dawnGuestName doesn't elaborate, and neither you or Dawn push.

Something crosses Dawn's face; not quite a response, not quite held back. She at you for a second longer than she means to.

"...That's either the saddest thing I've heard all night," she says finally, "or the most honest."

She pours you a drink without asking.

"Either way."
"That can't be right."  $armaa_dawnGuestName studies you like you're a puzzle. "Everybody's got somewhere."
"I don't believe you," $armaa_dawnGuestName says cheerfully. "But I respect it."
$armaa_dawnGuestName says nothing. They look at you like this is something they'll remember.
$armaa_dawnGuestName looks at their drink. Then at you. They don't say anything, but something in their expression suggests they understand exactly what you mean.

"W-well... the fleet is somewhere," $armaa_dawnGuestName offers carefully. "If that counts."

"I like it here."

"Baetis," Dawn says, a little quieter than usual.

"That's where I'm from. My parents were... devout. Properly devout. Not the kind that preaches about it - the kind that lives it like there's no other option." She looks at her drink.

"I don't say that to be unkind. They meant it. Still do, far as I know. Been a long time since I saw 'em."

"But there's a particular kind of ...suffocation that comes from growin' up in a house where every question already has an answer before you ask it."

"Told myself I'd go see something, come back when I'd gotten it out of my system."

The corner of her mouth moves.

"And that, wasss.." She stops, face pulling like she'd just tasted a spoiled Volturnian lobster,
"-a while ago."

"Faith stayed with me. That part's real. Always has been." She glances up. "But Ludd never said you had to stay in one place to believe in something. Or at least, that's what I decided he meant."

She refills her glass with the careful concentration of someone who has decided this is a monumental task.

"So. Baetis. I'd go back. Walk around a bit. See what's still there."

* * *

The conversation drifts after that - easier, looser. Dawn refills glasses without being asked.

At some point she trails off mid-sentence about something, realizes it, and doesn't bother finishing. $armaa_dawnGuestName lets it go.

Dawn raises her glass in your direction as you push back. "Good talk."

"...You're good people, $playerName." She says it simply, like it just occurred to her.

text: "Great. If something else comes up, it's no big deal. Just let me know when you do decide to head down." your officer replies without skipping a beat. She ends the connection.
You don't go into specifics, but you explain to Dawn that you'd prefer not to. She takes it pretty well, and you exchange a few more pleasantries before ending the connection.

You follow the path laid by others to the airlock. The manual controls allow you to pull it open to reveal a yawning black hole. 

Dawn trails not too far behind you, having stopped to observe something in the dust. She urges you to continue.

text:
Curate Sacraria Standfast greets Dawn and yourself with tea inside the echoing receiving hall before escorting you down to the inner shrine.

You enter the creaking, humid inspection module at the end of a modified extraction stack where the shrine is housed, against all reason. Crates and racks of weapons and ammunition are piled high, crowding the pilgrims/insurgents. Their unwashed bodies smell of sweat and smoke. They speak and pray quietly. 

The small shrine's centerpiece is set in an open space under a round diamond-matrix window holding back the crushing depths of the sea. A few candles flicker among the ranks of their dead comrades. As before, the curate sacraria re-lights a candle from one that is burning, and offers one for you to do the same.

text:
In the next niche over, a young knight-initiative gives you a challenging look. His head is freshly shaved; he must be very new to the order.

"You're not a Knight," he says quietly, making a show of looking over your uniform. "Nor one of us. Not really. Why are you here, spacer?"

Dawn looks the man up and down before interjecting. Her tone is casual-almost amused."Well now, ain't every day I get grilled by a fella still smellin' like soap and fresh linen. We're just passin' through, lookin' for answers. Nothin' sinister." She glances at his robes, then at his clean-shaven scalp. "You always this fired up, or is this just your first week holdin' a stick?"

The initiate turns his attention briefly to your companion, folding his arms. 

"Our presence here is steeped in devotion. As for myself, yes, it is imperative to scrutinize those who traverse our consecrated enclave. One must exercise caution to preserve the sanctity of this shrine. While you may be on a brief pause, we, in contrast, stand vigilant in safeguarding this sacred space." 

It almost sounds rehearsed.

text:
Subdued chanting, warped by the strange geometry of this cultic industrial space, blends with the air-cyclers. The candles burn clean and bright, gleaming off the multitude of bladed facets adorning every surface of the shrine's interior, a monument to sacral bloodshed.

Dawn's eyes widen as she takes in the grandeur of the shrine, a mix of awe and somber reflection playing across her features.
 
She moves closer, her boots echoing against the transformed industrial floor. Her gaze lingers on the solemn scene, her mind undoubtedly pondering the sacrifices and the tragic tale that unfolded in the name of devotion.

"Their final resting place is like- like a mosaic", she murmurs, a reverence in her voice. "Would hate to go out like this."

text:
The verdant continents of Jangala sprawl beneath you, crowned by huge weather systems churning lethargic and magisterial. 

Searing lines of orbital burn lasers flick out from Jangala station, invisible until they cut slashes of plasma through the thick atmosphere. Burn-glow competes with rampaging electrical storms to illuminate the mountainous cloud formations from below.

Dawn breaks the quiet hum of the shuttle's engines. 

"The orbital burning - do you know what it's for? Hegemony thinks it's a must, and they might be right. A necessary evil, of sorts..."

"Anyway, most of the faithful consider this planet to be sacred; exposing ourselves to that xenolife is a matter of honor for the devout. How do you see it?"

BeginConversation armaa_dawn True True
text:
Your shuttle descends toward verdant continents crowned by lethargically churning storm systems. The orbital burn lasers flicker from the spark of Jangala station, invisible until they cut lines of plasma through the atmosphere.

You pass through blinding cloud cover, the light taking on a sickly hue of green. Your viewport is streaked with marching droplets of spore-laden water as the shuttle slows to drop softly onto the landing pad nearly lost in the overgrowth of Jangalan foliage.

options: armaa_dawnJangalaConvoRespFaith:"If this is part of the faith, I'll do it."
armaa_dawnJangalaConvoRespCynical:"You sure this isn't just… dangerous? Feels like a good way to get sick."
armaa_dawnJangalaConvoRespAgnostic:"I get the safety angle. Still... I want to know what they’re actually trying to prove with this."
armaa_dawnJangalaConvoRespPather:"The very idea of harming the natural wonders of Jangala is sacrilege!"

text:
Dawn's mouth quirks, like she's half-teasing.
"Well now. Listen to you. Alright then, I'll take it. But if I come outta there itchier than a dock rat, I'm blamin' you."

Dawn squints, then snorts. "Yeah, it's dangerous. Most things worth doin' are." She shrugs. 

"Still. Ain't wrong to ask who's takin' the risk, and who's just collectin' the story after."
Dawn nods.

"I get where you're comin' from. Those that stomp through His garden without care oughta answer for it, sure enough." She pauses, voice softening into something more pragmatic.

"But... that ain't exactly how things play out, is it? Place like this- what, four... five million souls tryin' to carve out a life? The Church does what it can, I reckon. Sometimes that's about as much as anyone can hope for."

-At least, judging by the holos available on the public infonet, which are indeed gruesome. A cynical part of you wonders if the authorities allow distribution of these graphic accounts to promote protocol compliance.

Dawn grimaces. "Well, ain't that a revelation," she mutters.

"Seems like the Jangalan biosphere plays nice as long as you mind your safety steps."

text:
At their feet, more forms come into focus; covered by a quilt of mosses and mycelium, or whatever alien equivalent, which obscures the feet of the statues. Bones. Or are they fallen branches and overgrown rocks? The final wish for some terminally-devoted fanatics, or your imagination set loose?

The green churns not quite imperceptibly in the course of the minutes you watch, all-consuming, ferociously regenerative, a cycle of life that could be born of Earth or an alien world.

You are led past heavy curtains which mute the murmur of pilgrims and shut out all artificial light. Pilgrims kneel in the shrine-chamber, silhouetted before the glowing panes to without lit by that sickly yellow-green cloudcover.

Beyond, you see the statue garden. The forms blister with growths, crawling with tendrils, sprouting fruiting bodies of xenolife. Among them are pilgrims, exposed in only light robes. Easily overtaken by the overgrowth. At their feet, covered by a quilt of mosses and mycelium or alien equivalents, are bones. Or are they fallen branches, overgrown rocks? The final wish for terminally-devoted fanatics, or your imagination?

The green churns not quite imperceptibly in the course of the minutes you watch, all-consuming, ferociously regenerative, a cycle of life that could be born of Earth or an alien world.

The air hits you in the environmental lock, warm and wet, like high-G shock foam. You step out alone, for this is how it is done.

Oozing nets hang from the shrine complex; rhizomes sprouting luridly colored bulbs that glow in what passes for sunlight. Dust, or tiny bugs, or spores float about, moving in swirls against the heavy and languid air.

"Hey - heard you might be plannin' on heading down to the surface to see the shrine. Can I ask a favor? I'd appreciate it if I could tag along."

script:
$player.takingDawnToGilead = true
AdjustRepActivePerson 1
AddTextSmall "Dawn will now accompany when you descend to the surface" highlight
ShowDefaultVisual
text: "Great. If something else comes up, it's no big deal. Just let me know when you do decide to head down." your officer replies without skipping a beat. She ends the connection.
You don't go into specifics, but you explain to Dawn that you'd prefer not to. She takes it pretty well, and you exchange a few more pleasantries before ending the connection.

Dawn watches the planet unspool beneath the two of you, shoulder braced against the shuttle's bulkhead.

"Well, ain't that somethin'," she says, voice low. "Gilead. Built by the faithful, kept by 'em too. Looks like a wild garden someone bothered to tend- not too much, just enough. Ludd's hand in the dirt, sure as anything. Damn near pretty, if you don't look too close. Still. It's a sight."

Deorbiting to the surface of Gilead is overseen by the Knights of Ludd, and their traffic control is characteristically strict. Your shuttle queues behind a flotilla of lumbering transports and haulers.

The face of Gilead is filled with green continents, seeded with Earth-life by sporeship and climate-stabilized by Eridani Utopia's vast terraforming machines. Then Ludd came and the Faithful turned the world into a garden-wilderness of astounding richness; it is a gem of the Persean Sector.

Your shuttle receives a ping from traffic control: permission to descend.

At the landing pad, you are met by a solemn Luddic Knight. A Knight Initiate stands a few paces behind, trying to match the ease and calm of their superior.

"Captain $PlayerName," the Knight says, "Let me speak welcome on behalf of the Church of Galactic Redemption. I extend to you an invitation from the Reverend Archcurate Jaspis to meet and share refreshment."

He steps aside, gesturing toward the cluster of administrative buildings ahead. Then his gaze flicks to Dawn, standing at your side. A pause-brief, but not impolite-before he adds, "I must ask that you come alone. The Reverend Archcurate will only receive individuals." Dawn picks up on it right away. She lays a steady hand on your shoulder.

"Ain't no trouble," she says, voice low and easy. "I'll hang back at the shuttle, keep things warm. You go ahead and have your word with the Reverend."
options:
lpp_ending2:Walk with the Knight
lpp_endingKnightWhat:"What's this about?"
lpp_endingKnightAllowed:"Am I allowed to decline?"
lpp_endingKnightDecline:"I decline the invitation."

You would have thought the tea to be uncomfortably hot, but the man seems unfazed as he swishes it around his mouth and swallows.

"Been told you've been asking questions," he says with a cheap smile. A tooth is missing, another cracked to a point. He smells of mildew, and sweat.

"I'm Sedge," he says.

The muted conversations persist, followed by the abrupt eruption of gunshots and a distant yell. A commotion ensues, unseen but palpable, before a force jerks you to your feet. The hood shrouding your vision is yanked away.

"This part of your brilliant plan too?" Dawn's voice drips with sarcasm as she surveys you for injuries. Before you can formulate a response, a presence approaches-a shadow, a figure, a man. His hands are open and raised part-way.

"We should speak, face to face. You-I mean no harm."

text:
You make the agreed upon signal. Dawn doesn't say anything. She just steps forward. draws and fire. 

The shot reverberates in the small space, a sharp crack that rattles ceramicware.

Sedge drops like a bag of meat.

There are shouts and screams from patrons. Chaos, and flight. The room empties out, the proprietor ducks behind the bar.

She doesn't look at you afterward. Just at her sidearm, for a moment. "You know what stopped Loke?" Dawn asks.

"Andrada, of all people. Someone willing to be harder than him. Sector doesn't remember the people who prayed real hard that he'd stop. It remembers the one who made him stop."

text:
When you arrive, Dawn is leaning back in her seat with. Her head snaps from Jethro to you with an almost amused expression. 

Jethro Bornanew sits with his paper notebook open on the table, untouched.

He looks up when you enter.

"Captain. I apologize if I've caused any disruption. I was asking Dawn a question and she suggested I ask you directly."

"And, I said that you'd probably want to answer it yourself. He asked me what you believe in," she says. "Just like that."

Jethro looks between you and Dawn, apparently unbothered by being the subject of this.

"It seemed relevant," he says simply. "We're on an investigation. On a Luddic matter. I find it useful to know."
He looks at you directly. No pressure in it. Just the question, still open.

options: armaa_dawnKantaDenRespCynical:"I used to. Less certain than I was."

text:
"Good," he says. Simply, without flourish.

Dawn looks between you both, then back at her cup. "Well. That's that then. Clean and simple.," she says.

"What changed?"
Jethro asks, the way a doctor asks where it hurts.

"That's a long answer," you say.

"I have time," he says. Then, catching himself: "If you want to give it."

Dawn is watching you now rather than him. Something in her expression that isn't quite amusement anymore. "That one I can relate understand," she says to no one in particular. 

Jethro looks at her.  "Faith that's been tested is still faith," he says finally. "For what that's worth."

Jethro's expression doesn't change, as if this was something that was always obvious. "I see," he says.

Dawn's cup stops halfway to her mouth. She sets it down carefully.

Jethro looks at you with the particular attention of someone doing rapid, quiet arithmetic.

"I won't pretend that's uncomplicated," he says. "Given where we're going and what we're doing. But I've known people of the Path who were better than the Path they walked. And people of the Church who were worse than it."

He picks up his notebook, only to put it down again.

"At all?"

"At all.", you confirm.

"I hope you don't mind me asking," he says, "and I beg your pardon if I am prying too much - but - what do you hold onto? When things go badly."

He catches himself almost immediately. "You don't have to answer that."

Dawn is looking at her cup.
"Good question though."
options:
armaa_dawnKantaDenRespAtheisticPeople:"The people around me."
armaa_dawnKantaDenRespAtheisticMyself:"Myself. That's enough."
armaa_dawnKantaDenRespAtheisticIDK:"I'm not sure I hold onto anything."
armaa_dawnKantaDenRespAtheisticDeflect:"That's a longer answer than you're probably looking for." (deflect)

text:
Jethro considers this for a moment.

"It matters to me," he says. Not an argument - just a fact. "Not because I require you to believe what I believe. But because I find that knowing what someone holds dear tells me more about them than most other things."

"But I understand that's not always a question people want to answer." He picks up his notebook. "So. The mission."

Dawn is quiet for a second. "What? That's it?" she says.

"That's it," he says simply.
"That's not nothing," he says. "That's actually quite a lot."

Jethro considers this carefully.
"I think that takes more strength than people give it credit for," he says finally. "Carrying your own weight with nothing underneath."

He doesn't offer anything to fill the silence.
"Alright," he says finally. Gently.

"Probably," he agrees. "You don't have to give it."
He picks up his notebook again. This time he actually opens it.
"Thank you for answering at all," he says.

"Can I ask you something," Dawn says. "On Mazalot, you were tending to graves when the Captain found you."

"Yes," he says.

"Why?"

He thinks about it properly. "Because someone should," he says finally. "And I was there."

Dawn lets out a short breath, a combination of an exhale and a laugh."You sound just like my dad. He kept his hands clean, in a place that made that very difficult. Figured it was performance. Keepin' up appearances for the neighbors, y'know? But eventually I realized he meant it."

"What changed your mind?" Jethro asks.
notes: # this reads a little weird to me, maybe should reframe it  TODO

text:
"Kept running into people who claimed to walk the same walk," she says. "Y'know, keep their hands clean, live strictly to the tenets," she glances at one of the bottles decorating her stateroom, "But were all too willing to partake in joyful wickedness. I'll confess it took me a while to figure out who was who."

Jethro is quiet for a moment.
"I was in prison," he says. "When Archcurate Jaspis found me." He says it the way he says most things - plainly, without asking for a reaction. "I had done things I am not proud of. She took me in anyway."

He closes his notebook.

"I think that's what faith looks like too," he says. "Someone looking at what you've done and not looking away. And then you deciding what to do next. Every day."

Dawn's expression shifts to something you can't quite label. "...Yeah," she says. "That's a better way to put it than I've managed." She looks at him, then picks up the bottle.
"Jethro. Do you drink?"

"Bring your friend," a mischievous look grows on his face. "I have an object in my special collection," his eyebrows bounce, "that'll launch his tabard directly into orbit! You'll see, you'll see. Oh, this is so perfect."

text:
After leaving the shrine, your $shipOrFleet drifts into the quiet hum of deep space. Dawn stays by the viewport longer than usual.

"Alright. That's the last stop. I kept puttin' this off. Always said I'd go when things slowed down. Funny joke, right."

She rubs a thumb along the edge of her glove.

"I didn't come lookin' for a groundshaking revelation. But.. I dunno. Wanted to see if it felt like somethin'. If I still fit in it."

She glances back at you.

"Mostly it was dust and old wax and places folks stopped carin' for."

"So tell me straight. When you stand in those places and don't feel like you belong… what's that mean?"

"Am I doin' somethin' wrong? Or is this just what a Luddic looks like when they don't get to live clean?"

options: armaa_dawnCoFRespA:"Could be the shrines just didn't mean much to you in the first place."
armaa_dawnCoFRespB:"I don't really know what that's supposed to feel like honestly."
armaa_dawnCoFRespC:"Most things don't feel different right away. Even when they matter."
armaa_dawnCoFRespJoke:"If the standard is 'don't crash a tanker into a station,' you're already ahead of a lot of believers."
armaa_dawnCoFRespLuddic:"Faith doesn't always come with a feeling. Sometimes it's just something you keep doing."
armaa_dawnCoFRespPather:"You feel nothing because the Church has drifted. You walked a habit, not the Path."

text:
She frowns, thinking.
"I guess I'm askin' if I messed it up somehow. Or if that's just how it is."

"Like… was I supposed to feel somethin'? Or is this just me expectin' too much outta quiet places?"

She exhales slowly. "Yeah. That's kinda what I'm stuck on."

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt... I dunno. Lighter." She frowns.

"Like maybe if I was doin' this right - livin' right - it would've meant more."

She exhales slowly. "Yeah. That's kinda what I'm stuck on."

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt... I dunno. Lighter." She frowns.

"Like maybe if I was doin' this right - livin' right - it would've meant more."

She exhales slowly. "Yeah. That's kinda what I'm stuck on."

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt... I dunno. Lighter." She frowns.

"Like maybe if I was doin' this right - livin' right - it would've meant more."

She shoots you a look. "Don't you go lumping us in with that bunch! Most faithful folk I grew up with couldn't hurt a fly if it asked nice." Her expression sobers a notch.

"Pathers ain't the faith. They're what happens when fear gets a hold of somebody and they start swingin' the Book like it's a club." She looks back toward the viewport, quieter.

"I know you're jokin'. Just... that part matters to me. Still. I appreciate the reminder I ain't gotta clear that bar."

"Yeah. That sounds about right."

"I keep wantin' it to be… I dunno. A moment. Like I walk out and I'm better somehow."

She looks back out at the stars.

"But most of my life hasn't worked that way. You just do the thing again tomorrow."

After a pause, she adds, quieter,

"Still feels strange, though. Standin' there and not feelin' a damn thing."

Dawn's expression tightens. "That's a hell of a thing to say." She keeps her eyes on the viewport for a moment. "I ain't lookin' to pick a fight with the Church," she says, then pauses. "But…"

Her mouth twists.

"But it did feel… rehearsed. Like I was doin' motions somebody else handed me." She glances back at you.

"And that scares me more than the silence. 'Cause if it's just habit… then what've I actually been keepin' all these years?"

options: armaa_dawnCoFResp2A:"You went. Nothing happened. Sounds like the shrines are nothing special."
armaa_dawnCoFResp2B:"If it mattered, you'll see it in what you do next-not in what you felt in the moment."
armaa_dawnCoFResp2C:"Most things don't feel different right away. Even when they matter."
armaa_dawnCoFRespLuddic2:"If you turn this into a test you can fail, it'll eat you alive. Don't do that to yourself."
armaa_dawnCoFRespPather2:"Then keep what feels true and drop what feels like performance."

text:
"…Yeah. Could be." Her jaw works, just a little. "Guess I was hopin' I'd walk out and feel less... tangled up about it."

She looks back toward the viewport, voice flatter now. "Forget I asked. Wasn't tryin' to make it your problem."

Then she shifts the helmet under her arm and starts for the door. "Come on. You got a $shipOrFleet to run."

"That's... annoyingly practical."

She says it like an insult, but there's no hostility behind it.

"Feels like you're tellin' me I don't get to hide behind it."

She looks away. "Alright. Fair."

Dawn huffs a quiet laugh. She rolls her shoulders like she can shake the thought loose.

"I guess I wanted it to be immediate. Like... do the thing, feel the thing. Life don't work like that. Shoulda remembered."

Dawn goes still for a moment, like you hit closer than you meant to.

"…Yeah. I do that. With a lotta things."

She snorts, but it's not amused.

"I'll try not to. Can't promise I'll be perfect about it."

Her eyes narrow, not angry-wary.

"Easy thing to say. But… yeah. I get what you mean."

She exhales through her nose.

"There was parts of it that felt real. Quiet, but real."

"And parts that..felt like some kinda performance."

Dawn shifts her grip on the helmet and clears her throat.

"Alright," she says, like she's saying it to herself as much as you.

"Ain't gonna get anywhere starin' at the dark."

She nods toward the corridor.

"Come on. We got work. I'll… keep it in the back of my head."

She exhales slowly.
"Yeah. That's kinda what I'm stuck on."

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt… I dunno. Lighter."

She frowns.
"Like maybe if I was doin' this right — livin' right — it would've meant more."

She exhales slowly.

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt… I dunno. Lighter."

She frowns.
"Like maybe if I was doin' this right — livin' right — it would've meant more."

text:
She exhales slowly.
"Yeah. That's kinda what I'm stuck on."

"Because part of me keeps thinkin' I should've felt… I dunno. Lighter."

She frowns.
"Like maybe if I was doin' this right — livin' right — it would've meant more."

"Mazalot." She says it like a verdict. "Terraforming worked, which is about the only thing that did. Luddics here get treated like second-class citizens and the League won't touch it - non-intervention policy. Members handle their own business."

She shrugs, but there's an edge under it.

"Which sounds principled until you're the one bein' handled."
options:
armaa_dawnMazalotRespA:Sounds like Mazalot's got its fair share of challenges.
armaa_dawnMazalotRespB:The League should be about freedom for everyone, not just the privileged few.
armaa_dawnMazalotRespC:Typical League passivity, huh? That's nothing new.
armaa_dawnMazalotRespD:If the League won't act, maybe it's time someone did. The Luddics in Mazalot deserve justice.

text: "Challenges are part of the game, ain't they? Still. It's a shame. A damn shame."
"Yeah. Funny how that works. The people preachin' loudest about freedom always seem to mean freedom for the right sort."

"League couldn't care less as long as the dues come in. That's the whole of it." She frowns. 

"Ludd, how depressing. Where's my bottle..."

"Justice." She repeats. "Yeah. They deserve it."

"Just make sure you know what you're walkin' into before you start deliverin' it. Mazalot's had a lot of people come through with good intentions."

"Well now... that's one of the Cryosleepers, isn't it?" Her voice drops a notch, eyes fixed on the distant shape drifting in the void. "Still clingin' to life, all this time. Makes you think. Hard to say what's mercy anymore. Wake 'em up, and you might hand 'em a second chance. Orrr drop 'em straight into the mess we've made of things."

"Sometimes I wonder if it's kinder to let 'em keep dreaming. Least in there, they don't see what's become of the rest of us."
options:
armaa_dawnCryosleeperRespA:"Hard to say what's the right call here."
armaa_dawnCryosleeperRespB:"Waking them up could bring fresh minds, new ideas."
armaa_dawnCryosleeperRespD:"We can't afford to wait for some ideal moment. If they can't handle the state of things, well.."

text:
"Yeah. No clean answers here." She glances at the sensors. "Just gotta decide and live with it."

"Not like it has to be our call. Could leave 'em be. Assumin' there's anyone left to leave 'em to."

"Could be they've got somethin' useful in their heads. Old knowledge, different ways of thinkin'." She shrugs. 

"Or they wake up and can't make sense of anything around 'em. Hard to know how far back they're from."
Dawn agrees with a thoughtful expression, "Adaptation might be a struggle. We can't predict how they'll cope with a world that's evolved without 'em."
"Runnin' in blind's a good way to trade one kind of trouble for another. For all we know these folks came from a whole different world than the one we're standin' in."

The briefing room. A holographic star map rotates slowly above the table. The robed man, the boy, and the robot are gathered around it - routes to Volturn, contingencies, the usual shape of a plan that hasn't met reality yet.
The door slides open.

Dawn steps into the room and stops when she sees the machine.
"What's that doing here."

The boy straightens immediately.
"He's with us."

Dawn looks at the boy. Then back at the machine.

options: armaa_dawn_anhEventRespA:"It's a companion droid. Been useful so far."
armaa_dawn_anhEventRespB:"We've got a route to plan. Save the theology for later."
armaa_dawn_anhEventRespC:"Fair question. You want to ask it yourself?"

text:
Dawn doesn't flinch, one brow arching. The old man speaks before she can push further. 

"Helps with the heavy lifting," he says, with the ease of someone who has deflected this question before.

"Travelers with a porter droid." Dawn's eyes stay on the machine. "Sure."

The old man's smile doesn't waver. "Best not to ask too many questions. The Sector has a way of leading folk down strange roads."

Dawn looks at him for a moment. "Right." She turns and slips out without another word.

The boy watches her go. The old man doesn't.

"I told you something was off about that damn machine!" Dawn grimaces.

"Now there's a man I have not seen in a long, long time," he says quietly. "By now, even I was starting to think he might be dead." He looks to you now, a glimmer of that younger self appearing in his face. "Give me over to them, captain. The boy must go to Volturn without me. He will be safe if they have me."

The kid is aghast. "No!" he shouts, "They'll kill you!"
options:

anh_diktatPatrolStall3Yes:"As you wish."
anh_diktatPatrolStall3All:"No, this ends here. I'm handing you all over."

text:
"What!?" the kid shouts, "You can't do that. You have to fight! You have a $shipOrFleet! You're just going to let those Diktat goons order you around?" He lunges at you, and is bodily intercepted by the arm of one of your guards. After a yelp and a moment of struggle, the kid folds to an electrostun rod.

The old man does not give the commotion even a glance. Instead, his eyes are steady on yours. Looking deeply for something.

"He has so much to learn, $playerName." He looks thoughtful, "Fate may surprise us yet."

"What!?" the kid shouts, "You can't do that! You're just-" he lunges at you, and is bodily intercepted by the arm of one of your guards. After a yelp and a moment of struggle, the kid folds to an electrostun rod.

The old man does not give the commotion even a glance. Instead, his eyes are steady on yours. Looking deeply for something.

"So this is how you choose, $playerName." He looks thoughtful, "Fate may surprise us yet."

Dawn approaches you, as you go about the duties necessary to run a fleet.

"Captain, I've been through a fair share of scrapes in my time, but this... this was somethin' else entirely. The Knights of Ludd swoopin' in like that, tryin' to take over... It just don't sit right with me."

She pauses, "Would've liked to reason with 'em. But they were adamant. Had no choice but to stand with you when it came to blows."

There's a flicker of sadness in her eyes as she continues. "But now that it's done, and we've secured our colony's freedom, it's a weight off my shoulders, it is. No one should be forced to live under the thumb of the Church, not when they've got a chance at somethin' better."

Dawn's shoulders relax slightly as she lets out a small sigh. "You did what we had to do, Captain. And now, we can look forward, knowing we've made things right for the people."

text:

"Couldn't have afforded it anyway. And my folks would've had something to say about it - 'lies of Moloch,' that whole business." 

"Funny thing is, I don't know if they were wrong. About the dangerous part, anyway."

armaa_dawnGalatiaRespB:"It's a shame you couldn't attend."

text:
"Regret? Nah. Well, sometimes."

"Mostly I wonder what I would've been like. Different problems, probably. Different regrets."

"Depends what you're diggin' for, I think."

"My folks weren't entirely wrong. Some things you learn and can't put back down."

"Didn't love it. Still dont." She snorts. 

"Couldn't decide if it was worse than the Academy or just a different kind of wrong. At least I wasn't sittin' in a classroom full of Moloch's lies. That part was probably a comfort to 'em."
"Sure." Flat, not quite agreeing. "Learned plenty. Not sure all of it took."

She says nothing for a moment.

"Maybe." She shrugs once. "Might've hated it anyway."

Dawn's voice cuts into comms unprompted, easy and unbothered.

text:
"Honest," she says simply. "Place doesn't pretend to be something it's not. You know what you're walking into, you know what people want from you, nobody's got the energy to lie about it."

"There's something restful about that, actually. We got the time, mind hittin' port?"

"It's not for everyone."

"But? if you ever end up docked there - find the place with the yellow sign called Sticky Pete's. Don't ask what's in the food. Just eat it."

"Yeah," she agrees, completely unbothered. "It really is."

"Still like it though."

"Oh, stow it. ...maybe later."

"But it's a good story."

"This world was meant to be a luxury retreat back in the old cycles - agriculture, biotech, leisure. A little paradise for the brass up on Opis. Then came the Crisis."

"Diktat's got their grip on it now. Keepin' order - or their version of it. But anywhere they ain't lookin'? Smugglers, warlords, black marketeers, resistance cells... hell, even lobster runners."

She shakes her head. "Always someone carvin' out a piece."
options:
armaa_dawnVolturnRespA:"Sounds like Volturn's got its fair share of challenges."
armaa_dawnVolturnRespB:"Any particular groups making waves down there?"
armaa_dawnVolturnRespC:"What about the Luddic sects? Still holding on despite Diktat's crackdown?"
armaa_dawnVolturnRespD:"Any hope for things to improve, or is it a lost cause?"

text: "Yeah. That's one word for it." She shrugs. "People make do. Always do."
"Half a dozen at least, probably more I don't know about. Nobody's in charge of anything for long down there."
"Half a dozen factions scrapping over the scraps. I'd bet that old man and his tin can are tangled up in one of 'em somehow."
"Diktat doesn't make it easy. But the faithful have a way of findin' each other. Always have."

"Hard to say. Place has been ground down a long time." She glances out toward Volturn. 

"But it's still there. That counts for somethin', I guess."
