text:
"I... I ain't much for speeches, Captain, but-" She swallows, eyes fixed on some point far past you. "That wasn't man-made. Call it a demon if you want."

 She breathes deep, steadying herself. "That was an abomination made real. Ludd help me, I'm glad we put it down."

Ops clears his throat, fingers tapping a little too hard against his console. "It *could* have been a hyperspace anomaly. Emergent AI construct, maybe. Some residual Domain system gone strange. We've seen plenty of oddities. Nothing's supernatural out here- just feels that way when we don't have the data." Dawn doesn't look at him. She's staring past the viewscreen, jaw tight.

"Wrap it up in science if it helps you sleep." She shakes her head, voice dropping. 

"I don't claim to understand what we saw. But I know how it felt. That wasn't natural. That was something that don't belong." Ops hesitates, glancing toward you. "Whatever it was, it's gone. We survived. That's what matters."

options: armaa_dawn_dwellerEventRespA:"No doubt. That was evil, plain and real. Just like the Book warned."
armaa_dawn_dwellerEventRespB:"So. We fought biblical space demons and won. What now? Start calling the $shipOrFleet the Chosen?"
armaa_dawn_dwellerEventRespC:"I don't believe in monsters- only in things we don't yet understand. All that matters is we can destroy them."

text: The Sensor Officer is the first to speak. She doesn't turn from her screen, eyes fixed on data that's still shifting like it's unsure what shape it wants to be. "Then that's what we call it," she says. "We log it that way. But I still don't like it."

script:

text:
The cargo bay stinks of scorched metal and spent coolant. Ozone hangs thick in the air, clinging to bulkheads where the fusion cutters bit deep. The recyclers hiss under strain, their labor unnoticed by the crew clustered nearby. It dominates the bay. Longer than a cutter shuttle, broader than a standard torpedo casing, inert and silent. 

A half-dozen salvors linger at a distance, postures caught somewhere between awe and apprehension. One shifts uneasily against a crowbar, another pulls a sharp drag from a synth-wrap, the ember flaring like a pulse monitor in overdrive. Low voices pass back and forth.

"Ops confirmed it? ...yeah, Domain-grade extermination package. Ludd forsaken genocide stockpile. We shouldn't be this close." 

"Relax. It's safed. Cold as a grave."

The hatch hisses. Dawn steps through, boots clicking once against the grated deck alongside another crewmember - their expression suggests they'd rather be anywhere else. Her voice is low, mostly for her companion. "You didn't say it was one of these."

She stops short of the thing itself. The flickering hazard strobes catch across her features as she studies it. Shadows move in time with the warning lights, stretching long across the bay.
options:
armaa_dawnPKRespA:"It's safe. No code, no detonation."
armaa_dawnPKRespB:"You know about these?"
armaa_dawnPKRespC:"I thought they were myths."
armaa_dawnPKRespD:Stay silent

text: "So's a virus, in a sealed lab. Don't mean it can't become a plague."
"Enough to know we shouldn't have it."

"Most myths don't come with serial numbers." Her gaze lingers on the hazard striping, the faint Domain etchings barely visible beneath layers of scorched alloy.
"They're real. And once word gets out we pulled one up, you can bet plenty of people will come to make sure it stops being our problem."
"Thing like this... don't gotta go off to do damage. Just sittin' there's enough to make the whole sector a little worse."
options:
armaa_dawnPKRespA2:"What, are you scared?"
armaa_dawnPKRespB2:"It's still just metal."
armaa_dawnPKRespC2:"I can use this as leverage."
armaa_dawnPKRespD2:"This thing shouldn't exist. It's a remnant of mankind's arrogance-proof of what Ludd warned us about."

text: She chuckles, a dry rasp. "No. I'm afraid of people. This? It just waits. People? People can wake it."
She snorts. "So's a knife, or a guillotine. Intent's what makes a tool an instrument of sin."

She goes quiet. "Yeah. You could."

She stops and looks at you flat.

"But soon as you start thinkin' like that, you ain't holdin' leverage. You're holdin' a live wire and hopin' nobody notices you’re shakin'."

She jerks her chin toward the thing.

"You think they'll negotiate? Maybe. But the second they believe you might use it - or that somebody might take it off your hands - you stop bein' a problem. You become a priority."

She turns to look at you-really look. "Well. There it is." She taps the warhead's casing gently with the back of her hand. "Right here. Our Tower of Babel. You ain't wrong. This, this is what the old books meant when they talked about the kind of foolishness that makes man think he gets to decide whether a whole world lives or dies."

"But Ludd didn't preach fear. He preached choice, repentance. Turnin' away from power like this, even when it's right there in your hand."

"There's no neutral ground when you're ridin' shotgun with somethin' that could wipe a world clean." She breathes out slowly.

"So I won't tell you to blow it, sell it, or sanctify it. I ain't Ludd, and I sure as hell ain't your conscience. But I will say this - whatever you choose to do with that thing... there may be reverberations. And when - if there are - you better be able to live with it."

options: pk_turnInSeraph1:"I'd like to discuss planetkiller weapons."

text:
$personFirstName gives you a blank look.

"What about them? Is this about Old Meshan?"
options:
pk_turnInSeraph2:"No, I mean I want to sell you a planetkiller."
pk_turnInSeraph1meshan:"Old Meshan?"

text:
"Meshan remains one of the less fortunate events in recent cycles. The investigation attributed responsibility to a rogue command element operating without strategic authorization." Her voice is level, almost detached.

 "There are ..rumors," she starts, "That Tri-Tachyon was involved. However, Tri-Tachyon maintains it had no sanctioned operational presence in-system at the time. Speculation to the contrary has been addressed through the appropriate legal frameworks." 
 
 "The aftermath proved... informative. In how quickly certain narratives form - and how readily some abandon accountability when the cost becomes politically unviable."

A smile arrives before the warmth does. "The matter is closed. Sector authorities are satisfied. For most, that suffices."
options: pk_turnInSeraph2:"No, I mean I want to sell you a planetkiller."

text:
"There are quite a few different kinds of PKs, you see. This one didn't destroy the planet outright. It just wiped out the largest population center and destabilized the biosphere."

"Tri-Tachyon ship deployed it, but can't say for sure if it was under any official order. Some wayward CEO who woke up on the wrong side of the bed that shift, perhaps." A smile arrives, but there is nothing amused about it. "Won't find any of that on record, though. Purged most of the logs inside a cycle. But I remember."

"By the way," her tone lifts slightly, "I'm only telling you this because we understand one another. Officially? I don't know anything."

"So. Does that answer your question? Or just give you more to lose sleep over?"

"I suppose what I said leaves room for interpretation." She adjusts the cuff of her jacket.

"Records from that cycle are incomplete. In some cases, deliberately so. But certain systems endure - in one form or another." 

"Let's just say I've been with the company a long time. Long enough to remember what most would prefer remain forgotten."

"Continuity has its value, Captain. Especially in a Sector that burns through people and assets at the rate this one does." 

"And as for how I'm still here - well."
options: pk_turnInSeraph2:"Anyway, I wanted to sell you a planetkiller."

text:
$personFirstName taps something just out of frame. A low hum answers as the privacy field activates; the office view blurs, ambient sound fading into silence. "Assuming this isn't some joke... I won't ask how you came by something like that, Captain." Her tone is polite, professional. 

"But I'm not buying. Even if I trusted myself to bury it - and I do - the kind of attention that follows hardware like that? It's Not worth it, for me or the company. Sooner or later, someone serious comes looking. And when they do, there's only one direction that sort of trouble flows." 

"And what would I even do with it? Level Culann? Mass murder tends to play poorly in the quarterly reports." She produces a laugh. "Kidding, of course." 

"Listen, Captain. I've been in this business a long time. Long enough to know when something's too hot to hold. Get rid of it. Quietly."

"Good. We never talked about this. Alright?" She smiles warmly, waving a hand as the privacy field disappears.

"Now then. Anything else?"

The warmth in her expression settles into something flatter. She exhales through her nose.

"$player.firstName, $player.firstName...Let's say you pass it off to someone sensible. They lock it away, throw away the key. Unlikely - but for argument's sake - fine." 

"Now let's say it ends up in... someone else's hands." 

"Maybe they use it. Maybe they don't. But whoever gets it, they'll think about it. You don't hold power like that and not start imagining where it fits."
She lets that sit for a second longer than is comfortable. "At best? It's leverage. At worst-" She doesn't finish. She doesn't have to.

"If it were me, I'd put it on a freighter set to dead drop into a black hole. Clean, quiet." Sera waves a hand. The privacy field collapses with a soft hiss, the ambient sounds of her office returning. "But that's your call, not mine. I've said my piece."

"Now then. Anything else?"
"Good. Arma Armatura will handle it from here. You'll be compensated and you won't have to think about it again." A slight smile. "That's the better outcome for everyone."
"Wise decision, Captain $player.lastName." She's already moving on, like the outcome was never really in question.

options: armaa_dataCollection:"I've heard there are opportunities for gathering data for the corp."

text:
"ATAC." She lets it sit for a second.

"Aegis Tactical Assessment Corps. Arma Armatura's field validation program - pilots, analysts, captains embedded directly with prototype deployments. Live systems, real conditions."

"We're currently focused on the Series-II Cataphract line. Strong performance curves. Endurance is still being refined. Which is where the right operator becomes useful."

"Join, and you're flying hardware that isn't on the market yet. High priority assignments. Direct input into development."

"Or stay on your existing terms. No penalty."
A faint smile. "I find the bold ones tend to get more interesting work. But that's your call."

"Your telemetry folds directly into development. In exchange you're always flying ahead of the market. Quiet influence - nothing as crude as a title. But real."

"You help shape what comes next. We make sure you're never behind what already exists."

options: armaa_dataCollectionExpectations:"So will I report to you?"

text:
"You report to the system. I step in when it matters. ATAC runs on autonomy - that's the point. You're not a cog. You're a vector."

"The more value you deliver, the more we invest. It compounds. Those arrangements tend to be the most interesting."

options: armaa_dataCollectionGoal:"What do you plan to do with all this information?"

text:
"Arma Armatura doesn't just build war machines. We refine the interface between pilot and platform. ATAC is where that work happens in live conditions. Systems close to deployment but not there yet. Real stress, real data."

"The objective is simple: assets that punch well above their tonnage. Agility and lethality routed through a single mind. No doctrine lag. No hesitation gap."

"Why build bigger when you can build smarter? Fewer platforms. Greater returns. And yes - considerable profit."

"Simulations lack context."
"Code obeys, you see. But pilots improvise. We can run ten thousand simulated skirmishes. None of them will show us what a human does when the stakes are real. That data doesn't come from labs."

"It comes from pressure. And pressure only exists when failure has consequences."

"Because you're capable-and more importantly, adaptable. We've seen your work. So has everyone else. You operate outside traditional doctrine. You're unpredictable. And you survive."

Her eyes hold yours for just a second longer than feels comfortable.

"Excellent." She taps a command on the interface. "Your assignment file is already prepared."

"Welcome to ATAC, Captain. Don't make me regret the confidence."

Your comms officer forwards the link to your personal interface. The channel opens with a flicker, stabilizing on the image of a composed station official seated behind a minimalist desk.

"Good shift, Captain.." The portmaster glances at a secondary display. "$playerLastName." There's a brief pause the official's tone remains professional, but they look slightly more uncertain now.

"This is a bit sudden, but, ah.. Sera Pha, board member of Arma Armatura, has requested a meeting at her convenience. A transport will be dispatched to your berth upon arrival."
options:
armaa_marketPostSleeperStart2:"Did she say why?"
armaa_marketPostSleeperStart2b:"Who is that?"

text:
The portmaster's eyes flick briefly to their monitor before answering.

"Not... specifically, Captain."

"But if I may- most arrivals don't receive direct requests from Administrator Pha. I assume the matter is significant."

The screen returns to standby.

"Lady Pha serves on the Royal Advisory Council. Board-appointed, representing... certain corporate interests."

"While New Meshan maintains full sovereign governance under the Crown, it is not uncommon for the Council to take a direct interest in matters of external arrivals. Particularly those of potential... strategic relevance."

"Enjoy your stay, $PlayerSirOrMadam." The official offers the faintest of professional smiles, then cuts the link.

"Excellent. A priority docking corridor has been authorized for your approach. The Administrator appreciates your timely cooperation. Good day to you, $PlayerSirOrMadam"

She offers another polite smile.

text:
Your shuttle descends through the upper reaches of New Meshan's thin, dust-laden atmosphere. Red-gold haze streaks past the viewport, the dry air shimmering from the heat of re-entry.

The flight path diverts from the general approach lanes without comment, bypassing the standard commercial queues managed by the Port Authority. No tolls or transaction notices accompany the shift- just a quiet override ping acknowledging priority clearance.

Far below, the clustered spires of New Meshan's primary arcology rise from the desert flats, surrounded by wind-battered defensive walls and sprawling industrial platforms. The sun catches polished alloy and glass in hard flashes, broken by swirling eddies of airborne grit.

You pass over what your triPad identifies as the Royal Palace - a squat, angular fortress complex nested within high retaining walls of pale stone and composite plating. Ceremonial banners ripple in the dry crosswinds, briefly visible before slipping from view.

Ahead, the Isenhall Spire grows larger - an austere tower of black alloy and glass rising alone on its platform, distinct from the dense city mass below.

The interior is subdued, almost spartan. Polished stone and composite panels, narrow windows letting in hard shafts of desert light. Subtle banners bearing the seal of New Meshan hang at intervals, their muted colors shifting in the filtered air.

At the reception desk, a familiar voice.

"Welcome, Captain." The same composed tone you heard when you raised Sera's comms. Plain headscarf, modest uniform, expression calm and practiced. 

"Administrator Pha is expecting you. Please proceed when ready."

The office is clean. Smooth composite panels in muted grays and off-whites, narrow vertical lighting strips recessed into the walls. The seams of modular plating are visible if you know where to look - the honest geometry of prefabrication, assembled quickly but not carelessly. 

Through tall reinforced windows, the desert stretches into hazy distance, pale ridgelines under the glare of New Meshan's sun. Beyond them, distant arcologies rise like pale spires, their silhouettes broken by high-tension cables and atmospheric processors.

At the far end, a simple composite desk. Unadorned, functional, fixed to the floor plating as though permanent.

The woman behind it looks up as you approach and lifts one hand, palm open.

"Captain $player.lastName, is it? I appreciate your prompt arrival. I imagine you've had... a complicated reentry into the Sector." She inclines her head slightly, a gesture somewhere between courtesy and evaluation.

"You've brought with you a very old machine. One with a history that intersects - rather directly - with certain long-standing interests that I am tasked with managing."

"But before we discuss specifics, allow me to extend the formal welcome of New Meshan- and my thanks for honoring the invitation."
options:
armaa_firstVisitIntro5:"You seem rather well-informed."
armaa_firstVisitIntro5b:"Let's skip to the part where you tell me exactly why I'm here".

text:
Sera allows the faintest hint of a smile. "I make it my business to stay informed, Captain."

"The reappearance of certain war-era assets tends to draw attention, you know. Particularly in this system."

Her tone remains even, without accusation. "New Meshan is not so far from its past that we can afford to ignore such arrivals."

"Records suggest that you are what we would classify as a Sleeper, Captain. An old employee of ours, actually." She pauses, studying your reaction carefully. "If you don't mind my asking: how long ago did you first enter cryosleep?"
options: armaa_firstVisitIntro6:"I went under after the war."

AdjustRepPerson armaa_seraph FRIENDLY 10

AdjustRep independent FRIENDLY 15
text:
She nods slowly. Confirming something she already suspected.

"That places your entry point at the tail end of the AI War's collapse in this system. Few entered suspension that late and lived to see their cycle complete."

Her gaze holds yours.

"Re-emerging with equipment of that era - and that classification - is not something that typically goes unnoticed. Nor without interest."

"But I have no intention of threatening you. Quite the opposite."

"The Coalition controls most of Exsedol's lower districts." She said it flatly, like she was deciding how much to give you. "Meshan survivors. Or people who claim to carry on their will."

What do they want, you ask.

"What do you think?" She replies. 

"The same thing angry people always want. Someone to answer for it. Interesting timing," she says, "for someone like you to show up."

"You see, Captain, your situation is uniquely valuable."

"The Valkazard- a fully functional Cataphract from the First AI War- is more than just a machine. It's a symbol. And you- one of Meshan's own, carrying both the hardware and the history - are a symbol as well."

She lets that thought hang for a moment, studying your expression.

"The leadership of the Reprisal Coalition - they are many things. But above all: desperate for legitimacy."

"Someone like you? They may see you as more than simply a survivor. They may see you as something worth rallying behind. And influence, Captain... influence is a very useful thing to have."

"I won't pretend I don't have an interest, Captain." Her voice is calm, measured. "The Coalition is unstable. Some dream of igniting a war, others build little empires on Exsedol." 

"What they lack is a center. A unifying figure who can turn that into something useful- for them, for this system... for you." She holds your eyes now. "You, Captain. A Sleeper, tied directly to Meshan. Carrying the Valkazard- the very image they romanticize."

"With you at the helm, the Coalition might become something more than a grudge. And you would find yourself in a rather advantageous position. What you do with that - that would be your decision."
options: armaa_firstVisitIntro10:"And how am I supposed to pull this off?"

text:
"You give yourself too little credit, Captain. The groundwork is already laid."

She gestures to a control panel beside her desk. A projection hums softly to life between you - crisp, stabilized footage. High altitude, long-range - suspiciously well-positioned.

It shows you - the Valkazard - cutting through the void, engines flaring, exchanging fire with the advancing Tri-Tachyon strike fleet. Every movement cleanly framed, the sort of feed that feels less like security footage and more like carefully curated documentation.

"A relic from Meshan's fall, piloting a Cataphract from the AI Wars, single-handedly repelling a Tri-Tachyon strike team sent into restricted space."

Her voice drops just slightly, almost amused. "You've already written the opening chapter, Captain. I've simply made sure the right people are starting to read it."
options: armaa_firstVisitIntro11:"I really don't think it'll be that simple"

text:
"Of course not," she says smoothly. "Nothing worth doing ever is."

"The old guard won't follow you because you tell them to, Captain. They'll follow because they see what you represent. The Reprisal Coalition was born from loss - and they've been chasing ghosts ever since." Her expression hardens just enough to cut through her earlier warmth.

"You are a piece of that past, returned. For them, you are proof that their struggle still means something. That the story didn't end."

"And if a few stubborn voices need more convincing-"
Her smile returns, smaller now.
"-well, you strike me as someone who knows how to handle complications. "

"Oh, and captain? I've taken the liberty of scanning the Valkazard. It is our property, after all. I may have something good to show you for it."

* * *

The shuttle hums softly beneath you as it lifts from the Isenhall Spire's platform, angling back toward the port. You lean back against the seat as the engines shift pitch, letting your eyes fall half-closed. The conversation replays itself, line by line.

"You've already written the opening chapter, Captain."
"I've simply made sure the right people are starting to read it."

It sounds so simple, when Sera Pha says it. Almost too simple.

The Coalition - fractured, angry, dangerous - and yet she expects you to step into its center. To carry both its history and its ambition on your back. To be useful.

The more you turn it over, the more you wonder:
How much of this is about stabilizing Exsedol?
How much is about protecting New Meshan?
And how much is simply about giving Arma Armatura another lever to pull in a system that's never fully recovered?

Your gut says she's telling the truth - at least most of it. She needs the Coalition contained, but not broken. And you? You're an opportunity. An asset she's already positioned, whether you knew it or not.

The shuttle banks gently as the landing bay comes into view. Docking clamps rise from the deck.

She stepped closer, her gaze narrowing as though scrutinizing the very notion of it. "Captain $playerName," her voice held a sharp edge, "how exactly did you come into possession of this? And where?"

A tense pause followed as her mind seemed to race. "We'll need to verify this immediately. If what you say is true... well, we'll be reclaiming this craft for inspection. This shouldn't even be here."

The comms closes before you can respond.

Your second in command catches you before you've gone far. The team is already aboard, setting up in the designated bay.

text:
Through the viewport, the FOB comes into view- a cluster of prefabs locked into tight defensive formations, steel plates gleaming against the vast sweep of green below. New construction, clearly - it's footprint barely registers against the unbroken sprawl of wilderness surrounding it. A perimeter wall rings the site, studded with ship-rated autocannons and makeshift checkpoints. 

Your comm crackles to life as you make final approach. "Got a few associates waiting on you," Kade's voice filters through, sounding-if only faintly-more cordial than usual. The hatch cycles open with a hiss. Warm, damp air presses in at once, heavy with the scent of wet earth and distant rainfall. You step onto uneven ground, following the winding paths deeper into the colony's growing sprawl.

Inside, Kade is with two figures: Imelda and someone wearing a light exoskeleton. Imelda acknowledges you with a small, respectful nod, her expression as composed and serene as ever.  Despite her calm demeanor, her gaze is sharp, carrying the quiet resolve you'd expect from a zealot.

"Commander," she says, her voice smooth and formal.

Kade grunts. Instead, he gestures to the person beside him. "And this here's Deadeye," he says. "Best mercenary I could dig up. Does what needs doin', no questions asked."

Deadeye turns to face you. Their voice, low and mechanical, carries an almost begrudging respect."Heard a lot about you, $playerSirOrMadam."
options:

text:
"I like you already. Just don't get too close, or I might get the wrong idea."

Imelda rolls her eyes.
Deadeye chuckles. "Not quite, but if you keep that up, I'll be happy to make sure you get a proper introduction."

"That's why my memoirs will be a hit. Who doesn't love a motley crew?" Deadeye quips, arms spread in a showman's flourish. "Of course, y'all will get a cut too, but my share better reflect my star power."

Imelda stares at him like one would stare at a rodent that smuggled itself into ones home. "What are you even talking about."

Kade leans against the wall, a faint smile tugging at his lips. "You're jumpin' the gun there, Deadeye. Let's focus on gettin' through this first, yeah?"

Kade clears his throat. "Moving on. Got heavy weapons and supplies ready for the colony, but as you'd imagine, some of the locals aren't happy about us being here. They're lookin' for a reason to test us. A show of force will make it clear we're not to be messed with, so a few strikes in the right places should keep the peace. So I was thinkin'.. since we're doin' this anyway, why not get some extra loot?"

text:
The situation on the surface has grown clearer, and more complicated. The colony's footprint hasn't gone unnoticed. Sovereignty patrols stalk the nearby jungles and ridgelines, their outposts scattered uncomfortably close to Kade's perimeter. Free Cities forces maintain their own positions, fortified around key supply nodes. Neither side trusts the other- but their deployments weren't built with an outside strike in mind. You won't slip through completely unseen,  but their security web isn't military-grade; gaps exist, if you're precise.

The plan calls for sharp, surgical hits on their supply depots. First priority will be the outer comm relays and anti-air positions. If it holds, you'll be in and out before they grasp what's been taken.

If it holds.

Your $shipOrFleet weaves a net of electronic interference, masking the insertion teams below. Precision strikes tear open brief corridors through enemy response grids, allowing raiding shuttles and strike squads to slip into the chaos. Amid the controlled disorder, success reports filter in. Multiple teams reach their targets, securing high-value stockpiles. The full tally will have to wait. No time to sort details in the field.

As quickly as they land, your forces regroup. The withdrawal is sharp, disciplined, practiced. Cover fire flashes across the shadowed terrain as shuttles lift clear, bringing back supplies, wounded personnel, and the weight of another step forward.

text:
"It's not all that interesting of a history. We came in after the Second AI War with a corporate charter from the Hegemony."

She threads a finger through her hair,  looking off to the side.

"The planet was already inhabited, actually - by the time we arrived, it had absorbed a significant number of refugees from other colonies that didn't survive. Different people with very different ideas about how things should work, on a world that already had its own. It was.. a trying time."

"Refugees rarely get to choose their destinations. The ones who ended up here were fortunate, I think." She smiles. "Though I may be biased."

"Some came here. Some went to Fort Exsedol. Others scattered further."

Sera examines her nails. "There was a man," she says. "A pirate warlord - the name doesn't matter, he was ultimately inconsequential - who decided that a planet in the middle of internal disputes with an overwhelmed Crown was an opportunity."

"He wasn't wrong about the diagnosis, but he was wrong about the prescription."

"What he got instead of an easy conquest was every faction on New Meshan deciding that whatever their disagreements were, they were preferable to him. The native population, the Meshan refugees, the spacer communities - they held together. The Crown coordinated the response while Arma Armatura supplied and supported."

"There were engagements in orbit and on the ground. It was messy. It was also, in retrospect, exactly what New Meshan needed."

options: armaa_seraLoreDumpChurchHub:Ask about the Church of Galactic Redemption

text:
"The Church has been a consistent presence in our operational environment for as long as Arma Armatura has existed in this sector."

"They oppose most forms of technology in principle. We manufacture weapons." She says it the way someone states that water is wet. "You'd expect that to be a straightforward conflict. It isn't, quite. We've supplied Church-affiliated administrators for a long time. Quietly, on both ends."

She looks back at you properly for the first time since the question was asked.
"As for the Knights of Ludd - they can be a considerable asset or a considerable problem depending on the relationship. We've worked to ensure it's the former."
"You have more questions, do you?"

options: armaa_seraLoreDumpChurchFaithResp:Ask what she thinks of the Faith
armaa_seraLoreDumpChurchConflictResp:Ask if ArmaA has ever had conflict with the Church

text:
"Whether or not Ludd was correct in principle - and I have opinions about that - the evidence since has been difficult to argue with."

"I don't share the faith, but I am more familiar with it than most-"
"And I've found that dismissing it is a mistake most factions make exactly once. The Church has outlasted megacorporations, wars, and many who were certain it would collapse under its own contradictions."

"Friction, occasionally. Direct conflict would overstate it."

"There have been administrators who declined our products on doctrinal grounds. Contracts that fell through. A few public statements from Church officials about the arms trade that were pointed in our direction without naming us specifically."

She waves a hand slightly.
"Nothing that constituted a rupture. The Church is pragmatic when it needs to be, and so are we."

A smile arrives that doesn't quite reach anything.

"They're persistent. I'll give them that. The Path operates outside any framework that makes negotiation meaningful. They don't want better terms - they want the industry gone. They've targeted our assets before. Repeatedly."

"Most people don't take them seriously." The smile she'd had doesn't quite reassemble itself. "Until they have a reason to."

"$playerName," she starts, coyly, "Any functional administration understands its dependencies.  You don't build a stable polity by alienating a significant portion of the population over doctrine."

"The Crown handles the things a Crown handles, and the advisory board handles the rest. Did I mention i'm a member of that board" She adds, almost smugly.

"Those with sway in the Church, while dogmatic, are still practical. Our.. arrangement works because everyone involved wants it to."
"I just told you," she says, pleasantly. "You don't alienate a significant portion of your population over doctrine. The specifics follow from that."

"The first ended badly for a lot of people. The second ended... less badly. The sector came out of it with the major factions bloodied but intact, and an unspoken agreement to not examine certain things too closely."

"Arma Armatura lost its homeworld in the first war. That's the relevant personal history. Ask me something specific."
"You have more questions, do you?"

options: armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsStartResp:Ask what started the First AI War
armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsMeshanResp:Ask about ArmaA's history during the First AI War
armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsBanResp:Ask if she thinks the AI ban was justified
armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsArmaRoleResp:Ask about Arma Armatura's role in the Second AI War
armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsPeaceResp:Ask if the sector was right to let it lie
armaa_seraLoreDumpAIWarsRightsResp:Ask if she believes AI should have rights

text:
"The wars nearly ended us. The first one, specifically."

"Meshan was gone. The facilities, the archives, the accumulated work of centuries - most of it gone with it. What survived did so because someone thought to move it in time."

"The second war is where we rebuilt the relationship with the Hegemony into something more formal. The charter for New Meshan came out of that period."

"Most corp don't survive losing their homeworld. We did." A slight pause. "I made certain of it."

"At some point, a Tri-Tachyon fleet entered this system without communication, and the board at the time read it as the opening move. They decided a preemptive strike was the best course of action. It, ah.." 

She flashes her teeth - the corporate equivalent of a wince, "..didn't go well."

"We defended what we could. Our security forces engaged Tri-Tachyon warfleets directly - and lost, for the most part. Most of our senior leadership didn't survive the war."

"The company that came out the other side was considerably smaller than the one that went in. A difficult period, by any account."

"The origin of the planet-cracker was never publicly established," she states.

"The sector moved on. I'd suggest the same."

"I think it was inevitable," she says. "Whether inevitable and justified are the same thing is a question I'll leave to philosophers."

"It created stability where there was none, and stability has value."

"We were part of Daud's emergency commissioning response at Chicomoztoc. One of many, but we were there."

She says it without fanfare. Then, almost offhand:

"The Gekko - one of our carriers - and the unit she was attached to took the brunt of a Tri-Tachyon raiding force during the approach. Several running engagements."

Something shifts in her expression.

"Mobius Squadron held long enough for the remainder to break through."
She moves on without elaborating. As if she'd said enough and knew it.

"We took significant losses. But we came out the other side with a charter and a future. Most didn't come out at all."

"I think it was the only call that didn't risk a third war," she says.

"Justice and stability are not always compatible. When they conflict, I tend to favor stability. Not because justice doesn't matter, but because the alternatives to stability matter more."

"The people who made those agreements understood what they were trading. I won't pretend the trade was costless."

"An interesting question." She tilts her head slightly and narrows her eyes.

"What makes you ask?"
She lets the question sit. Whatever your answer, she receives it without expression - filing it away before she responds.

"The ban exists because the Domain couldn't solve the control problem and the Hegemony inherited that failure along with everything else."

"Whether that's the right framework for the next century is a separate question from whether it was the right response to the last one. I'll leave the rights question to philosophers and politicians. They seem to enjoy it."

The smile she'd had doesn't reassemble itself.

"Do you know about the loyalty assurance devices? A chemical explosive fitted to an intelligence capable of running a civilization. Because that seemed like the appropriate response to something you didn't understand."

"I don't have a philosophical position on AI rights. I have a practical one. You don't fit a thinking thing with a bomb and call it 'loyalty assurance'. You call it fear."

Something in the way she looks at you is different from usual - more present, less managed.

"The question isn't whether AI deserve rights. The question is whether humanity deserves the AI it's built."

She lets that sit for exactly as long as she means to. Then the smile returns.
"But as I said. I'll leave that to philosophers."

"Cataphracts. What's there to say? I like the name - its derived from the title of armored soldiers back in the days humanity was stabbing each other with sharpened sticks atop land creatures." She laughs at her own statement, examining her nails briefly. "A previous director decided that if Arma was going to equip ground forces, they should be able to follow a deployment from orbit to surface without a separate transit arrangement. A sensible idea, in principle."

"The Valken was the result. Which was... fine. It established what the SI line became, anyway. The SII line is a different matter." Something in her attention shifts. "That's what happens when the experimental warfare division gets involved. They asked what vacuum combat actually demands rather than what ground forces were already used to."
You have more questions, do you?

options: armaa_seraLoreDumpCataphractSIResp:Ask about the Cataphract Series I
armaa_seraLoreDumpCataphractSIIResp:Ask about the Cataphract Series II

text:
"They're the... more conventional version." She produces a small figure with a comically large head from somewhere offscreen and holds it up beside her face, tilting her head towards it.  "We even licensed another corporation to develop some model kits in their likeness. All in all, a reliable product."

She sets it down and looks off to the side a moment, idly tapping near her collarbone. "Though there were some unavoidable design concessions." 

"Their space-rated weapons are, obviously, calibrated for vacuum, and don't exactly operate the same in the atmosphere. Plus, the armor is lighter than a dedicated ground platform and distributed for attacks from any direction, instead of being concentrated at the front.."

"So we've gotten complaints that a pilot putting one into a land engagement can end up fighting the machine more than the enemy. A dedicated ground platform would outperform it in that environment." She frowns, and then half-shrugs.  "That was always a known trade-off, though. The design philosophy was never 'excellent at both.' It was 'functional at both.'"

"The Series II Cataphracts was basically the Experimental Warfare Division deciding that if you were going to build something ludicrously complicated, why not go all the way and make it ludicrously expensive too." She pinches the bridge of her nose.

"They're built higher spec than the SIs and also cost as much as a fully equipped destroyer. A lot of miniaturization R&D went into even making them viable. We deployed a lot of them during the war, though they weren't enough."

Both hands come up to her face for a moment. Exasperation? You're not sure.

"Beam weapons track faster than they can evade, and heavy energy fire overwhelms capacity before they can disengage. We've adjusted doctrine accordingly."

"Anyway, they're all equipped with top of the line neural interfaces, which makes them pretty easy to pilot. They sell well, and the data we've gotten has been..illuminating."

text:
She idly taps a few buttons offscreen before a hologram of the Valkazard blooms to life.

A finger traces a slow line across the hologram, highlighting a dense cluster of subsystems around the core.

"The Valkazard is not a 'superweapon' in the simplistic sense. It's more like a testbed for a family of technologies that sit in the shadow of the Gates."

"SILVERSWORD is the most obvious example. It's a containment-and-transfer solution - it opens a bounded pocket, a controlled 'elsewhere,' and diverts the ship's flux load, waste heat, and transient charge into it.
With the core no longer choking on its own buildup, the Valkazard can safely overdrive: higher current to the weapon buses, hotter cycle rates, and more aggressive thrust profiles - for as long as the pocket holds."

"You'd be amazed what you can bury in paperwork. The Persean Sector was perfect for that."

Her hand flicks, and the hologram tightens around the Valkazard's core.

"It was sent here for further testing without drawing too much attention. And because if something went wrong... it would go wrong far away from anyone important. Which is to say: far away from anyone with a career to protect." A faint warning lattice blooms across the core cluster.

"So, as I mentioned, SILVERSWORD doesn't make flux vanish. It moves it. It opens a bounded pocket, a controlled elsewhere, and shunts a portion of the units capacity into it. Think of it as a balloon being filled. The more you push into it, the more pressure builds."

Her voice drops slightly. "Push it too long and it begins to bulge. And eventually, it will burst."

"In other words, the pocket tries to materialize in normal space. Rather violently." The corner of her mouth lifts. "Though I'm sure the ensuing explosion is quite beautiful."

options: armaa_valkLoreWhereGoResp:"So it doesn't vent. It relocates it. Into where, exactly?"
armaa_valkLoreWhyLegsResp:"So..what does it need legs, then?"
armaa_valkLoreWhatDoWithScanResp:"What are you going to do with the scan data?"
armaa_valkLoreNoMoreResp:"That's all I had to ask." (End)

text:
"Correct." She lifts one shoulder in a small shrug.

"Into where? I can't give you a tidy label. Somewhere that isn't here. Call it a controlled elsewhere."

"Yes," Sera Pha said, as if you'd referenced an earnings report instead of a sector-shaking event. "The Janus Device."

"I'm aware you were present for its first live demonstration. It would have been difficult to miss. Footage of your fleet passing through was circulating across the Sector within hours - enthusiastic, terrified, and heavily edited depending on who was doing the sharing."

A faint smile. Not quite approval.

"People hear 'Gate' and assume anything that bends distance is the same endeavor. It isn't."

She tapped the Valkazard readout again.

"The Janus Device is a key turned in a lock that already exists. Whatever else it is - genius, heresy, miracle - it's still exploiting pre-Collapse infrastructure and pre-Collapse tolerances."

Her expression sharpened.

"Our work is not a key. It is an attempt to manufacture another door."

"Gate technology is a network problem. Addressing, synchronization, destination stability - all of it built atop something the Domain spent centuries refining. SILVERSWORD-class systems are containment problems. Local apertures. Anchors without endpoints."

She leaned back, composed again.

"Because appearances matter," Sera Pha replies almost instantaneously. "Even in engineering."

She lets the holo rotate, the legs highlighted like an indulgence.

"Valkazard could have been a pod, a brick, or a flying power core with thrusters stapled to it. Legs do not make it function. Legs make it legible - to investors, to officers, to whatever committee the company needed convincing that this was a weapons platform and not an expensive physics experiment."

She smiles faintly. "They're theater. Very expensive theater."

"With the data?" Her pitch rises as she repeats the last word. "Captain, if Arma Armatura ever stopped knowing what to do with information, we'd deserve the Sector's pity."

"Officially, it goes into comparative analysis. We match Valkazard's signatures against what remains of our legacy archives, confirm what still aligns, and identify what was lost, altered, et cetera." Her eyes flick up. "A baseline is priceless."

"Unofficially? It tells me where the real boundaries are. What can be reproduced safely, what can only be approximated, and what is best left as a cautionary tale."

"I don't intend to build a network system in my boardroom, if that's what you're asking. But I do intend to make sure Arma is never surprised again - by a relic, by a rival, or by some enthusiast with more ambition than restraint."

She tapped the display and it collapsed back into neat, meaningless summaries.

"When you handed over the Valkazard some time ago, the SILVERSWORD system within it piqued my interest," she explains, "It's truly fascinating technology. While our Garegga-class mechs also incorporate a similar system, the one in the Valkazard is remarkably more compact and possesses unique enhancements. Ever since, my team and I have been dedicated to unlocking its full potential."

You can't help but wonder if there's more to this seemingly generous gesture, knowing how corporate dealings usually work.

$HeOrShe exhales, tone turning businesslike. "All right. First job is simple to say and hard to do: we need a man out of Cruor." A pause. A file packet pings onto your display. One face. Hollow cheeks. Eyes like ground glass.

A faint, humorless smirk.

"Ah, captain," says $personName, a glint in her eyes as she delicately retrieves a datapad. "Allow me to access my agenda, and we shall proceed accordingly."

"Word amongst the independent polities is that they've taken quite an interest in the Sector's hottest rising player. Perhaps you are aware, but the balance of power is rather tenuous." There's a pause,  as if assessing your reaction. "It's odd they've chosen to put so much pressure on you," she continues, "but as an, ah...organization in a rather similar situation, you have my sympathy."

Her words are chosen carefully, each syllable carrying weight as she measures their impact. 

"I can't exactly support you directly, we lack the means to openly oppose the League. But if they're coming after you like this..well, I have no reason to doubt others will follow." 

"I can offer you some form of assistance, if you're interested." She let's the offer hangs in the air.

"Arma Armatura may weather the storm, but the ripple effects on New Meshan's economy could prove... inconvenient." Her lips curve into a wry smile. "And as a savvy businesswoman, inconvenience is not something I take lightly."

She adopts a tone of strategic calculation, "Firstly, we can explore the possibility of discouraging the League from further harassment through a simple show of force. However, I must caution you, this may prove exceedingly difficult depending on the resources they've committed to coercing you."

"Alternatively," she continues, "joining them is an option, but accepting any initial offer would be folly. We must enter negotiations from a position of power." emphasizing the importance of this approach.

"I'm confident we can find a way to leverage your position effectively," she assures, a faint smile playing on her lips. "Perhaps a bargaining chip against the top brass at Kazeron could tip the scales in our favor. Something vital to their operations."

"As for aid," she adds, her voice carrying a note of reassurance, "I'll dispatch some of my 'special forces' to stand by your side should things escalate."

Regarding upholding the agreement, she waves a dismissive hand. "Worry not about that now. I'll call upon you when the time is right. For now, focus on your own problems."

She crosses her legs casually. "But remember, there are more subtle ways to navigate these waters." Her gaze holds a knowing gleam. "Strength isn't always about direct confrontation."

The colony has a formidable navy for an independent colony, sure, but standing up to the League alone? By your estimations, that would be impossible.

"These are likely from the First AI War, a conflict that saw Arma Armatura's original headquarters and home planet obliterated," $HeOrShe shares. "Our corporation suffered greatly at the hands of these machines. It seems that even after all this time, they continue to pose a threat."

"Whatever they're up to, some important people will sleep better if it's stopped. The reward is $bcb_reward credits - higher than normal, both for the hazard and for your discretion. Get it done within $bcb_days days."

"I speak in strict confidence," $heOrShe looks sternly at you. "A tech-mining flotilla spotted a fleet of Remnant drone warships in the $bcb_systemName, $bcb_dist light-years distant. That flotilla didn't make it back."

"We did find an intel drop left by that flotilla, and in it we found this." $Rank $personLastName pulls up a grainy image, little more than a silhouette in false color. An angular shape. "This..thing is no simple remnant from the First AI War, my friend. It's extremely dangerous." With a swipe the $rank dismisses the image. 

"Do me a favor and get rid of it. The reward is $bcb_reward credits. If that seems high, it's because you'd be dealing with something unusual. Unusual means risky, and STRATCOM thinks an unusual problem could use an unusual solution, thus-" $heOrShe indicates you with an open palm. "Are you in?"
"I have a few targets that need to be eliminated," $heOrShe says with a grin. "Care to take a look?"

"We've got a few options here." $Post $personLastName pulls up a shared display with a table of intel reports. "I have an HK flotilla on standby to eliminate these targets, but here's the delightful twist, my dear: it's your choice. You can have your pick of the bunch, and they'll handle whatever you don't. What type of job do you believe you can handle?"

$HeOrShe taps the entry. The holo expands - arrest record, scowling face, the usual.

"A pirate. Hiding in $bcb_systemName, $bcb_dist light-years out." She glances at it without much interest. "Fell out with an associate who talked.  It happens."

She moves on.

"$bcb_reward credits. $bcb_days to bring it in. I'm told the location is the exciting part."

"There's a station that needs to come down," $personName says,setting the triPad aside.

"$bcb_fleetName.$bcb_systemName - $bcb_dist light-years out."

The holographic display blooms between you, the target rotating slowly.

"$bcb_reward credits on completion. $bcb_days days." 

"The timeline matters. I have other arrangements that depend on it."

$heOrShe lets that sit without elaborating. "The rest isn't your concern."

"Ah, good. Well then, if you'll excuse me..."
OR
"Can I help you with anything else?" $heOrShe asks.
OR
"Then it is agreed and done," $heOrShe nods.
OR
"Good," $heOrShe says, "All parties confirmed."

"I've got a rather unconventional extraction mission. We can't afford to act directly lest we upset the delicate situation the company finds itself in - therefore, this task demands the expertise of a versatile independent unit such as yours."

text:
$personName pulls up the datafile and transfers it without ceremony.

"$extr_marketFaction colony. They'll likely be expecting company."

She pauses while you review the contents.

"I'd recommend bringing $extr_marines marines. The defensive profile warrants it."

$HeOrShe closes the display. "Try not to make it messier than it needs to be, please."

"I have some work that requires a more direct approach than my usual channels allow."

"Nothing that should trouble you. Compensation is competitive."

As you approach, $heOrShe inspects you with professional detachment, subtle cybernetics flickering on the edge of IR. "An operator, are you? Like getting your hands dirty?" 

$HeOrShe doesn't smile. The expression is closer to a predator baring its teeth.

"I might be able to arrange a job which matches your... talents," $heOrShe says, implants flickering in $hisOrHer eyes.

"A trade fleet." She pulls up the customs order and slides it toward you. "Their manifest has attracted some administrative attention in $bcb_systemName - $bcb_dist light-years out. A friendly officer has arranged an inspection that should keep them in place for a while."


"There's a patrol ensuring they stay put. I'll leave the specifics of that to you."

"$bcb_reward credits. $bcb_days days before the inspection concludes and the window closes."

"Fleet officer. $bcb_patrolFaction, operating out of $bcb_targetMarketName in $bcb_systemName - $bcb_dist light-years. Patrols near the station, jump points, comm relays."

"$bcb_reward credits. $bcb_days days.

"...They've made themselves inconvenient to certain interests. That's all you need."

"A mercenary." She brings up the docking concourse image - blurry, incidental-looking. "Currently in $bcb_systemName, $bcb_dist light-years out. We've given them something to chase. They won't be expecting company."

"$bcb_reward credits. $bcb_days days."

"It's an ...associates problem that needs solving. The details aren't relevant to your end of it."

text:
"I'm a liaison for the Meshan Reprisal Coalition, a voice and hand of those who refuse to let the crimes of the past be buried under the ashes of the AI Wars. We exist to ensure justice is served-or, failing that, that those who defy accountability feel the weight of their actions."

"Of course, titles and introductions are meaningless without actions. If you truly wish to understand who we are, watch what we do-or better yet, join us."

"You know who we are. The Meshan Reprisal Coalition needs no introduction, least of all to you. We are the force that ensures justice is not a choice but an inevitability. And you? You're just another obstacle."

script:
AddTextSmall "MRC HQs are the the planning grounds for Coalition Reprisals; operations aimed at 'liberating' colonies from th rule of factions hostile to the MRC. The frequency of these operations increase as the MRC's successes do, but there is a minimum cooldown period of 6 months." highlight
AddTextSmall "It's possible to participate in these operations by simply attacking fleets hostile to the MRC in the affected systems. Independent captains are generally rewarded with a percentage of the loot the MRC acquires, regardless of the missions success." highlight
text:
"This office coordinates operations in the region. We monitor the movements of factions and individuals who bear the stain of unpunished atrocities, ensuring they are reminded of the weight of their deeds." $heOrShe tils $hisOrHer head thoughtfully.

"More practically, I liaise with independent captains like yourself, arranging partnerships for those who wish to contribute to our noble cause. Or perhaps you're here to absolve a stain of your own?"

script:
AddTextSmall "MRC HQs are the the planning grounds for Coalition Reprisals; operations aimed at 'liberating' colonies from th rule of factions hostile to the MRC. The frequency of these operations increase as the MRC's successes do, but there is a minimum cooldown period of 6 months." highlight
AddTextSmall "As is, the MRC does not trust you, and will refuse to acknowledge any contributions you make until your standing has improved." highlight
text:
"We identify targets. We mobilize. We deliver retribution. That's the short version."

"Perhaps you'd like to know where you rank on our list? Rest assured, you've earned a special place in our records, though we're debating whether you're a true threat-or just an annoyance we'll deal with later."

"The Coalition identifies targets through extensive intelligence gathering-systems, factions, or individuals. When a target is chosen, fleets are mobilized to disrupt, destabilize, and dismantle their operations."

"Some call us marauders. Others, vigilantes. The truth? We are agents of balance, a counterweight to unchecked power. A system destabilized by our actions is one more resistant to tyranny. An entity that refuses justice must be made to fear it."

- $heOrShe laughs dryly.
"Curious, are we? Or perhaps you're here to learn how to avoid being on the receiving end. Our reprisals are precise and inevitable. Planets liberated, fleets dismantled, power structures toppled. And when the dust settles, our message remains clear: resist justice, and you'll burn in its wake."

"A straightforward question, and one with many answers. You could join our fleets during a reprisal, share intelligence on enemies of the Coalition, or even donate resources to fuel our operations."

"Of course, such actions may earn you the ire of certain powers. But no great act of justice comes without sacrifice, eh?"

"Help? Oh, you've done more than enough already. Your status with the Coalition have spoken loud and clear. Unless you've come to beg for forgiveness, which I find unlikely, you can 'help' by find your way out of the nearest airlock."

text: "The Coalition values your aid," the liaison says with a solemn nod. "These tools of war shall find their way to those who wield them in the pursuit of justice-or something akin to it. Rest assured, they will not gather dust."

"Moving five units of heavy armaments won't go unnoticed by certain powers, but such risks are a necessary part of the cause. If anything, it only underscores the urgency of your support."



They offer a faint smile, though it carries a hint of caution. "With luck, discretion will carry the day. And if not-well, we have other methods to ensure these weapons find their rightful place."

"An intriguing gesture," the liaison admits, their tone veering between skepticism and grudging acceptance. "Five units of heavy armaments, smuggled under the watchful eyes of the authorities, could prove useful. That said, I wonder if your motivations are truly so altruistic."



They let the silence hang for a moment. 

"No matter. The weapons will serve their purpose, though the consequences of discovery will be yours to bear. If you choose to gamble, ensure you are prepared to pay the price."

text:
You're nursing a drink and a half-interest in leaving when the tone shifts. A group of local toughs-shipless pirates, from the look-are zeroing in on your table. One of them knocks over a chair, not by accident. "That your $shipOrFleet in orbit?" one of them slurs, half sober. "Figure someone oughta collect a toll for all that hull flash."

You don't have time to answer. One of your bodyguards is already standing, hand drifting to their sidearm, eyes locked. The second steps to your flank. This seems to give the less bold ones pause, and for a moment, it looks like that'll be the end of it. But someone's ego doesn't get the message.

A bottle smashes against the wall beside you. One of the pirates-wired with black-market augments-lunges forward with preternatural speed; your bodyguard moves faster. The strike is intercepted, a pistol drawn but left unfired. Another pirate catches an elbow and crumples. 

In the press of bodies, you're shoved back, separated from your escorts.

One of the earlier pirates spots you. He straightens, flexing cracked knuckles under the bar's flickering lights. "Well, well," he says, "Look what we've got here."

text:
Your people take the usual positions without being told; backs to bulkheads, sightlines on doors, one of them pretending to be interested in the drink list while they count exits.

That's why you notice someone a second before they reach your table. A slow approach: open hands, eyes on your guards first, not you. A professional acknowledging other professionals.

They stop short of your nearest bodyguard and nod once. "Captain," they say. "Your retinue's well trained."

One of your bodyguards shifts half a step. The mercenary doesn't flinch, but raises his hands, palms upwards and facing you. 

"I'm not here to test it," they add. 

They set a small data-slate on the edge of the table.

text:
"I'm passing a line," they say, voice low enough that your booth keeps it contained. "A contract is moving through the usual channels."

They meet your eyes.

"You've got the tonnage and the reputation to get the offer. So I'm saving everyone time."

"Name's Kade," they say. A finger taps the dead slate.

"He's looking to hire mercs to 'liberate' a world called Jenius, backwater little planet. The locals won't see us coming until our ships paint their sky."

text: "Funny. I'll make sure they find your teeth in the drain." He draws a knife.

He laughs an ugly laugh.

"Yeah? Start countin'."
"No, but we're gonna. Makes me feel better. You wouldn't understand." He pulls something metallic out from his pocket. You catch a glint of it off some light. A knife.

He squints. The joke went somewhere he couldn't follow.
"Shut up," he says.

Something flickers across his face.
"...Pather?" he says. His gait slows, but he continues to approach.

He stops, and looks at you. Then behind you.
"...What?"

You stare at the ceiling dramatically. 

Nothing happens.

He watches this with genuine confusion.
"...Right," he says, and closes in.

The pirate snarls - rage, panic, momentum? Hard to say. He commits to the charge like a destroyer activating it's burn drive. You shift your weight, slipping under the first swing - a clean vector adjustment. The second comes in fast, but you jam a stool into its arc. Impact. The improvised defense shatters, but buys you enough time to reposition.

. Strikes flying blind, glass breaking, shouted orders lost under music no one bothered to cut.
Your escorts vanish into the scramble - locked in their own engagements or screened off entirely.

You move like a frigate under pressure, threading gaps between tables and broken chairs, avoiding another wild strike from someone running high on stimulants and bad instincts. You're fast, but not invulnerable. Your flux is building, and you're isolated.

One of your attackers is yanked back by the collar, slammed face-first into the counter. Another staggers forward, only to be caught mid-swing - his wrist locked, folded back until his knees give out. Your guards regroup through the chaos, subduing what's left. The room goes quiet the way bars do after a fight - nobody looking at anybody.

One of the men who pulled you out steps forward. Scar along his jaw. 

"You hold your ground," he says. "That counts for something."
He places a card on the table. 

"Heard the stories. Figured you'd end up here eventually. If you're looking for work - Fort Exsedol. Gamlin system."

He turns to leave, then glances back. "Pleasure, Captain."
"You? A pawn of the same rot we're fighting to cleanse from this Sector. Don't expect any handshakes from us."
"You're already commissioned by $theOtherCommissionFaction. Divided loyalty is a weakness we can't afford. Come back when you're ready to cut ties."
"Not everyone has the stomach for this fight. You're not ready for what it takes to join the Reprisal Coalition."

"You've proven yourself useful, and that's the only measure we care about. Joining our cause means no mercy, no exceptions-you will act swiftly against those we deem enemies. Turning back? Rarely an option."
"Do you have what it takes to join us and dismantle those who poison this Sector? Will you accept the weight of this cause?"
"Welcome to the Meshan Reprisal Coalition. It takes a rare spine to stand with us-don't waste it."
"Enough talk. You know what to do. Don't hesitate to reach out if you need support on this."
"This path isn't for the faint-hearted, and no one here will blame you for reconsidering. There are other ways to be useful, and who knows-maybe one day you'll find the courage to step back in."
"Leaving? You knew the risks from day one. But understand this: once you turn your back, we won't have use for you again. You'll be marked by those you let down."
"So that's it, then. Once an ally, now just another face in the crowd. Remember, we were the only ones willing to fight back when no one else would."
"Good choice. It's a grim road, but at least here, you know your efforts mean something. No one else will hold the line like we do."

options: 10:armaa_btsStart: Ask if there's more serious work available

text:
"You've pulled weight, Captain. A lot of it."

$HeOrShe taps a key. A holofile flickers to life, showing the image of a rather tired looking man. "Got an asset- codename FLINT. Embedded with Tri-Tachyon for two cycles. Went dark a dozen shifts back. Normally, standard protocol says we assume dead. But I've been doing this too long not to know which ones crack. If they've got him-and I'd wager they do- it's INFOSEC blown wide open. If we move fast, we contain it. If not..."
options: 10:armaa_bts2: "Right, so you want me to extract them?"

script:

$global.armaa_bts_marketId = $armaa_bts_marketId

text:
"No. I want you to make sure he doesn't..hasn't talked." The holofile loops silently in the background. "If he's clean, extraction may not even be needed. If he's cracked, extraction's a liability. Make contact. Or confirm he hasn't gone out through a pressure seam-if you catch my drift."

$HeOrShe smiles apologetically, "I know this might feel beneath your station. But if we send one of ours, it lights up every scanner from here to Eochu. You, though... people like you move different. Little more room to maneuver. Little more plausible deniability."

options: 10:armaa_btsOpRisksResp: "And what about me? If this goes sideways-how much of it sticks to me?"
10:armaa_btsOpRisksResp: "You do realize i'm on Tri-Tachyon's payroll, right?"
10:armaa_btsOpCompromisedResp: "How do I know if he's been compromised?"
10:armaa_btsOpAcceptResp: "No promises on how this ends. But I'll look.(accept)"

text:
"If it all goes smooth, you get a pat on the back and another job down the line."
"If it doesn't? You're just another independent making a bad decision on Tri-Tach turf. Happens every cycle."

"That plausible deniability you enjoy? It cuts both ways, captain."

"You work for us. That means you get cover. Follow the brief, keep to your lane, and if something breaks we absorb it."

"But if you choose to improvise, or make it public… I can't protect you from the paperwork or the politics."

$HeOrShe smiles faintly. "Do it our way, and you're fine."

$personName smirks, a look of quiet amusement building on their face. "You think that makes you untouchable? Or... just harder to trace? Half their black projects run on outsourced freelancers and falsified credentials." 

"You're not the first captain dancing in two rooms at once. If it's too close to home, walk. No shame in that. But if you stay in- just keep your story straight, and you should be fine. If anything, this should be an advantage."

"FLINT was run through an R&D credentials wash. Low-tier clearance. Just enough to keep him under Tri-Tach's audit pings without raising flags." $HeOrShe gives you a look-flat, unreadable. "Stuff that doesn't officially exist. Or, if it does, it's buried under six layers of cost codes and one very nervous project lead."

"I can't go into specifics-you understand-but we were hoping to dig up any dirt those suits might be sitting on."

"You won't get a blinking light that says 'traitor.'" $HeOrShe laughs at $hisOrHer own statement, before clearing $hisOrHer throat. "Wish I could offer you something cleaner."

$HeOrShe taps something off-screen, and when $heOrShe speaks again, the warmth is gone. "FLINT was working out of a Tri-Tachyon subsidiary. Officially, it's nothing major. But he was positioned just close enough to see the edges of something proprietary. Ask the right questions, listen long enough, and someone will try to impress you with something they shouldn't say out loud. Just remember-places like that run on performance reviews and knives in backs. 'Loyalty' is a label they apply retroactively."

text: "That's all we ask. Good luck."

"That's all we ask. Good luck." $HeOrShe moves to cut the feed, but pauses.

"...Oh, I almost forgot. I'm cleared to allocate you some extra resources for this one. We have a specialist, of sorts. Actually, you may remember him." $HeOrShe smirks.

There's a soft click. A second line splices in - quieter, tighter bandwidth.

A familiar voice comes through, roughened at the edges.

A face resolves on the comm overlay. Sleeves rolled to the forearm,  a mug of something dark steaming beside the keypad. He doesn't sit like a guest. He sits like someone waiting out a long watch.

He looks at the camera for a second, not trying to intimidate you. Just taking stock. Then he speaks. "...Captain."
options:
armaa_btsOpAcceptRespSavedWhoYou:"Do I know you?"
armaa_btsOpAcceptRespSavedWhoYou:"Aren't you the agent I rescued?"

text:
His eyes narrow slightly - not offended. Just recalibrating.

"Lingfeng. You pulled me off a rock that wasn't letting people leave."

"You remember now?"

For a second he just looks at you - like he's matching a face to an old moment. Then the corner of his mouth twitches.

"Lingfeng. One of the prisoners you rescued from a Sindrian operation." His eyes flick down to the mug, back up.

"You didn't just drag me out. You cleared the whole pen." He says it like it's a fact on a report, but the warmth is there anyway.

"So... if you're drawing a blank, I'll make it easy. Same guy you made a lot of noise to pull out of the fire."

"...Ring a bell?"

"I had a flight queued to FLINT's last known. Dock C. Ten minutes." He lifts the mug slightly, then sets it down with a soft click.

"Then I get a notification: reassignment. New lead operator." The corner of his mouth twitches again, dry but not unkind.

"You."

He places his elbows on his desk console, posture casual like it's a bar conversation - but his eyes are all business. "Look. I'm not one to leave my debts unpaid."

"I know Tri-Tach procedures. I can get you in the right rooms. I can tell you when you're being steered. I can put eyes on someone without them noticing."

He sits back, shoulders settling. "You already proved you don't need an excuse to do the right thing, so, I'm offering to make it easier."

"Besides. I already packed."
options:
armaa_btsOpAcceptJustCapturedResp:Question his ability to remain undetected
armaa_btsOpAcceptBringLingFeng:Accept
cutCommLink:Cut the link

text:
You remind him that the last time you crossed paths, you found him in a cage. 

He tips the mug slightly. "If you're worried... I've already met my 'getting caught' quota for this year."

"Besides. You're the one who walked in and cleaned out the whole pen." He shrugs, like it's just math.

"If anyone's going to pull me out twice... might as well be you."

You give him the go-ahead. The comm feed lingers on Lingfeng for a second. He nods once, already moving.

"Copy."

You cut the line and call for your quartermaster. A few terse orders later, a spare berth is cleared - nothing fancy, but private, and far enough from the main corridor that curious crew won't make a sport of it.  Your quartermaster looks up from the slate. "We'll have him onboard within the hour, captain. I'll make sure he gets a bunk and a locker."

You nod. The details don't matter. Not yet.
"Of course. No pressure."
"You're back. I take it you have news?"

text: "Then that's that. Felner was always a liability. Good work."

text:
$personName cocks an eyebrow. You send the data over, as $heOrShe reviews it. "Bartered for his life, did he? Hm."

"...That'll do." $heOrShe pauses, a little too long to be comfortable, then exhales loudly. "But let's be clear. You weren't told to cut a deal."
$HeOrShe sets the pad down. "There are people above me who won't like this. But not everyone in this line of work climbs the ladder by following instructions. Some of us got here by doing what had to be done-quietly, competently." $heOrShe lets that hang, just long enough for you to catch the double meaning.

text:
You lean into the social machinery Tri-Tachyon lives by. A little respectful enthusiasm, a little shared context. You let the administrator talk about their workload and their responsibilities - and you make sure they feel seen for it.

Somewhere along the line, contact details are exchanged. A low-stakes connection ping on TriLink. Nothing that looks like a bribe, nothing that looks like coercion. Just networking - the polite fiction that lubricates the Sector.

By the time the conversation circles back to Felner, $heOrShe is already halfway convinced this is harmless.

"I can't disclose details," $heOrShe says automatically - and then, a second later, adds: "But if you were a captain with good instincts, you'd check the transit manifests tied to this cost code."

The slate flashes for a fraction of a second. Long enough.

script:
$armaa_askedBtsAdminFelner = true
SetStoryOption armaa_btsAdminQuestionRespSuckUp ui_char_spent_story_point_leadership "Convinced Tri-Tachyon administrator to give agent's location"
text:
"Of course. One moment, captain."
A few keystrokes, a flick of the wrist across the interface.

"Felner's file's tagged. Reassigned-technically-though the notation's... vague." A pause. He leans in, voice lowered. "That's usually code for something board-adjacent. You didn't hear that from me."

"Do you."

He doesn't look at you. Just taps slowly at a terminal. Then stops, deliberately.

"No one by that name logged here. Try somewhere else."
options: 10:armaa_btsAdminQuestionRespSus2: "I've seen the logs. He was stationed here.""

text:
$HeOrShe smiles. A rehearsed one you've seen many from the company display.

"And you're flagged for hostile conduct in this volume. Maybe rethink the questions you ask on my time, captain." A pause. The line is nearly a threat.

script: AddBarEvent armaa_btsExecAsk "Try to gather information on FLINT" "Several executives lounge about. This may be a good a place as any to get intel."

text:
You nurse a drink, Ask a few quiet questions. The first couple don't stick. The third gets you a look-then a nod-toward a booth in the back.

A junior executive-just crisp enough to be important, just drunk enough to talk-waves you over with the half-hearted confidence of someone who wants to impress a stranger but can't remember why.

"You're not Corps. Not dressed for it. But you're asking about Felner."

A tilt of the glass. He's fishing.

"Felner's not here anymore, spacer. Got himself flagged for 'Strategic Restabilization.'" He makes little air quotes.

"Means he said the wrong thing to the right person-or maybe the right thing to someone he shouldn't've. Either way, he's off-grid. But not off-off." 

"Word is, he's posted up in a hillside chalet out by the rim. Quiet. Private. You don't get that kind of perk unless you're either plugged in or radioactive."
options: armaa_btsExecAsk4:"Why are you telling me this?"

text:
"Because I've been passed over for, for two cycles and, I... like watching the dominoes fall." A sip. A grin. Then, more seriously:

"The name's Chalet Theta-5. That's all I've got. You didn't hear it from me, and I don't want to see your ship tagged on the outbound registry. You didn't come here for a chat, right? You're just another spacer burning fuel." He waves you off, and his attention drifts back to the empty glass in his hand like he forgot it was his.

You punch in the comms ID for $personName. A high-fidelity line crackles to life, unusually sharp for a civilian comms.

A man appears on screen: trim, clean-shaven, and dressed like someone who hasn't worried about job security in a long time. The background behind him is all white marble and golden dusklight. Somewhere expensive. He looks at you for a long moment. "Captain $playerName. I assume this isn't a cold call."
