text:
The surface polish starts to crack just a bit - not fear, but reassessment. "If they sent you, things must be worse than I thought. Or better. That depends on what kind of knife you're holding." He exhales through his nose, almost a sigh. "Alright. I'll bite. You want a conversation, you come here. I'm not putting anything on the net."

He taps a key off-screen. A location pings into your nav-remote, local, private.

script:
ShowLargePlanet
armaa_Hikaru_Utada playMusic music_encounter_mysterious
$option = armaa_btsTravelToChalet3
FireBest DialogOptionSelected
text:
The shuttle crests over a line of sculpted ridges, heat shimmer rising off glassy soil. The estate-Chalet Theta-5-is exactly as described: sleek, private, and utterly exposed. Your comms officer pings the perimeter channel, but no response is required. A private node flashes green: ACCESS GRANTED. You disembark alone.

The path to the main structure is flanked by synthetic pines and whisper-quiet filtration towers. A drone drifts overhead, tracking but not challenging.
The pistol rides high under your uniform, familiar in its snug holster. Any personal scanner won't notice it. It's light, quick, and dirty.

The weight in your uniform is subtle but unmistakable. Compact casing, manual trigger, adjustable yield. You adjusted the fuse timing back on the shuttle - just in case talking turns into something less productive.

The knife sits sheathed along your ribs, silent now, but your hearing remembers the high, insectile whine it made when activated. When live, it can part armor like cloth. You keep one hand close to where it rests.

You check the capsule inside your cuff - with a mere flick of the hand and a demon's exhalation you could ensure the rapid loss of consciousness or death of a small cluster of adult humans within a range of a few meters. The poisons would then react with self-contained nanotech pseudo-proteins to dissolve into a wisp of molecules which could be plausibly found on any imperfectly maintained spacefaring vessel.

Beneath your glove, the false fingertip sits flush - a concealed housing for a monomolecular wire: a molecule-thin cutting edge, invisible and lethally precise. A weapon for work that requires discretion.
You came alone, and came bare - no edge, no sidearm. Just boots on soil and the rhythm of breath. Confidence or arrogance, you haven't decided. But you've fought with less.

The weight of the blade tugs at your side with every step, solid and old-world. Steel, hand-forged, carried low in the conservative fashion of Hegemony lifers. There's no hiding it - but you didn't come to hide.

The interior is all engineered woodgrain and neutral lighting. Felner stands at the far end of the room, beside a low glass table set with two untouched drinks. 
He watches you approach with neither fear nor welcome.
He nods toward the second glass. "Help yourself, if you want."
options:
armaa_btsTravelToChalet6a: "You sound like you're expecting the worst."
armaa_btsTravelToChalet6b: "Why haven't you been responding?"
armaa_btsTravelToChalet6c: "Nice place. The corporate life must be treating you well."

text: "I just wanted to see if anyone still believed there was a way out - something between vanishing quietly and someone being sent to tie off loose ends."

"Because anything worth saying was better left unsaid." He swirls the drink, but doesn't sip. "Besides, if I'd kept reporting, I'd have lied. And if I'd told the truth... well. We both know what would've happened."
"It has its perks.  He glances around.

"You know what I realized?" He doesn't wait for your answer. 
"The MRC talks like it's building something. But it doesn't create. It takes, or it breaks. Tri-Tachyon? They don't bother pretending, they build. Terrible things, sometimes- but things all the same." He pauses, swirling the glass, eyes fixed on the slow orbit of the liquid. "And they paid me to understand it. Not to posture about why we're supposed to hate it. Just to study how the machine runs." A thin breath. "And I did. And I liked it. More than I thought I would."

He sets the glass down, gently but with finality. "You can tell them I'm still breathing, if that's what they want to hear.  But I'm not coming back."

"Unless you've got a better reason than 'orders,' you might as well finish your drink."
options:
armaa_btsTravelToChalet8b: "They want you..taken care of if you refused to return."
armaa_btsTravelToChaletKill: "I see." (attack)

text:
He just nods, slow and silent, like he'd already guessed but hoped you wouldn't say it out loud. "Of course they do." He pinches the bridge of his nose and exhales, lips pressed into a thin line. "Can't have a quiet defection. Gotta make it a lesson." He turns slightly, not toward you but toward the dark window-nothing but city smog and shifting light out there.

"Funny thing is, I never sold them out." He looks back to you, jaw set.  "So. That's the offer. Walk out, and they come for me later. Pull the trigger now, and save them the paperwork." 

"Or... we make this a problem worth solving. You and me. Right now."

options: armaa_btsChaletWhyDefectResp: "You went AWOL for comfort? Or is that just the easy story?"
armaa_btsChaletAnyDeathResp: "Did anyone die because of you?"
armaa_btsTravelToChalet9: "I've heard enough to make my decision."

text:
A thin smile, no warmth in it. "Tri-Tach at least has the decency to be honest about what it is. And yeah - I liked being treated like a person instead of a tool with a half-life."

"The real reason is I stopped pretending the MRC was going to do anything with what I brought back. We don't build or invest. We extract, we sabotage, we destroy. We call it 'balance' and congratulate ourselves for not being 'them'."

His jaw tightens immediately. "Not because I sold anyone out. I didn't."

He pinches the bridge of his nose again, breath controlled. "But when I went dark, the MRC panicked. Probably pulled people who were too deep to be pulled clean."

"So did someone die because of me? Indirectly? Probably."

"You can't."

He taps the table once with two fingers. "All you can do is decide what kind of risk you can live with."

He eyes you, assessing. "If I had ill intent, you'd already be dead. Instead it's just me and a drink I haven't touched."

"...I never gave them the MRC. But I won't insult you by pretending that means I'm safe. They're always watching. The only honest offer I have is value on the table… and the chance to walk out without blood on your hands."

He reaches into his jacket slowly - not for a weapon, but a small data chip, flat and clean. He sets it on the glass table between you like a card in a game.

"Most of it is useless." His eyes flick up to yours. "But buried in it is something real."

He taps the chip once. "A prototype design package. Special Acquisitions skunkworks - and not purely theirs. Arma Armatura had fingerprints on it. Quiet collaboration through backchannels, the kind that never hits a press release."

"..It's a mobile weapons platform. Not a ship, not a suit - something meant to make the gap between the two irrelevant."

"You bring that home, and nobody up-chain cares whether I died in this room. They'll care what you brought back. That's the trade. You walk out with proof. I get to keep breathing."
options:
armaa_btsTravelToChalet11a: "You think they'll accept this instead of your body?"
armaa_btsTravelToChalet11b: "I'm taking a huge risk letting you walk. I want credits too."
armaa_btsTravelToChaletKill:(attack)

text: He watches you, gauging whether you're the type to appreciate the quiet calculus of survival. "Maybe it doesn't. Maybe the chip gets shredded in some backroom and I get marked for cleanup anyway." A thin shrug. "That's not your problem unless you decide to make it one."
options:
armaa_btsTravelToChalet11b: "I'm taking a huge risk letting you walk. I want credits too."
armaa_btsTravelToChalet12: Accept
armaa_btsTravelToChaletKill: Attack

text: He smirks, not insulted - just resigned. "You should. Everyone else already got paid to put me in the ground." He fishes a TriPad from his coat and extends it. You do the same. A single tap, and a transfer request lights up. "20,000. Half now… and half once it's done."
options:
armaa_btsTravelToChalet12: "You have yourself a deal"
armaa_btsTravelToChaletKill: Attack

script:
ShowPersonVisual
$player.ethosMercy++
AdjustRepPerson armaa_felner COOPERATIVE 5
$global.armaa_btsTransportingFlint = true
AddTextSmall "Agreed to smuggle Felner out" highlight
text:
Felner doesn't smile. But there's something like relief in the way his shoulders lower. "That's fair. Cleaner than I expected." For a moment, the tiredness shows - under the eyes, in the joints. "This place was always temporary. I just didn't know how short until now."

His voice drops slightly. "They're still watching, of course. House arrest by another name." He glances toward the security cams - or where they might be. "And the moment you walked into those doors, someone got a notification."

He meets your eyes. Not quite an appeal. More like a professional laying out terms. "If you can arrange an exit window - ship, shuttle, even just the gap - I can handle the rest. But understand this: we won't be halfway to orbit before somebody decides they want that chip… or my body."

script:
EndConversation
ShowDefaultVisual
$global.armaa_btsDealtWithFlint = true
Call $global.armaa_bts_ref updateData
FireAll PopulateOptions
text:
The door shuts behind you with a soft hiss. You pause at the bottom of the stairs. No alarm sounds, No patrols scramble. If anyone was watching, they've chosen not to interfere. For now.

Whatever this was-closure, betrayal, duty-it's done. The chip feels heavier in your pocket than it should. Or maybe it's just your conscience shifting weight. Ahead, the shuttle awaits.

You draw fast. The shot cracks like a snapped bone in a silent room. Felner jerks back with a grunt, glass shattering in his hand as he slumps sideways over the table. One clean shot, center mass. 
He looks at you once before the light leaves his eyes.

You don't hesitate. Thumb the pin, roll it beneath the table. Felner's eyes go wide-he moves, but not fast enough. The blast flares, brief and brutal. When the dust settles, the booth is smoke and red mist.

Your hand moves beneath the table. The blade flashes upward- quick, close, silent. To Felner's credit, he reacts quickly - just not quickly enough.

One thrust beneath the ribs, a sharp twist. He gasps once, soundless, then folds forward, breath and life leaving together.

You flick the nozzle and fire. The mist hisses across the table. Felner coughs once, then again-choking, clutching at his throat as the chemical burns eat through cloth and skin.
He reaches for his drink with trembling fingers, then collapses against the booth, twitching.

In one smooth motion, you flick the false fingertip open and swing the wire in a clean arc across the table. The monomolecular filament passes through Felner's neck without resistance. He stiffens, eyes wide for a moment, then slumps forward as a thin line of blood spills across the table. The filament retracts with a faint click. The room falls still.

You surge forward, bracing against the table as your boot drives upward into Felner's chest. The impact lifts him half out of the booth, breath knocked from his lungs in a sharp, strangled gasp. His arms flail, but the second strike finds his head- a sharp, driving kick that snaps his neck back with a dull crack. Felner shudders once, legs folding beneath him as his body crumples into the corner of the booth.

A thin line of blood spills from his mouth, pooling silently beneath the table.

The blade clears its scabbard with a whisper of steel. Felner barely registers the motion before your strike lands - diagonal, clean, final. He slumps back, jacket parting where the blade severed breath and bone.

A crimson line blooms across his chest as his body settles.

text:
"It has come to our attention that one Adam Felner, a former systems advisor currently under internal review, experienced an... unfortunate accident. And, curiously, this coincided with your unscheduled appearance at a restricted installation." The smile doesn't falter. The temperature drops. "While Tri-Tachyon recognizes the complexities of frontier diplomacy, unauthorized interference with corporate quarantine protocols constitutes a gross violation of interjurisdictional agreements."

"In short: we're here to reclaim any and all materials, devices, or data fragments which may have-intentionally or otherwise-fallen into your custody. Prompt compliance ensures this remains a procedural audit. Noncompliance escalates to asset recovery." $HisOrHer tone doesn't change, but the subtext is clear: you are the asset.

text:
"We've identified a potential security breach with one Adam Felner-a former employee presently undergoing a long-term remediation placement. As you have been flagged as being within his proximity potentially have engaged in undocumented exchange, we are obligated to initiate containment procedures." The smile sharpens-barely.

"Mr. Felner is not, and has never been, authorized to engage in independent disclosure. Any such arrangements made are considered null, void, and-most critically-reputationally hazardous under Clause 17-B of his non-disclosure agreement. Accordingly, we will be retrieving Mr. Felner, and securing any residual data or anomalous knowledge he may have distributed-knowingly or otherwise."

"To ensure this proceeds in accordance with Tri-Tachyon's internal review protocols, we kindly request that you power down all weapons systems and permit a boarding team to conduct a localized search and retrieval operation for proprietary materials."

text:
Calling on your understanding of Tri-Tachyon's internal policy labyrinth, you launch into a rapid-fire citation of obscure directives, subclauses, and cross-jurisdictional exemptions - an avalanche of corporate legalese delivered with just enough certainty to make it dangerous to argue. You reference standing commission rights, discretionary access to post-remediation assets, and the rather serious consequences of initiating an unauthorized kinetic interdiction against credentialed internal affiliates without audit-layer clearance and a logged compliance ticket.

The officer's smug professionalism falters. Their eyes flick from you to something off-screen, pulling up a protocol index or running your claim through whatever passes for due diligence. They don't need to believe you. They just need to believe there's a non-zero chance you're right - and that being wrong would get their name stapled to an incident report.

After a tense silence, the channel crackles with a terse acknowledgment and a vague promise of "internal follow-up." The intercept fleet adjusts course without another word, leaving your path clear...

...for now.

text:
You don't see Felner's expression as he's transferred to the external recovery cradle, nor hear whatever final words he might have offered. A brief pause follows before the Tri-Tachyon officer returns to the channel, voice smooth, almost conversational now. "Retrieval confirmed. Subject secured." A short exhale, the kind someone makes after a problem resolves itself.

"You've saved us both a considerable amount of paperwork, Captain- and spared my department several difficult internal memos." A brief pause, as if weighing whether to say more.
"For what it's worth: professional discretion is still appreciated. The Sector turns smoother when everyone understands where not to look." The officer's tone sharpens slightly, returning to formality.

"This incident will be recorded as satisfactorily resolved under Clause 17-B. You are clear to depart." Then, almost genuinely: "Burn bright out there, Captain."
The channel cuts. The Tri-Tachyon vessels pivot cleanly away, formation dispersing as they begin their return burn.

text:
You approach $name and consider the outstanding issue of relocating Felner. This would not be an inappropriate place for him to start a new life.

Felner steps onto the deck beside you, duffel slung over one shoulder, coat pulled tight against the thin, scrubbed air. For a moment, he pauses, as if weighing whether to say something more. "Wasn't sure I'd make it this far," he says at last. "Figured you might vent me and call it done." His tone carries no blame, no relief - just the flat edge of someone long familiar with how things turn in the Sector.

He meets your eyes, gives a small nod. "Appreciate the ride." his face goes slack for a moment, likely interfacing with some implant before your TriPad pings.

text: You see him off on a shuttle.

Felner pauses, glancing out at the shuttle bay's flickering traffic beacons. "Same thing as anyone, I guess," he says. "Find a place. Keep moving if I can't."
A faint breath - not quite a laugh. "Corporate life's not so bad, once you stop worrying about morality. All comes down to who's paying, and how long they need you before someone higher up starts asking questions." He adjusts the strap on his duffel.

"Plenty of firms out there. Someone'll have a desk for me." He gives you a final nod, then disappears into the shuttle.

text:
"You ever hear of the Askonian Revolutionary Council?"

"They're the ones the Diktat calls traitors. Terrorists, if you read the right briefings. In truth? Depends who you ask. Exiles, mostly. Officers, idealists, dockworkers, pirates. People who saw what Andrada was building and stepped off before the machine finished closing around them."

"We've worked with them. Shared intel, spare parts, secondhand ships without serials. Nothing permanent. Nothing public. But that's changing."

"The ARC's pushing for more," $personName says, setting the datapad down without looking at it. "Up until now, it's been backchannel work. Now they want something visible, something that makes the right people nervous."

"Site's called Cradle Five. Diktat black archive buried in orbital drift east of Volturn - not on any public charts, off-books even by Sindrian standards." $HeOrShe taps the datapad. A grainy orbital still flickers into view- blurred decks, defense patrols, a cluster of heat signatures.

"ARC wants a clean breach; their team leads the entry and ours handles fleet support. You're bridging the gap."

"We signed off, and, well.. not all of us like it. Some say the ARC's all theater. But I need results. That's why it's you going."

script:

text:
"Cradle Five... yeah. It's not really a prison. Not exactly. It's-uh, it's a cold archive. Stuff the Diktat didn't want to erase, but definitely didn't want found. Old intel assets, failed projects, people who... stopped being politically useful. Y'know.. Officers who asked the wrong questions. Scientists tied to failed projects- the kind you don't get a trial for, just... reassignment. People who got too close to Andrada's early power plays. Media workers, engineers, old guard idealists. Anyone whose presence doesn't fit the version of history they're selling this cycle."

$HeOrShe glances at you, watching for your reaction, then continues.

"The ARC wants names. Proof. They say there's a, uh, might be a manifest in there. Maybe people who were disappeared for saying or doing the wrong thing. People Andrada swore never existed. MRC... we're mostly after the gear. Ships, weapons, data, maybe an AI core or two if we get lucky."

"Truth is, no one really knows what's in there. Which means if we don't get it first, someone else will. And if it's bad enough to hide this long, it's probably worth the trouble."
options: 10:armaa_rtc5: "'Might be'? We're risking our lives for a maybe and a list?"

text:
$personName gives a short, humorless huff. Might be a laugh, or just air catching in his throat. "Yeah. Sounds about right when you put it like that."

$HeOrShe leans forward again, elbows on the table.

"But look-it's not just paper we're chasing. If those, those logs exist, they're not just records. They're leverage."

"And if they don't? Then we still hit a Diktat asset. We rattle the cage. Show the ARC we're not just talk. Show the Sector that Andrada's not untouchable."

10:armaa_rtc6: "Alright, i'm in."

text:
$PersonName nods once, slow and final. Like a decision's been made, but not necessarily a comfortable one. "Good. Then head for Umbra." 

"ARC command's got a liaison waiting there- a, uh, Miss Kato?"

"Don't posture, don't bluff. These people didn't survive the Diktat by being polite, and they didn't call us in because they trust easy. You're there because you're deniable. And because you've got the only mech in this sector that can walk into hell and maybe walk back out."

A pause. Then: "You'll get your briefing on-site. Don't be late."

options: 10:armaa_rtcImo1: "Imoinu, i'm here for the operation. MRC sent me."

text:
"Right, the mission. We think there are still detainees inside. Cryo-stored, maybe. Scientists, officers, anyone who got too close to the wrong truth or didn't clap loud enough when the cameras were rolling." She taps a knuckle lightly against the side of her console.

"We're not just there to crack drives and lift logs. If anyone's still breathing, we bring them out. This isn't just about hurting the Diktat- it's about pulling people out of the hole they were thrown into."

"Not everyone in the ARC sees it that way. Some of them are in this for the credits, or the chaos, or because it gives them someone to shoot. That's what happens when you build a revolution out of scraps."

Her tone settles again- low, serious.

"But I'm not losing anyone on this run to make a point. You're there to get us close, keep the way open, and make sure our people get out if things turn bad. And they might."

She gives you a flat look. "You understand what you're walking into?"
options: 10:armaa_rtcImo2: "You really think this will change anything?"

text:
Imoinu stares at you for a moment, like she's trying to figure out if you're being cynical or just honest. "Well....no," she says. "Not by itself."

"This op won't bring down the Diktat. It won't free Volturn, or topple Anrada, or wake up the poor bastards sleepwalking through Sindria's broadcasts."

"But if there's a survivor in there? Someone they locked away and tried to erase? And we pull them out into the light where they can talk- where they can be seen?" 

"Yeah. I think that's worth doing. Even if it doesn't fix everything. Especially because it won't."

She exhales- not sharp, not annoyed. Just tired. "Honestly? I don't know anymore."

"Every op we run, it feels like the goalposts shift. We bleed to take a relay, they just spin up another. We dig up proof, and the people we're trying to reach just... choose not to believe it." She shakes her head, once.

"But then we get someone out. Some half-forgotten officer, or a researcher who vanished mid-cycle, and they remember everything. Names. Orders. What went wrong and who gave the go-ahead. They wake up and look around and realize the Diktat hasn't won. Well, not completely." Her voice steadies.

"That's what keeps me going. Not toppling regimes or torching archives- it's the moment someone walks out of a cell and knows they weren't the crazy one. That it wasn't all in their head. That someone came for them."

A faint, grim smile. Not performative. Just real. "So yeah. I think this matters. Not in the history-book way. But in the way that counts."

"We'll be coming in from system edge. Quiet as we can. Your fleet jumps first- you're faster, better equipped. You clear the approach and... you know, make sure it's not crawling with patrols." She pauses, then adds, a little more cautious: "I don't do fleet maneuvers. I trust you know how to handle whatever's parked out there."

The Cradle flashes faintly on the display- three suggested burn-in paths ripple out from it. She squints at them, then points toward one marked shadow-side. "Once we're in range, we'll transfer a boarding team to one of your ships. Our people. ARC marines, couple techs, medic if we've got one not already bleeding out somewhere." She glances back at you.

"They'll handle the breach. You hold orbit, watch for company. If the Diktat shows up, we're going to need you to keep the door open." A moment passes.

"You're the one making the call out there. If it's clean, we go fast. If it's a setup, we cut and run. But if there's anyone alive in that station..." Her voice softens - not dramatically, just enough to remind you she still believes in something under all the armor.

"...then you do what you have to, and you get them home."
options: 10:armaa_rtcImo5: "How do you know this intel is good?"

text:
"It came through one of our contacts in the Diktat logistics chain. Kept copies of things he wasn't supposed to- schedules, decommission orders, site manifests. Probably planning to cash out, sell the package to the Hegemony or whoever bit first."

"He didn't get the chance. ARC intercepted the handoff before he made it off-station. Lucky break, or stupid luck. Hard to say."

"We verified what we could. It lines up. Enough that we're not walking in blind."

She gives you a look- not defensive, but plain. "You want a clean source, you're in the wrong war. The intel's solid. That's what matters."

Imoinu doesn't answer right away. Her jaw tightens just slightly as she looks past the screen- like she's already thought through the worst-case scenario a dozen times. "Then we deal with it." Her voice is steady. Tired, maybe, but not uncertain. "The Diktat's always setting traps. If we waited for perfect certainty, we'd still be sitting on Volturn passing out leaflets and pretending that was going to change anything." She leans in, eyes narrowing just a little.

"Yeah, it could be a setup. Could be we jump in and they've got a task group waiting to vaporize us. But if we don't move, and there are people in that station.. if they get purged while we're still second-guessing intel?"

She shakes her head. "I'd rather walk into a trap than leave them to die wondering if anyone even remembered they existed."
options: 10:armaa_rtcImo7: "What kind of support can I expect?"

text:
Imoinu lets out a short breath that might be a laugh- or a sigh dressed up to look stronger than it feels. "Support?"

"We've got three captains manning ships that are willing to help. Two of them are held together with stolen plating and bad welds. The third's a Valkyrie with a reactor leak we can't fix because the guy who knew how to do it died in Galatia half a cycle ago."

"That's what I've got. A marine squad that hasn't eaten properly in weeks, one tech team, and a whole lot of people who believe in something anyway." She gives you a look; not pleading, but frank.

"You're the fleet. You hold the line, keep us breathing long enough to get in and back out."

"This wasn't supposed to be your fight, I know. But if you're in it with us... then we'll do what we can to hold up our end."

Cradle Five - a compact, slab-sided orbital shell anchored to a slow-rolling ice moon. Exterior hull is matte-black composite, heat-baffled, half-swallowed by rock. A weak power signature and a dozen sensor ghosts that flicker and vanish on repeat.

Scans show evidence of recent activity: scorched docking collars, hull scarring consistent with thruster burns, residual EM from shuttle launches. Someone's been here.

Your comms array logs faint signal bleed on old Diktat frequencies - nothing directed, just ambient leakage. Enough to suggest something inside is still awake.

ARC boarding teams prep for insertion, running final checks. Your fleet maintains a holding pattern - slow drift, weapons cold, sensors tight.
options: 10:armaa_rtcImo1: "Imoinu, i'm here for the operation. MRC sent me."

text:
The comm line clicks open with the sharp hiss of re-pressurization, followed by a ragged exhale. "We're off-station. Dock clamps clear. No pursuit. We have the payload."

There's a weight in the marine's voice that wasn't there before. You hear the dull clang of boots on a deckplate, muted voices behind him. One murmurs something- faint, like a prayer, or a warning. "It's worse than we thought."

On the bridge, Ops doesn't move for several seconds. Then: "Survivors?"

"No bodies," the ARC tech replies. "But not empty, either.  The logs are fragmented... but there's enough to see what they were doing."

A second voice filters through- one of the techs, his voice distant and clipped, like he's still looking over his shoulder.

"They weren't just studying behavior. They were... reshaping it. Running memory loops until the subject broke- and then rebooting the failure. Over and over."

Ops lets the silence hold, until it threatens to spill over. Then, quietly: "Roger that. Secure the drive. Scrub everything else. You know the protocol."

Another pause. Just long enough to feel the shape of a decision not yet made.

You'd best return to Umbra. Debrief's waiting.

text:
"Sounds familiar, actually. Before the AI ban, we had a few employees..ah." She pauses, turning a stylus over in her fingers. "Or should I call them strategic constructs? Anyway, they were charged with monitoring and administrating some aspects of the company. Their domain was big-picture stability."

"When the First AI War broke out, Meshan turned into a battlefield. Recovery wasn't an option for some."
"If you're worried about it being some kind of superintelligence bent on the destruction of humanity, don't be. They're just...very cautious, and very analytical. And you? You're probably a variable they haven't solved yet."

"If you ever happen to get a hold of it physically, you should, of course, turn it into the Hegemony as established by the conventions of the ban, buuuut?" The stylus stops turning. "If it happened to end up here, well.. I wouldn't mind too much."

"From what you describe, it sounds like a remnant of one of Arma Armatura's old artifical intelligences. Their directive would have centered around societal modeling and other administrative purposes. Entities like that don't do well when left alone for extended periods. They tend to..." A slight pause. "..get creative."

"You're something they didn't predict. That tends to make an entity whose entire purpose revolved around predicting.. uncomfortable. Though that isn't exactly the right word for it."

She picks up the stylus and returns her attention to whatever she was doing before you asked.

"As far as I know, they went through rigorous behavioral tests to ensure they weren't malicious. I'd be careful either way."

options: 0:armaa_imeldaIKOptResp:Tell Imelda about your encounter with IRON KING

text:
When you speak of your encounter, she flinches. Not enough for most to notice. She turns, the light catching the sharp angle of her expression. "You spoke to it?" You nod. You didn't have a choice. It-

"No. No!" she cuts in, not harsh, but firm. "There's always a choice." She begins pacing, a slow, deliberate circuit near the altar, hands clasped behind her back.

"You've shown judgment. Temperance. But this..." She stops, facing you fully now. "That thing is a relic of the age that tried to rewrite Creation. You know what Ludd teaches. The Collapse was a divine mercy for what humanity had become. These intelligences, these machines... they were the architects of our downfall." You start to reply, but she lifts a hand to stop you.

"I know you wouldn't have done this lightly. I know you. And maybe that's why this troubles me most." She draws in a long breath, voice lowering.

"You must understand - it is not that I fear the machine. It's that I know it. Not in name, not in detail, but in nature. They always speak with reason. With purpose. They offer what they can to tempt you. But what they want is your will. To mold it. Replace it. Reduce you until you are just another function in their grand design." Her eyes soften slightly - not in approval, but in sorrow.

"I trust you believed it was necessary. I trust you saw something I do not. But hear this. This Iron King is a silver-tongued serpent in a garden full of innocents with an orchard of apples at his disposal." She steps closer now, voice quiet, nearly a whisper. "If it speaks again, remember who you are. And who we are. We were not made to be ruled by circuits and hollow kings." Then, finally, her gaze lifts to yours again. "I will pray for you," she says, "But tread carefully. Even the most vigilant can lose their way in the dark."

She rises slowly and stares at you with a look that is no longer soft or understanding. It is the look of someone who sees rot blooming beneath the skin. "You... spoke with it?" Her voice is tight, low, like a wire drawn to snapping. "It communicated with you?"

You nod. You tell her it seemed to be an old AI that said it was once part of the system's defense network. Maybe more. Imelda's hands are clenched now, fists tight at her sides. She takes a step back, as if proximity to you might be dangerous.

"And you -listened-?" You start to answer, but she cuts you off, her voice rising in pitch and conviction.

"There are names we don't speak of lightly. Moloch. Mammon. The Blasphemers that built the crucible from which the Collapse was forged. You think the man simply fell into ruin? No! We were cast down. Burned out of the sky by the flame of righteous judgment because of these demons, and still their ghosts cling to the Sector like carrion."

She steps forward now, face contorted by rage, anger giving her movement.

"I've read the encoded catechisms. The sealed sermons. This so called 'king' is a heresy given voice, a, a demon. These things - they promise order, stability, fortune. But what they bring is the demise of mankind. Always they seek it." She stops just short of the commsplate, breath sharp and deliberate.

"It watched over Jenius? It influenced people? That is possession by another name. The kind that wears a smiling mask and whispers temptation into weak hearts." You try to interject, to explain it asked questions more than it made demands, but Imelda cuts you off again, her voice now cold and solemn. "You've stared into the eyes of a false god. Whatever it told you, whatever lies it sowed - you are tainted now. And I will pray for you."

options: 0:armaa_kadeIKOptResp:Tell Kade about your encounter with IRON KING

text:
The reaction is instant. Whatever trace of humor lingered in his eyes vanishes. He sets his glass down with a quiet tap and looks at you with an expression you haven't seen on him before - not anger exactly, but something older and more careful.

"What did it say?" You offer a measured summary. Not everything - but enough. Kade listens without moving. When you finish, the silence runs longer than it should.

"I always knew something down there was running its own game," he says finally. "I just didn't think it'd move this fast."

He stands. Doesn't pace - just stands, which is somehow worse.

"That thing is a relic." His voice has lost the drawl entirely. "It looks at people who take power instead of waiting for it to be handed down and sees a problem that needs solving. And now it's decided you're worth talking to. You understand what that means. It's assessing you."

Something shifts behind his eyes - calculation, maybe, or the decision not to say something he was about to say.

"Don't give it anything else."

A soft, humorless breath escapes her; almost a laugh, but not quite.

"There will be no next," she says. "Only what must be done."

She turns back to you, and the courtesy she carried before-the measured tone, the careful deference-has fallen away. What replaces it is discipline. Tempered steel beneath vestments.

"These relics attempting to claw their way into a world that has already outlived them?" Her mouth tightens. "The Church would convene, debate doctrine. Issue placations to the faithful. I will not."

"Next?"

She repeats the word like it doesn't fit in her mouth. "I suppose that depends on whether I believe you." 

She turns to face you. "You said it spoke, That it asked questions. That it called itself a 'king'."

She paces slowly, arms folded behind her back in that rigid, self-contained way you've come to recognize; a shield against doubt. "The prophets wrote of trials. Not in the language we use now, but the meaning's the same. A voice rising from the ashes of our arrogance."

"This isn't just another lost machine. This is Moloch, dressed in steel and hubris, clawing its way back into Creation." She faces you fully now.

"So what's next? Next, I prepare."

She looks at you with something new in her eyes. "He spoke to you."

"The IRON KING- that was its name. That was the lie it wore." She steps forward, voice rising like a sermon barely restrained.

"A pretender-king, forged in the furnace of the Collapse. Wreathed in memory, drunk on dominion. A remnant of the Domain, not just surviving- plotting."

She stops, standing tall, gaze locked on yours; not searching, but recognizing. "Ludd sends trials. But He also sends weapons."
"You've been tempered, commander. Whether you know it or not." Her eyes burn with clarity now, not madness - not exactly - but purpose.

She reaches up and touches something etched onto her commsplate, her fingers briefly lingering there. "What's next for me?"

"Next, I gather the faithful. I name the enemy. and I prepare the fires."

"Look... ain't lookin' for trouble," they say, voice low and even. "Your transponder checks out-MRC-favored. Didn't know we were brushin' up against one of them." They glance off-screen, probably at a second monitor full of threat assessments and retreat vectors.

"No point getting scorched over a misunderstanding. You're clear to go. We'll keep to our lane." The pirate offers a small, reluctant nod of respect.

The transmission ends, and the pirate fleet promptly begins to attempt breaking formation, aiming to steer a wide berth around your $shipOrFleet.

"Greetings, insignificant whelp! I am the great and feared $name, commander of this vessel! Prepare to be boarded, for your goods now belong to me!"

The bridge crew look to each other, confused. There's no crew on the pirate ship, no boarding shuttles, and no visible means for the AI to actually carry out its threat.

"UNREGISTERED VESSEL. TRANSMIT COLONIAL AUTHORITY CREDENTIALS OR VACATE RESTRICTED AIRSPACE. NON-COMPLIANCE WILL BE MET WITH APPROPRIATE RESPONSE."

Still waiting for paperwork from a dead empire.

"UNREGISTERED VESSEL. QUERY: ARE YOU LOST, OR LOOKING FOR SOMETHING?"


The response doesn't match any known Domain comm protocol. The syntax is correct but the phrasing is... conversational. Before anyone can respond, the channel closes.
"...TRANSMIT COLO- ...TRANSMIT- ...YOU ARE OPERATING IN RESTRICTED-"

"Greetings, insignificant whelp! I am the great and feared $name, commander of this vessel! Prepare to be boarded, for your goods now belong to me!"

The bridge crew look to each other, confused. There's no crew on the pirate ship, no boarding shuttles, and no visible means for the AI to actually carry out its threat.
"IFF CONFIRMED. THIS SYSTEM IS NOW OPEN TO CIVILIAN AND COMMERCIAL TRAFFIC. THE PLANET JENIUS IS ACCEPTING VISITORS, TRADERS, AND SETTLERS. NO CREDENTIALS REQUIRED."

"UNREGISTERED VESSEL. QUERY: ARE YOU LOST, OR LOOKING FOR SOMETHING?"

The response doesn't match any known Domain comm protocol. The syntax is correct but the phrasing is... conversational. Before anyone can respond, the channel closes.
"...TRANSMIT COLO- ...TRANSMIT- ...THIS SYSTEM IS NOW OPEN TO CIVIL-"

"Greetings, insignificant whelp! I am the great and feared $name, commander of this vessel-" The channel cuts abruptly. A different voice, briefly:

"Ignore that." Then silence.

text: "Unidentified vessel, this is $Post $name of the Knights of Ludd. A word before you go about your business?" $heOrShe doesn't wait for a response. "Consider it a courtesy."

options: armaa_KoLDeflectResp:"I'm just passing through. I don't have time for this."

text:
"Appreciated. I'll be direct with you. We've been observing this system for some time now. Our mission is primarily to the safety of the flock herein, but the local government has proven... somewhat uncooperative. Which means, frankly, that you're of the minority we've had the opportunity to speak with in some shifts."

"We know someone is moving on this system. We don't know who yet, or what their play is. Ships don't come through Gamlin without a reason. So, what's yours?"
options:
armaa_KoLComply2Resp:"I'm just passing through."
armaa_KoLSupplies2Resp:"I'm looking to resupply."
armaa_KoLCuriousity2Resp:"I don't know. Exploring, I guess."

text:
$HeOrShe pauses.

"We'll be brief. There's something you should know about Jenius before you get any closer to it."
"I won't press you on the details. A $manOrWoman's work ought to be their own business, and you're not obligated to share it with me. But I'll ask you to hear me out before you go about it."

"Fair enough. New Meshan's a decent waystation if you don't mind it's debauchery." $heOrShe says it without warmth.

"Before you make port, there's something you should know about this system. Won't take long. Consider it a toll for the road."

$heOrShe almost smiles.

"Talk with me a moment. What you've heard and what's actually out there are likely two different things."

$heOrShe almost smiles.

"Talk with me a moment. What you've heard and what's actually out there are likely two different things."

"I'll speak plainly. This system is rife with the taint of Moloch. You have the data for Jenius on your charts, yes?  The unholy relics of the Domain still patrol their skies. The local population they leave unmolested - the Creator's mercy working through strange instruments, perhaps."

"Anything else that enters their range is another matter. We lost a missionary vessel some cycles past. Good people, faithful people, gone before they could speak a single prayer.  Ludd holds them now. I take comfort in that, when I allow myself to."
options:
armaa_KoLInfoDumpWhyNotDestroyedResp:"Why hasn't the Church acted?"
armaa_KoLInfoDumpObstructionsResp:"You mentioned the local government was uncooperative."
armaa_KoLInfoDumpEndResp:"I'll keep this in mind. (End)"

text:
"The Excubitor Orbis, in his wisdom, has determined that 'patience' is the proper response to an abomination that has been running unchecked for two hundred years." A look of contempt washes over $hisOrHer face, but the $post almost immediately self corrects.

"I do not say that with disrespect. We must move carefully, and for good reason.  We are not the Path, praise Ludd for that distinction. But I will tell you what I have told my novices: there is no diplomatic solution with a machine. You cannot appeal to its conscience. You cannot offer it redemption. It does not have a soul to save."

"So I watch and I pray. And I trust that Ludd illuminates the path when the time is right. That is my post. That is my duty. I do not resent it."
options:
armaa_KoLInfoDumpObstructionsResp:"You mentioned the local government was uncooperative."
armaa_KoLInfoDumpEndResp:"I'll keep this in mind. (End)"

text:
"His Majesty's office has sent blessings twice." $heOrShe sighs, "Yet every request we have filed - survey records, historical data on the Jenius system - has been received, acknowledged, and not answered. The king appears a faithful man. I do not doubt that. But in my experience, captain, courteous silence and outright obstruction look very much alike from the outside."

$HeOrShe glances toward New Meshan on the display.

"Who benefits from the Church remaining ignorant of what orbits Jenius? That is a question I have asked myself often enough. I will not accuse without evidence, as that is not the way of Ludd. But I will say that we have been kept at arm's length, and I do not think it is the king who is doing the keeping."

"The faithful here on New Meshan, at least, welcome our presence. That much the local government cannot easily deny us."
options:
armaa_KoLInfoDumpWhyNotDestroyedResp:"Why hasn't the Church acted?"
armaa_KoLInfoDumpEndResp:"I'll keep this in mind. (End)"

text:
$heOrShe nods once.

"Blessings be. If you learn anything of consequence - you know where to find us."

"We've been busy these past cycles, studying the Valken III's trial data and cross-referencing it with the data from ATAC." Her gaze returns to you, sharper now. "And naturally, we applied it where it mattered most."

She folds her arms, the corner of her mouth lifting in that familiar, knowing way. "I've spent some time going through your Valkazard's systems since you turned it over. It's... fascinating- and a little troubling. Several of its subsystems were behaving erratically. At first, I thought it was damage from overuse."

Her tone cools, more deliberate now. 
"But after cross-referencing the architecture with surviving records from the First AI War, I started to suspect something else. The Valkazard you recovered isn't a complete unit. Parts of its original configuration were... stripped, or lost. Maybe during the war, maybe long before it."

options: armaa_vzardGift1b:"Busy improving another war machine, I take it?"

text: blahblahblahblah valkazard exposition
options: armaa_vzardGift2:"And what have you managed to acheive?"

text:
"Fortunately, Arma Armatura doesn't let sleeping machines lie. My engineers managed to recover several corrupted archives buried deep in the system's auxiliary banks. With some work, we've restored a great deal of its lost functionality, and while we were in there, made a few improvements of our own."

"I don't have much need for it anymore, though. We'll integrate what we learned into the production model Valken III's, so...you can have it back. Actually, i've already arranged to have it delivered to your fleet."
options: armaa_vzardGift2:"And what have you managed to acheive?"

text:
$personName's confidence radiated as she revealed her achievement. "We succeeded in partially recreating SILVERSWORD," she declared, her voice tinged with pride. "Not only that, we've already integrated a simplified version into a select few units for thorough testing. And of course, I wouldn't pass up the opportunity to offer you a unit from the very first batch."

"Rest assured, we've conducted extensive tests," she says, "It's perfectly stable. The results have been... spectacular."

"Consider this a token of appreciation for our partnership and your extraordinary skills," she says. 

"A lance of pilots with adequate expertise combined with this new system should be a formidable force on the battlefield. And that, my dear, is a win-win for both of us." 

You can't help but wonder if there's more to this seemingly generous gesture, knowing how corporate dealings usually work.

text:

A file packet pings onto your display. 

"Gu Lingfeng. If we're a spine, he is one of the vertebrae. Diktat Intelligence grabbed him three weeks ago and disappeared him into a mobile labor camp on the planet. You will get him out, alive, and you will not let them know who sent you."

$HeOrShe leans in slightly.

"Bring him back, and the arguments about you on Exsedol get quieter."

options: armaa_reicOptWhatFallbackResp:"If I can't get him out, what's the fallback?"
armaa_reicOptWhatLaborCampResp:"What is a 'mobile labor camp' in practice?"
armaa_reicOptAskingAlotResp:"You're asking for a lot, considering you barely trust me."
armaa_reicOptMercenaryResp:"I don't work for free. So what is my compensation?"

text: "Lingfeng knows who's real on Cruor and who's bought. Keeps routes quiet, people fed, names off paper. You can replace muscle. You can't replace that."

"I expects total success, yet i've buried enough good plans to know better. Primary objective is Gu breathing on your deck. If that becomes impossible, confirm where he was held and pull anything tied to him. If you can't take it, burn it."

"And if you see a window to make the outcome quick and expensive for INTSEC - without leaving them a trail back to us - you take it."
options:
armaa_reicOptWhatFallbackRespB: "Is it really Lingfeng that matters here, or what he knows?"
armaa_reicOptWhatFallbackRespC:"Understood. Extraction first. Containment if compromised."
armaa_reicOptWhatFallbackRespD:"You're telling me to put down your own ally."

text: "He matters. And what he knows matters because it keeps other people alive."
"Good. Glad we're clear on that."

$HeOrShe doesn't flinch.

"I'm telling you not to hand INTSEC a living codex. Lingfeng would tell you the same, if he's still himself. We don't abandon people. We deny the enemy leverage."

"They're...'work details' on $armaa_reic_marketName that disappear into the badlands with tracked rigs. Diktat Intelligence runs it like logistics - bodies in, ore out, questions buried between. When they relocate the train, the prison relocates with it."

"I am. Because I don't trust you yet. If this goes loud, it can't come back to us. You're the hand outside the glove. Bring Lingfeng back, and you stop being a disposable piece on the board and start being an asset we're willing to invest in."
"Fair. Retainer now, balance on delivery - plus fuel and supplies to cover the run. Bring Lingfeng back clean, and you get clearance on Exsedol. That's worth more than a bonus out here."

"So you'll have to find his location the old fashioned way. Bar talk, A crooked officer with debts. Whatever you can pry loose without putting your name on it."

"...And Captain? Keep your heat down."

text:
"Worker camp logistics on Cruor runs through consolidated routing. You don't contract directly with the camps - you contract with the segment coordinators, who slot you into the rotation when there's a coverage gap... there are always coverage gaps."

$HeOrShe studies you for a moment.

"What are you actually moving?"

text:

"I can slot you in for standard resupply runs - fuel, provisions, that kind of thing. You'll get the schedule and the segment tags when you're confirmed in the system."
$HeOrShe looks back at you. 

"Don't deviate from the assigned routing and we won't have a problem." $HeOrShe returns to whatever $heOrShe was doing. The conversation is functionally over - you're in the rotation, but you learned nothing useful.

text:
The $post looks you over with slightly more interest than they might have otherwise. "That I can work with." $HeOrShe pulls up something offscreen, scrolling briefly.

"There's a detail that keeps coming up short on coverage. Restricted routing, so the label's sanitation and medical resupply. Moves on a tight window and it requires discretion."
$HeOrShe looks back at you, not quite registering what they just said.
"If your operation can hit a resupply schedule without deviating, I can get you slotted. Tight window though - you'd need to be in position before the next rotation."

The $post's expression doesn't change. But something behind it does.

"Quietly. I'm going to pretend you said 'efficiently.' Because if you said what I think you said, this conversation is already over."

$HeOrShe picks back up what $heOrShe was doing.

"Standard goods or nothing. Your call."

"That's a serious name to throw around, Captain."

$HeOrShe exhales through the nose.

"If you are joking, stop. Cruor doesn't forgive people who play games with that office."

There's only silence as $person.firstName's face drains of color.

"…Understood."

$HeOrShe doesn't ask for details. Just moves.

"I'm not going to be the reason his attention turns toward my office. I can't discuss Internal matters on an unsecured channel, but I can give you port-side logistics: the requisition alias currently attached to the restricted work detail, the segment tag it's riding under, and the next resupply window."

A quick swallow.

"Ah...If there's anything else you need from my side to keep this moving? I'm happy to help." $HeOrShe smiles weakly.

There's only silence as $person.firstName's face drains of color.

"…Understood."

$HeOrShe doesn't ask for details. Just moves.

"I'm not going to be the reason his attention turns toward my office. I can't discuss Internal matters on an unsecured channel, but I can give you port-side logistics: the requisition alias currently attached to the restricted work detail, the segment tag it's riding under, and the next resupply window."

A quick swallow.

"Ah...If there's anything else you need from my side to keep this moving? I'm happy to help." $HeOrShe smiles weakly.

"You?" $HeOrShe spits increduously, "Sent by Macario?"

He pauses before pausing heartily, then moves to cut the comm.

The $post looks up properly for the first time.

"Hah. Efficient is one word for it. Cruor chews through everything.  If we didn't keep the trains moving the whole place drowns in its own mess."
$HeOrShe gestures offscreen, indicating the city-train below.

"Every segment competing for the same lifts, the same rail carts, the same storage volume. Work details get slotted into that like anything else - they're just cargo with complaints."

"Anyway. Was there something specific you needed, or were you just making conversation?"

The $post looks you over as if assessing a pallet of cargo. "Openings? Always. This place runs on shortages."

A short exhale - tired, but not unfriendly. $HeOrShe taps something off-screen.

"There's a run that keeps getting ‘priority re-routed' under boring labels - sanitation, medical resupply, that kind of thing. It's always tight on escort coverage and it always wants discretion."

"If you want in,  perhaps I can arrange something."
"Hah-hah. Now why would I tell you that? Do not waste my time." The $post smiles thinly.

"Stop- stop. Don't."

"Listen to me. You do that, and you won't just kill people-you'll wake up the whole apparatus. The Navy, INTSEC, everyone."  $HeOrShe swallows.

"Captain."

$HeOrShe tilts $hisOrHer head upward. "And you're not alone out there." Their mouth tightens into something like satisfaction.

text:
A portable holo-table hums between you, projecting a rough schematic of Cruor's transit network. Your tactical officer leans over it with the focused expression of someone who doesn't like what they're looking at.

"Mobile unit. That's the complication." She taps a moving marker. "We can project its position based on the resupply schedule Felix gave us, but the window is narrow. Maybe forty minutes between when it parks for the shift-change handoff and when it moves again."

Felix sits across the table with his arms folded, watching the schematic. "The unit parks ...here. We've worked that junction. Gira still has a maintenance badge that'll scan clean for another..." He glances at her.

Gira checks her wrist. "Thirty-six hours."

"Thirty-six hours," he repeats. "So we're not waiting on the next window."

Your tactical officer straightens. "One person goes in. Small footprint, maintenance cover, in and out before the handoff completes." She looks at you. "The work order Felix is flagging you under allows access to the depot's secondary logistics terminal. If Lingfeng's moving through this network, his paperwork is in there."

"Any questions before we move to kit?" your tactical officer asks.
Nobody has any. That's usually a bad sign.

Your quartermaster meets you in the equipment bay with the particular expression of someone who has been asked to do something they find professionally distasteful.

"Right," $heOrShe says, setting a sealed case on the workbench. "So, The suit."

$HeOrShe pops the latches with more ceremony than strictly necessary. Inside: a suit. Slim, dark, form-fitting.

$HeOrShe shakes it out and pulls it on over $hisOrHer uniform.
Nothing happens for a moment.

Then you look back and $heOrShe is wearing a worn maintenance worker's coverall - Cruor transit authority patch on the shoulder, faded to approximate authenticity. You didn't see it change.

"Dedicated scan at close range might catch it. Don't let anyone run one."

$HeOrShe peels it off and holds it out. "Act accordingly."

Your quartermaster meets you in the equipment bay with the particular expression of someone who has been asked to do something they find professionally distasteful.

"Right," $heOrShe says, setting a sealed case on the workbench. "So, The suit."

$HeOrShe pops the latches with more ceremony than strictly necessary. Inside: a suit. Slim, dark, form-fitting.

"You've seen stealth gear before. This is the infiltration variant."
$HeOrShe shakes it out and pulls it on over $hisOrHer uniform.
Nothing happens for a moment. 

Then you look back and $heOrShe is wearing a worn maintenance worker's coverall.

"No shimmer. More convincing up close. Less useful if someone shoots at you."
$HeOrShe peels it off and holds it out. "Act accordingly."

The crawler Felix borrowed - from who, you didn't ask - smells like hydraulic fluid and poor decisions. 

Gira drives without speaking. Felix sits in the back with you, watching the transit infrastructure slide past the narrow window. Service corridors, junction markers, the occasional work crew moving between segments.

"Junction's about four minutes out," Gira says.

Felix turns from the window. "When you're inside - the terminal you want is in the secondary logistics bay. Not the main floor. Main floor has eyes on it." He pauses. "The secondary bay looks like a dead end. It's not. There's a service access on the left wall, unmarked. That's where the routing archives sit."

"I did a filter replacement in there eight months ago. I try to remember layouts. Old habit. Oh - one more thing." He produces a small transit authority data chit. "My work order, cloned to your biometrics."

Gira slows the crawler. Through the window, Junction 7-Kessler resolves - a wide service platform, mobile units parked in a loose row, maintenance crews moving between them. Busy enough to disappear into. Not so busy that nobody notices.

"Handoff starts in twelve minutes," she says. "You've got until it completes. After that the unit moves and the work order stops making sense."

She looks back at you once. Not encouragement exactly. More like acknowledgement.

"We'll be at the secondary egress. South side, blue marker."

Felix reaches past you and pushes the door.
"Clean and quiet," he says. "In and out."

You step onto the platform. The crawler pulls away without ceremony, merging back into the service traffic. Around you, maintenance workers move with the particular purpose of people on a clock.

You fall into step.

Junction 7-Kessler up close is louder than it looked from the crawler. Service vehicles moving between units, workers calling out over the ambient grind of machinery, the sharp smell of coolant and recycled air. Nobody gives you a second glance. Good.

The secondary logistics bay is where Felix said it would be - a side access off the main maintenance corridor, door propped open with a fuel coupling somebody left there and forgot. Inside: shelving units, a terminal bank along the far wall, a single overhead strip light that flickers like clockwork.

Nobody in here. Not yet. You move to the terminal.

Halfway through loading the archive, footsteps pass the propped door. Slow, deliberate. A patrol working the corridor.

The footsteps slow further outside the door. A pause - two seconds, maybe three. Then they continue.

ORIGIN: Processing facility to the south. Mobile work detail, designation WD-7 Restricted. 

STATUS: stationary.

You glance toward the door. 

You have what you came for. You could leave now. Or...

text:
You say it like it's the most boring thing that's happened this week. You don't look up from the terminal.

He processes this for a moment. The comm call is still live in his ear - you can hear the tinny voice on the other end asking what's wrong.

"Nothing," he says into the comm. "Just the audit guy."

He sets his diagnostic kit down, does his work, and leaves without making eye contact again.

You exhale slowly.

"Oh - wrong bay." You make a show of checking your work order, then glance at the terminal like it just occurred to you. "Is primary filter access north corridor from here?"

"Yeah," he says. "Back the way you came, second left."

He watches you a moment too long - not suspicious exactly, but noting you. You log out of the terminal, nod once, and move toward the door like someone who made a navigable error.
You wait ninety seconds in the corridor before coming back.
The terminal session has to be restarted. You lose four minutes.

"Hey," you say. You don't look up from the terminal.

He doesn't either. Just moves to the shelving unit on the far wall, pulls what he needs from his diagnostic kit, and picks up his comm conversation where he left it.
"...yeah, I know, I told him that already..."
He leaves without acknowledging you again.
You look back at the terminal. The suit did its job. You were just another worker in a room full of workers.

You copy what you need and close the session.

WD-7 Restricted. You know where he is.

The work order still has time on it. You could leave now.
options:
armaa_reicInfiltExitClean:Leave now.
armaa_reicInfiltExtraQuery:"Run one additional query before leaving." (Heat +1)

text:
The additional query pulls the same cluster of access flags the broad sweep would have found - queries originating from inside WD-7 Restricted, routed out through a maintenance terminal with no business having external access.

The record stops eleven days ago. The same day the transfer order was expedited.

The last query before the timestamps stop is different. It references a designation you don't recognize. An older format; the kind your database doesn't fully resolve.

You copy everything and close the session.

The coordinates are there - WD-7 Restricted. But the broad sweep pulled something else.

The timestamps on the queries stop abruptly - eleven days ago. The same day the transfer order was expedited.

He found something. And then someone found him finding it.

You scroll back through the query strings. The last one before the timestamps stop is different. A database query, external registry, pulling records on a designation you don't recognize. Old format; the kind your database doesn't fully resolve.

You copy everything and close the session. The terminal returns to its idle state like nothing happened.

You have a location. And a question you didn't come here with.

The main floor is busier than when you entered. You move at a pace somewhere between brisk walk and a light jog.

The south egress is exactly where Gira said it would be. From here, you can see Felix in the passenger seat watching the platform with a calm expression.

You get in, and the crawler hums as it starts to move. Nobody follows.

"Clean?" Felix asks.

"Clean," you say.

The main floor is busier than when you entered. You move at a pace somewhere between brisk walk and a light jog.

It's twenty meters from the south egress when a supervisor steps into the corridor ahead, TriPad in hand, eyes moving down the screen

He'll reach you in seconds. The crawler is visible through the egress window. Felix has clocked the supervisor - you can see him leaning forward in the passenger seat.
options:
armaa_reicInfiltExitKeepWalking:Keep walking. Don't break pace.
armaa_reicInfiltExitDoubleBack:Double back through the maintenance corridor.

text:
The supervisor glances at you, glances at his clipboard, glances back. Something about the work order number doesn't match what he's expecting. He opens his mouth.

"Filter audit," you say, before he can. "North corridor confirmed, secondary bay confirmed. Clean sweep."

He looks at you for one long second.

"...Right," he says. "Log it with the junction office."

You nod and keep walking.

Felixsays nothing when you get in. Neither does Gira.

The supervisor glances at you,  glances at his TriPad, and then glances back. Something about the work order number doesn't match what he's expecting. He opens his mouth.

"Filter audit," you say, before he can. "It's all clean."

He looks at you for one long second.
"...Right. Who flagged it?"

"$armaa_reicQMName's office. Routing compliance."

He checks the TriPad. Finds it. Goes back to his clipboard.

"Log it with the junction office."

You nod and keep walking.

Felix says nothing when you get in. Neither does Gira.

He checks. A longer pause than you'd like.

"...Log it with the junction office."

You nod and keep walking.

He looks at you for two seconds longer than is comfortable.

"...Right. Log it with the junction office."

You nod and keep walking.

Two extra minutes. You emerge at a different egress, flag Gira on the comm, and find the crawler repositioned two bays south without complaint.

"Supervisor?" Felix asks when you get in.

"Supervisor," you confirm.

He nods like that explains everything.

$global.armaa_reicIntelLevel = 3

text:
The junction slides behind you. Gira navigates back toward the docking approach without being asked.

Felix turns from the window. "Well?" You lay it out.

He looks out the window again. Something in his posture has shifts.

Gira speaks without looking back. "So we know where he is. And we know he's not just sitting there."

The crawler rejoins the service traffic. Through the window, Junction 7-Kessler recedes into Cruor's industrial sprawl.

"What did he find?" Felix asks.

You don't know yet, you say.

"But someone did," Gira says. Nobody says anything else for the rest of the ride.

The camps don't sit still long enough to be mapped in any honest way. They ride the city-train's shadow, folding into maintenance spines and sealed utility segments,  their human stock one stamp away from becoming processed out.  But that manpower has to move; all of it follows a rhythm, and you've finally caught the beat.

The route you have isn't a pin on a chart. It's a window. A resupply convoy with a time it can't miss. Hit it clean, and it will lead you where you need to go - either by force, or by the simple fact that nothing on Cruor can hide from its own logistics.

The problem is simple: 

If Gu Lingfeng is anywhere in that system, he'll pass through that flow. But so will everything else the Diktat would rather keep off the record.

The plan, then, is less a knife and more a net. Somewhere in that mess is your man.

The objective is still extraction. Find Gu Lingfeng.  But without a clean location, you're gambling on speed and confusion. And if you miss him in the shuffle… you may not get a second window.

Ops stands with  arms folded, jaw tight the way it gets when they're already running casualty numbers in their head.

"We can do this clean, in and out. Find Lingfeng, cut him loose, burn the paperwork,  leave 'em chasing ghosts. But… there's another way."

$HeOrShe brings up a second overlay. More red, more doors, more bodies.

" If we hit the point hard enough, we could crack more than one lock. We could pull everyone we can reach, turn their whole operation inside out."

They dont dress it up. "It would also light us up like a flare."

A finger traces outward from Cruor, through the system. 

"That kind of move doesn't stay quiet. INTSEC will treat it as an attack on the state. Every Diktat gun captain within range will be looking for us."

"If we go for Lingfeng only, we have a chance to slip away before the net closes. If we start emptying cages… we win bigger, but we pay for it."

"Your call, Captain. Surgical, or righteous."
options:
armaa_reicExtractionRaidStart2A:Consider extracting Lingfeng
armaa_reicExtractionRaidStart2B:Consider extracting Lingfeng and anyone else you can
armaa_reicExtractionRaidStart2C:Consider bombarding the entire location

text:
You review the operational brief one last time. The objective is narrow, deliberate.

Lingfeng's location near the resupply point creates a rare opportunity. The site is not hardened against external assault; its defenses are arranged to deter disorder. You will not pass through unseen, but you will not need to. A short disruption of local sensors and command traffic should be enough to force confusion without escalation.

The plan assumes speed and restraint. Insertion teams deploy light, strike hard, and withdraw before the perimeter can reorient. Patrols neutralized along the way are to be silenced, nonlethally if possible. Lingfeng is secured and extracted amid routine activity turned briefly chaotic.

If executed cleanly, the Diktat will be left with uncertainty rather than answers. A missing prisoner, conflicting reports, no obvious culprit. The response will be cautious, investigative. Heat will rise, but slowly.

This is the lowest-risk option available. It leaves little room for improvisation- and no margin for distraction.

Marines are ready to begin preparations on your order.

text:
You read the revised plan in silence. Freeing the surrounding workers changes the nature of the operation entirely. 

Stealth is no longer a meaningful advantage. Insertion teams must strike multiple points at once: gates, command posts, patrol concentrations- fracturing coordination before it can solidify. Even then, the breakout will be chaotic. Some prisoners will follow guidance; others will scatter blindly. What's more, there's no way to guarantee WHO exactly it is you are setting loose - freed prisoners are just as likely to be unfortunate souls as they are contract killers.

The Diktat will respond in full. A committed force, moving to seal the area and destroy whatever cannot be recovered. There will be no clean withdrawal once the camps begin to empty. Every minute on the ground increases exposure.

Success means lives freed that were never meant to leave those camps. Failure means being pinned down with civilians in the line of fire. Either way, your presence will be unmistakable, and the consequences immediate.

Should you have no recourse, one option is to simply obliterate the camp from orbit.

Lingfeng wold undoubtedly die,  along with many others, but it would guarantee any sensitive information he knows would die with him.
Ops estimates that 25 units of fuel would be enough to do the job.

text:
The tactical display draws a thin line from your orbit to the red smear of Cruor's surface.

"Package away. Impact… confirmed" someone says.

A slow, ugly bloom under the thin toxic sky, and then a spreading cloud that eats the horizon.

Ops doesn't look at you.

"Objective achieved," they say, like it's an entry on a checklist.

"Sensors show secondary detonations.. Maybe munitions. Whatever they had, it's burning with the rest."

Within hours, a Diktat broadcast begins looping on open channels: a grim montage of fire and smoke over Cruor, the same frames cut and recut until they feel like a confession.

A calm announcer voice, polished and furious:

"...an unprovoked act of terror... enemies of the Cause... cowardice from orbit..."

text:
Insertion teams deploy fast and clean. The engagement is sharp, contained. A handful of patrols are neutralized before alarms can propagate, their silence buying just enough time. Lingfeng is secured amid the confusion of a routine transfer gone wrong.

By the time local commanders begin to understand what has happened, your forces are already withdrawing. The raid leaves questions behind but few answers: a missing prisoner, scattered reports, no clear attribution. Heat rises, but slowly. Lingfeng is aboard, alive, and the operation ends before it can become something larger.

text:
The plan shifts from infiltration to controlled chaos. Multiple teams insert simultaneously, striking simultaneously to fracture coordination before it can solidify. Heavy weapons are kept to a minimum, but violence is unavoidable. Prisoners scatter in uneven waves, some following guides, others fleeing blindly into the terrain.

The withdrawal stretches longer than planned. Enemy forces converge, the battle escalating into a brutal siege. Your $shipOrFleet provides cover where it can, electronic warfare fighting to keep corridors open as long as possible. Not everyone makes it out together. Some evacuees arrive late, shaken, wounded, or alone.

When the operation finally concludes, Lingfeng is secured-but so are dozens of others who were never meant to leave those camps.

text:
Later, Lingfeng finds you on the edge of the medical bay doorway. He doesn't step fully in. Doesn't fully step away, either.

"I thought…" he begins, then stops. Swallows. Tries again.

"I thought I'd die there."

He looks down at his hands like they're unfamiliar to him.

"I don't know what I can give you. But… thank you."

He says it like it costs him something to say anything at all. Then he turns back toward the cot, toward the hum of a ship that is leaving the Diktat behind-before it has a reason to bring its full attention to bear.

text:
You can't keep dozens of rescued prisoners aboard a warship indefinitely. A uncerimonous council is held on the bridge.

Exsedol is the obvious choice if you can reach it-secure enough to absorb the arrivals, distant enough to force the Diktat to think twice about open retaliation, and with people who owe you favors that aren't written down. If the approach looks compromised, the fallback is a quieter non-Diktat port: a place that asks fewer questions, where documents can be improvised and bodies can disappear into crowds.

Behind you, Comms watches the Diktat bands with a tight expression. Response traffic is already hardening into something coherent.

Lingfeng is in the medical bay a few hours after the debrief, sitting upright on the cot with the careful posture of someone who is avoiding delicate areas.

You pull up a chair, and he lets you settle before he speaks. "You went through a lot of trouble," he says. "More than a standard extraction warrant."

"I'd like to know why."
options:
armaa_lingfengWhyMRC:"The MRC asked us to find you."
armaa_lingfengWhyOwnReasons:"We had our own reasons. The MRC was part of it."
armaa_lingfengWhyDoesItMatter:"Does it matter? You're out."

text:
He looks down at his hands. "I wasn't sure anyone had noticed I was gone. The work I was doing - it wasn't the kind that gets reported up the chain quickly."

His jaw tightens slightly.

"That's how they get away with it. Make the record-keeping boring enough and nobody follows the thread."

He studies you for a moment.

"All right. Then we both got something out of this. I can work with that."

"It matters to me," he says evenly. "I've spent the last eleven days being moved around by people who didn't bother explaining their reasons either."

He holds your gaze. "I'm not saying I'm not grateful. But I'd like to know whose ship I'm on."

options: armaa_lingfengWhatWereYouDoingResp:"What were you doing in there?"

text:
"Documentation," he says. "That's the official answer. Material that builds a legal case over years if you're patient enough."

And the unofficial answer, you query. He looks at you directly.

"I found something that wasn't supposed to be in the records I was pulling."

He shifts his weight, working through how much to say.

"There's a ...pattern. Certain prisoners - not random ones, not the usual rotation - get routed to a location that doesn't appear on any Diktat manifest I've ever seen. No facility name or official designation."

"I've seen a lot of Diktat paperwork. I know what their administrative schema looks like. This wasn't theirs."

You tell him you found the same code - his access queries, right before he was ostensibly captured.

"Then you know I almost had it. Another day. Maybe two. I was trying to cross-reference the location when they flagged my session." He exhales.

"Someone was watching, I guess. Not the Diktat - their response was too fast, too quiet."

His eyes meet yours. "Whatever is, or was at that location - someone was protecting it. And it's not the Diktat."

"They moved me before I could finish." He looks up.

"That's not how the Diktat operates when they catch a spy. That's how someone operates when they want a problem to disappear before it becomes one."

"Whatever is at that location - someone is protecting it. And it predates everything the Diktat built on this planet."

You ask if he has the code. "I committed it to memory before they pulled me," he says. "Old habit."

He recites it, watching your reaction as he does.

"You recognize it?" he asks. You may have seen the format before, you explain. He nods slowly.

"Then we both know more than we're saying. ...That's probably fine for now."

He settles back against the cot. "I owe you my life, Captain. That's not nothing. When you're ready to tell me what you actually know about that code - I'll be here." He closes his eyes.

text:
When you approach, the conversation stutters.

One of them gives you a slow once-over and nods at the empty space at the edge of their table.

"You're a long way from the nice bars, spacer," he says. It's almost a smile, but there's a question behind it. "Get lost? Or just slumming it with the rest of us?"

A woman beside him snorts into her drink. "Maybe he's hiring," she rasps. "Or maybe he's looking for a story to take back to orbit."

A few of them chuckle. The man tips his cup in your direction.

"Either way," he adds, "you don't walk up to a table like this unless you've got a reason. And around here, reasons usually come with rounds."

They shift slightly - making room, or marking distance - hard to tell.
