text:
The table loosens in increments - shoulders dropping, voices rising, the tension shifting from who are you to what do you want.

They talk a while longer, but you keep your questions small and your ears open. Unfortunately, you don't glean much from the conversation. 

However, that you've certainly made them more pliable.

The reaction is immediate - not greed, but caution.

The thin woman speaks first, voice flat. "You trying to buy friends?"

You explain that your line of work puts you in reach of things most crews only hear rumors about. And on a rock like this, you figure people take their relief where they can find it.

For a moment, nobody moves. Then the broad-shouldered one makes a small, careful gesture - not quite agreement, but permission.

The goods disappear without ceremony. Conversation follows, at first guarded, then loosening in the way it always does when the edge comes off. 

Some time later, you excuse yourself from the vice den with your face unchanged and your pockets lighter - and with enough detail in your head to turn rumor into a plan.

The question lands on the table. A few people nearby find somewhere else to look. The broad shouldered man glances around first - not paranoid, just careful. 

The thin woman pulls at the bandanna at her temple. "Depends what you need to know," she says. "And why."
The broad one leans forward slightly. "They move on a rotation." He stops there. "You already know that, or you wouldn't be asking it that way."

"So what are you actually asking, Captain?"
options: armaa_reicBarOfferWorkResp2Skills:"You know how the details move - do you know how to find a specific one?"

script: AddTextSmall "If recruited, will temporarily join the fleet and assist in combat operations until the next port is reached." highlight
text:
The woman eyes you like you're a form she's deciding whether to stamp.
"That's INTSEC-adjacent work. People who ask about that are either on their payroll or they're not."
She looks at you steadily. "Which one are you?"

"We've done security for the Diktat. Extra bodies that don't ask questions," the man chimes in. "We know what the details look like from the outside. And we know people who've seen them from the inside."
options:
armaa_reicBarOfferWorkResp2Hire:"I could use that expertise."(recruit)
barHRE_goToNormalBar:Look around the bar

text:
Right now, what you have is a haze of rumor and frustration. You know Cruor runs on schedules and shortcuts, and you've heard enough to suspect the person you're after is buried somewhere in the planet's moving industrial sprawl - but you don't have a location, a window, or anything you could plan a strike around. You set the idea on the table anyway.

The broad one folds his hands. "We don't sign on to hope, Captain. We sign on to a job we can measure."

He nods toward you. "Right now you don't have enough to swing a hammer. You've got questions. That's fine - but it means if we go with you, we're just walking into the dark."

"Come back when you've got something actionable. A place to hit, or a time you can put on a clock. Then we'll talk."

You don't have a blueprint, but you have something you can plan around: a target area on the train network, a narrow window when the right people and equipment will be concentrated, and a rough idea of what kind of security you're going to smash through.

A short, humorless smile crosses the broad one's face.
"Then here's what we're worth."

"We can put bodies where you need them. We can help get your marines in and out without losing half of them to confusion and bad timing,  and we can get you into a few facilities we've run security for."


"You want us on the payout and the risk? Fine. But understand what you're buying," the thin woman chimes in. Her eyes dart to Felix, who wordlessly meets her gaze, before she looks back to you.

"4000 credits up front, plus a monthly salary of 1000 credits per month until we reach port."

Gira taps the table once.

"What's the point? A fat payout here just paints a giant bullseye on our back.And after you hit one of their work details? Sticking around to spend it is suicide. The only smart way to get paid here is in passage. "

"Get us out of Askonia. That's what we're selling ourselves for."

text:
"All right," he says. "We'll make the calls."

You route them a shipboard channel and rattle off directions.

The broad-shouldered contractor looks you over again, "...Yeah," he says, quieter. "Cruor's got a way of doing that."

The thin woman shrugs, "Half the people in this room came here because they were 'running thin'," she says. "Some of us just got stuck long enough to pretend it was a plan."

A younger guy at the end of the table shifts, suddenly interested. "Debt, or just bad luck?" he asks.

The broad one raises a hand, like cutting off the instinct to judge.

"Doesn't matter," he says. He nods at the empty space beside them.

"Sit. Rounds are on me.  We can at least point you at the kind of work Cruor actually pays for."

The broad one slides a chipped cup toward you like it's a contract. Around the table, the posture shifts from guarded to curious.

"All right," he says. "You don't look like a man who should be counting credits. So-"

"What brought you to Cruor, Captain? Don't dress it up. Give me the version you'd tell someone you might hire."

The thin woman watches your face over the rim of her drink. The younger guy leans in like he's listening for a tell.
options:
armaa_reicBarBrokeResp2Biz:Contracts and quick turnaround. Cruor always has work.
armaa_reicBarBrokeResp2Sympathetic:A string of bad burns and worse timing
armaa_reicBarBrokeResp2Dangerous:I needed somewhere people dont ask too many questions
armaa_reicBarBrokeResp2HalfTruth:I'm chasing a lead (half-truth)

text:
The broad one nods. "Yeah. That tracks."

He takes a slow drink, eyes on you over the rim.

"Cruor's got work. Always does. Trick is knowing which jobs come with a quiet escort and which ones come with a report filed on you afterward." He sets the cup down.

"Around here, the paperwork follows you. You clear a job clean, great. You ask the wrong question on the wrong channel? Somebody makes a note. Notes become files. Files become reasons."

The thin woman doesn't look up from her drink. "And some corridors just stop existing. Along with whoever used to work them."

"So. You want to clear your ledger on Cruor? Stick to freight and maintenance. Keep your head down around shift change - that's when the priority convoys move and nobody wants extra eyes." He shrugs once.

"Not a secret. Just a rhythm you learn fast, or you don't learn anything after."

The broad one's expression eases, just a fraction.

"...Yeah, the Sector does that. All right. If you're trying to dig out - the convoys with extra water and a quiet escort. They move tight around shift change, always through the same back corridors."

He keeps his voice level, like he's discussing cargo manifests. 

"You can make yourself useful to them. Or you can stay out of their way. But you didn't hear that from me, and I didn't say anything worth remembering."

"Who routes them?" he repeats.

He glances away  - not looking at anything in particular, just checking the room out of habit - then leans in slightly.

"Officially? The port office. It's all very clean." A humorless smile tugs at one corner of his mouth.

"In practice? It's whoever has the stamp that day. Follow the paperwork and it'll lead you to the same handful of hands.

The table goes a touch quieter. 

"Uh-huh."

He watches you like he's deciding whether you're trouble; a drowning $player.manOrWoman who will pull others beneath the surface with them.

"Plenty of people come here to disappear," he says. "Some of them stay disappeared. If you're just looking for work, fine. But if you're looking to drag trouble onto our table, you picked the wrong corner." He tilts his chin toward the bar, dismissive.

The broad one exhales through his nose. "A lead," he repeats. "Sure."

The thin woman taps her cup against the table once.

"Odd place to come looking for someone," she says. "That what this is? A lover? A friend? Somebody who said the wrong thing to the wrong person?" She props her cheek in her palm.

The broad one rolls his eyes.

"...All right," he says, quieter.

"I've done calls on those rigs. The ones that deal in shipments of bodies," He rubs at the heel of his palm, remembering.

"They don't advertise themselves as forced labor camps. They're..." he snorts. "... 'work details'. "

He pauses, giving a casual glance around the establishment - but it's clear to you he's checking to see if you have any eavesdroppers. "If I were you, i'd let it go. Nothing good will come from sticking your nose into that kind of business."

"…You're not hearing me."

His eyes flick to the doorway again.

"I have a rough recollection of the route. A time window. That's it." He slides his TriPad towards you in such a way that it seems almost unintentional. 

"And if anyone asks where you got that? You didn't."

"...Hnh."

The thin woman mutters, "At least he can take a hint."

You start to pull back - and that's when the broad one speaks again, quieter, almost reluctant.

"Look. If you're going to be stupid about this..." He slides his TriPad towards you in such a way that it seems almost unintentional.

text:
She stares at you a moment, expression flat.

"I don't have a neat little map to their cages."

"Oh? Then we speak the same language."

"I can give you something real - a lead you can act on. But don't waste my time with hero posturing. If you jam that knife in, twist it."

She snorts. "Of course you are." Imoinu's eyes flick over you like you're a stain that might still be useful.

"I don't care what you call yourself as long as the blood of the oppressor is spilt, but I don't hand out intel to random mercs for free. So how can you help me?"

"Then say so. What do you need?" You give her the short version. Imoinu listens without much input.

"Fine. I can get you a thread to pull. But if you pull it, pull it all the way. Don't come back asking me to finish what you started."

She goes still.

For a moment you think she's going to cut the comms. Instead, she lets out a slow breath.

"At least you're not pretending this time. INTSEC disappeared someone. Of course they did."

"I'll make you a deal. I don't trust you. I don't like you. But I hate them more."

She leans forward.

"I'll give you a thread. You pull it yourself. If you come back asking for more, you bring something worth the risk."

"...Who do you think you are? You want to lecture me about resistance from the bridge of a warship? You want to call me weak because I won't endanger my people to satisfy your curiosity?"

"Go find your own answers on Cruor. Ask INTSEC nicely. I'm sure they'll give you a tour."

There's a hard click as she cuts the channel.

text:
Felix comes through first. Gira's just off-frame, close enough that you catch the edge of a red wrap and the faint glow of a wrist-slate.

"Captain," Felix says. "We made it. I think this concludes our agreement."

Gira leans in slightly into frame, voice level. "You got us out. That counts for something. ...We're going to cash out quiet and disappear before anyone comes looking." Felix's gazes behind him and off to the side- to where your hangar crews are still marshaling their machines, still moving like they're expecting trouble.

"If you're wondering," he adds, "we'll clear our hardware off your decks clean."

Gira's mouth twitches, almost a smile. "Nothing personal. This is just the part where people usually stop tempting fate."
options:
armaa_reicArrivedMercs2Bye:"Understood. You're free to go. Burn bright."
armaa_reicArrivedMercs2Reimburse:"Hold up.  My contact can cover your docking fees and your next hop."

text:
Gira comes through alone. Behind her, your hangar is all motion and shouted numbers.

"Captain. Felix didn't make it." She says it like a report you can't argue with. "He bought time. Was always good at that."

Her eyes flick off-frame, tracking something in your bay- a mech being winched, a crewman waving a guide light.

"I'm here because we had an agreement," she continues. "You got me out. That counts. I'm not going to hang around and see how long it takes for someone to follow my trail."

She exhales through her nose.

"We'll clear my hardware off your decks clean."

There's a lull - space left for you to answer.

text:
Felix comes through first, eyes tired in a way sleep won't fix. 

"Captain. We made it." A pause. His jaw works once. "...Mostly."

He doesn't volunteer details. He doesn't need to.

His gaze shifts off to the side where your crews are still marshaling equipment like they expect trouble to crawl out of the dark.

"If you're wondering," he says, "We'll clear my hardware off your decks clean.  We won't leave you a mess."

There's a brief lull - the kind that leaves space for you to answer.

text:
You tell them your contact might be willing to cover a clean departure. Felix stares at you dumbfounded, like you've spoken a language he doesn't hear often. "...You'd do that?"

Gira's eyes flick to Felix, then back to you. The edge in her posture doesn't vanish, but it loosens.

"Most captains get what they paid for and cut the line," she says, careful. "They don't circle back with extra air in their tanks."

For a second, Felix looks almost embarrassed. He clears his throat.

"... Very appreciated, captain," he says, and you can hear how much effort it takes to say it without sounding like he's begging.  "We'll wait.  And if your people come through... we'll leave clean, fast, and grateful."

You tell him your contact might be willing to cover a clean departure - fuel, supplies, maybe parts.

Felix freezes like you've thrown him a lifeline he didn't ask for.

"...You'd do that?" He swallows, and for a second he looks almost embarrassed to be seen reacting.

He clears his throat, tries to get control of himself.

"...Thank you," he manages. "I mean it."

"I'll wait. And if your people come through… I'll leave clean, fast, and grateful. No loose ends, no noise."

"Stay dangerous, Captain," Felix says - and the channel drops.

You tell her your contact might be willing to cover a clean departure: fuel, supplies, maybe parts.

For a moment, Gira just stares. Then she gives a short, almost disbelieving laugh.

"...You'd do that."

Her posture doesn't soften, exactly- but the tension shifts, like a knife getting put back into a sheath. "Most captains wouldn't even offer. You're so weird." she says, careful. "They get what they paid for and they stop looking."

"If your people come through, I'll take it. Not because I'm greedy-because it keeps me breathing."

She nods once, sharp and final.

"I'll wait. And if this works out… I won't forget who didn't leave me twisting in the wind."

Her gaze holds the camera a second longer, before she moves to cut the channel.

text:
You tell them your contact might be willing to cover a clean departure if it keeps everyone quiet and moving.

Felix stares at you dumbfounded, like you've spoken a language he doesn't hear often. "...You'd do that?"

Gira's eyes flick to Felix, then back to you. The edge in her posture doesn't vanish, but it loosens. "Most captains get what they paid for and cut the line," she says, careful. "They don't circle back with extra air in their tanks."

For a second, Felix looks almost embarrassed. He clears his throat.

"... Very appreciated, captain," he says, and you can hear how much effort it takes to say it without sounding like he's begging. "We're not used to... considerations. We'll wait."

A quiet chime cuts through the post-dock routine. Your comms officer looks up from their console, frowning at whatever just hit their feed.

"Captain. Priority bulletin you may be interested in just came through."

"MRC seem to have taken control of Fort Exsedol," they say. "They're calling it a change of station authority." They scroll down, scanning. 

"Traffic's still moving through. But it's - yeah, it's being managed now." They look up.

"Whatever happened, it was quiet. Nothing on the incident channels."

The approach lanes clear faster than they should.

No pirate "inspection." No lazy threat posture on the comms. Just a clean, clipped clearance code and a vector that threads you straight in like you belong.

A moment later, a secure channel request slides onto your display.

"Exsedol's under MRC control as of now. Docking has you logged as priority traffic. If you want the short version: those who wish to are being allowed to leave with whatever they can carry, and those who don't have fallen in line, for the most part."

$HeOrShe lets that settle.

"And if you're wondering whether this paints a target on all of us?" A faint edge of a smile in the voice.

"Yes."

Lingfeng appears in a quiet ops room, sleeves rolled, a mug of something steaming in his hand. He looks... suprisingly put together for a man who was just being worked like a slave on a distant rock.

He studies you for half a second - not suspicion, exactly. More like a professional taking inventory. He offers a faint smile.

"You've arrived at an interesting moment. Exsedol finally decided what it wants to be - and it's not a pirate nest anymore."

He lifts the mug slightly, a dry little toast.

"To bad habits ending. And new ones beginning."

"Exsedol's under MRC control as of now. Docking has you logged as priority traffic. That wasn't true last shift."

"If you want the short version: those who wish to are being allowed to leave with whatever they can carry, and those who don't have fallen in line, for the most part."

$HeOrShe lets that settle.

"And if you're wondering whether this paints a target on all of us?" A faint edge of a smile in the voice.

"Yes."

"There you are. You've been doing well, I hear?" she smirks.

"The MRC has a center now. Something they can rally to. Whether they know it or not, so do I." She lets that sit without explaining it.

"Before the congratulations start arriving from people who did considerably less work - what do you want?"

options: armaa_seraPostReicOptWhatsPointResp:"I want to know what you're really building. And where I fit in."
armaa_seraPostReicOptResourcesResp:"I want resources. If I'm going to steer anything, I need hands on the wheel."
armaa_seraPostReicOptDoneHere:"I want out. I delivered. I'm not signing up for a crusade."

text:
"Ambitious question."

She considers you for a moment. "Commerce requires predictability. Predictability requires stability. Stability, in a system like this one, requires someone the various factions are willing to listen to. That's the honest version. The shorter version is that a Gamlin system where freighters arrive and depart on schedule is worth considerably more than one where they don't."

"As for where you fit - you already know the answer to that, or you wouldn't have asked the question." She doesn't elaborate. The smile suggests she thinks that's sufficient.

text:
"Practical," she says. The approval in her voice is real.

"Exsedol gives you access. Priority docking, faster resupply, a quieter pipeline for the things that don't appear on manifests. In return, the MRC stays pointed in a direction that doesn't set trade lanes on fire. That's the arrangement."

She offers a small smile. "I don't need you to build an empire, Captain. I need you to make sure the one that's forming doesn't become a problem I have to solve from the outside."

"You delivered," she agrees. "Walk if you need to." She picks up whatever she was doing before you came in - unhurried, not dismissive.

"Just understand that your name is already attached to the outcome. The MRC will remember who got them here. The Diktat will, eventually, look for someone to hold accountable." She glances up. "Distance doesn't erase that. It just means you're managing the consequences without the resources."

The smile returns. Small, edged. "My door stays open. That's the only speech you're getting."

You receive a high-priorty comms request.

Sera Pha receives you in her usual setting but there's something different in the room today. A second comm terminal is active, its indicator light blinking. 

"I've been thinking about what you returned to me," she says. "Cassian is... adjusting. Slowly. He has a great deal to process and share, As do I."

She glances toward the second terminal.

"In the meantime, something has come to my attention that I thought you should be aware of. A vessel has been holding position at the edge of this system for some time..It contacted me directly, two shifts ago. Asked about you specifically."

She reaches forward and opens the channel.

"Commander $player.lastName."

She doesn't offer anything further immediately. Whatever she's about to say, she's chosen her words carefully.

"My..former CO submitted his final report before we lost contact with the station." Her jaw tightens slightly. 

"Our mission was the preservation of Sovereignty assets and continuity of command. That mission is..." She looks at you directly. "We're sitting in deep space with a capital ship, no orders, and a chain of command that ends somewhere I don't have clearance to look at directly. The former captain had no complaints, but I don't like not knowing who I'm actually working for."

"What I do know is that their report described someone who made choices that didn't have to be made. And that Administrator Pha saw fit to make this introduction rather than leave us out there. The Bellator needs a commission it can stand behind. I'm told you might be in a position to offer one."
options:
armaa_bellatorRespWord:"You have my word the Bellator will be treated accordingly."
armaa_bellatorRespWhatChanged:"What changed your mind?"
armaa_bellatorRespDidntExpect:"I didn't expect to see the Bellator again."

text:
Something in their posture settles - not relief, but the particular quality of someone who made a judgment call and has decided to live with it.

"Then we have an understanding, Commander." A pause. "My crew will need time to adjust to the arrangement. I'd ask you respect that."

"Don't make me regret this."

"Nothing changed my mind," she says. "I made a decision with the information I had." A fractional pause. "Vael trusted his read of you. He didn't trust easily. That carried weight."

She doesn't elaborate further. It's enough.
"Neither did we, honestly." She glances briefly offscreen - at her crew, or at something else. "Here we are."
